File.Name: 5319b_5314-068.jpg
Notes: 9706572 Pretty flowers of Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum), aka Brodiaea for the squeamish. It's framed by the introduced annual grasses that are trying to smother it out. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2005)

File.Name: 5319c_5314-054.jpg
Notes: 9706553 I believe this pretty wildflower is native Sky Lupine (Lupinus nanus). It has a fragrance. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2005)

File.Name: 5319d_5314-062.jpg
Notes: 9706517 Tiny fly resting on a flower of native Johnny-Jump-Up (Viola pedunculata). (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2005)

File.Name: 5327a_5323-082.jpg
Notes:
9763704 What a surprise to find this brilliant native Scarlet Milkvetch (Astragalus coccineus) flowering in a crack on the exposed bare rock on the windy gap through Optimo in the upper Pinto Basin. (Joshua Tree, 23 March 2005)

We backpacked across the upper Pinto Basin in Joshua Tree National Park last week on our school trip, hiking from White Tank across the flats and up a beautiful wash to Lost Horse Mine. It was cold and windy all week. We had 1/4 inch of rain on Tuesday night and frost on our tents Friday morning! That's not my choice for a spring desert trip, though I did find new flowers to photograph and we had a great time surviving the elements. Reptiles and butterflies are just starting to appear. It's an interesting year in California with so much rain.

File.Name: 5327b_5322-061.jpg
Notes: 9763030 Beautiful flower of native Desert Bluebells (Phacelia campanularia) growing in a rocky desert wash. It's stunning to find such rich flowers in this harsh desert landscape. (Joshua Tree, 22 March 2005)

File.Name: 5327c_5323-119.jpg
Notes: 9762864 Beautiful native daisy - maybe Mojave Desertstar (Monoptilon bellidiforme) - growing on the sand flats of the Pinto Basin. (Joshua Tree, 23 March 2005)

File.Name: 5327d_5324-041.jpg
Notes: 9762769 Strange flowers and stranger fruit of Turpentine Broom aka Mohave Desert Rue (Thamnosma montana) in the desert. It's a little subshrub in the Rue (Rutaceae) family. (Joshua Tree, 24 March 2005)
CommonNames: Turpentine Broom; Mohave Desert Rue; Rue

File.Name: 5327e_5324-024.jpg
Notes: 9762665 Intricate spines at the top of a little Hedgehog Cactus (Echinocereus sp. or kin) in the desert. We didn't find any cacti flowering yet, though some are getting ready. (Joshua Tree, 24 March 2005)

File.Name: 5327f_5324-008.jpg
Notes: 9762461 Me and Julie backpacking across the Pinto Basin in Joshua Tree National Park last week on our school trip. We're both desert rats at heart, and starting to show it. I used my monopod for my walking stick and took many photos. (Joshua Tree, 24 March 2005)

File.Name: 5328a_5322-062.jpg
Notes:
9772606 Elegant "scorpioid" cyme flower head of native Desert Bluebells (Phacelia campanularia) growing in a rocky desert wash in Joshua Tree National Park. The two inch long flowers are so blue, but the geometry of the flowers and buds is equally impressive. The flower stalk is curled up like a scorpion`s tail. New flowers get ready to open as the stem unrolls, and the old flowers fall away as seeds develop. It's a good strategy that lets the plants keep unrolling to make new flowers as long as they can. (Joshua Tree, 22 March 2005)

Today's photos are all from my school backpack trip across the upper Pinto Basin in Joshua Tree National Park last week as we hiked from White Tank across the flats and up a beautiful wash to Lost Horse Mine. It was cold and windy all week. We had 1/4 inch of rain on Tuesday night and frost on our tents Friday morning! That's not my choice for a spring desert trip, though I did find new flowers to photograph and we all had a great time surviving the elements. It's an interesting year in California with so much rain!

File.Name: 5328b_5322-058.jpg
Notes: 9772465 Looking straight down on a flower head of Chia Sage (Salvia columbariae) in the Joshua Tree desert. The seeds are edible and tasty and easy to harvest by beating the summer-dry seed heads into a basket. They are said to be an energy boost used by Native American runners. I think "chia" is a generic word in Mexico for annual sage seeds, so this might not be the same species that grows from the spilled seeds of your Mr. T Chia Pet. It's a fragrant California native wildflower that also grows in the backcountry near home. (Joshua Tree, 22 March 2005)

File.Name: 5328c_5323-093.jpg
Notes: 9772351 A bristly desert Popcorn Flower aka Forget-Me-Not (Cryptantha sp.) in the Borage (Boraginaceae) family. The "splitters" claimed this genus. There are 16+ recognized species in the Joshua Tree desert and 50+ species in California, and you need a microscope to tell them apart. That makes it easy for me to be a "lumper" and simplify: this is Cryptantha, never mind the species! We have these at home too - at least kin. (That hedge is the splitter in me!) (Joshua Tree, 23 March 2005)

File.Name: 5328d_5324-039.jpg
Notes: 9771985 New flower of Desert Dandelion (Malacothrix californica aka M. glabrata?). In another week or two, these flowers will carpet the desert washes in Joshua Tree National Park, but we were a bit early this rainy year, and they were just beginning. Most of these flowers are all yellow ray flowers, but a few have this magenta center that sure looks like disk flowers. It looks like the inner stamens/styles are bending over to protect or worship the center. I still have lots to learn about the most common desert flowers! (Joshua Tree, 24 March 2005)

File.Name: 5328e_5322-055.jpg
Notes: 9771890 New flowers of native Bristly Fiddleneck (Amsinckia tessellata). These plants (and many more) usually grow in mounds at the base of desert shrubs. That little bit of shade and extra drip-moisture under the shrub makes all the difference in this harsh landscape. The flowers are curled up in a "scorpioid inflorescence" - the flower stalk is curled up like a scorpion`s tail or a fiddle's neck. New flowers open on the end as the stem unrolls, and the old flowers fade as seeds develop behind. It's a good strategy that lets the plants unroll to make new flowers as long as conditions allow. (Joshua Tree, 22 March 2005)

File.Name: 5328f_5322-079.jpg
Notes: 9771752 Bright red Desert Paintbrush (Castilleja angustifolia) blooming in a dry desert wash. The real flowers are the slender green tubes top-left and middle-right. All the brilliant red color comes from leafy bracts. We have a very similar species at home. (Joshua Tree, 22 March 2005)

File.Name: 5329a_5323-080.jpg
Notes:
9780450 Brilliant 2+ inch flowers of native Scarlet Milkvetch aka Locoweed (Astragalus coccineus) on the exposed bare rock on the windy gap through Optimo in the upper Pinto Basin. It's shocking to find these brilliant flowers in such a harsh desert landscape. (Joshua Tree, 23 March 2005)

Here are more photos from our backpack trek across the upper Pinto Basin in Joshua Tree National Park last week on our school trip. There are more photos in the archive.

File.Name: 5329b_5323-092.jpg
Notes: 9780408 We have many boring introduced yellow mustard species near home that cover the coastal hills in spring. Pretty, but boring. It`s always a treat to find the rare beautiful native mustards (family Brassicaceae) in the wilds. I can't identify this native from my photos - I guess it is genus Arabis, maybe Shockley's Rock Cress (Arabis shockleyi)? I found a few plants at the rocky top of a desert wash. I was lucky to get this photo in the wind. (Joshua Tree, 23 March 2005)

File.Name: 5329c_5322-097.jpg
Notes: 9780365 Brown-eyed Primrose (Camissonia claviformis) flowers in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family. The pretty little flowers don`t really close during the day, but they get a little droopy and are best in the morning. (Joshua Tree, 22 March 2005)

File.Name: 5329d_5321-134.jpg
Notes: 9780340 Little Flower Fly (Syrphidae) dancing over new flowers of Basket Bush aka "Squaw Bush" (Rhus trilobata) in the desert last week. The same native shrub grows near home in the Santa Barbara mountains. I saw many familiar plants and insects during our desert backpack trip - sometimes the same species like this and sometimes slightly different. It's a reminder how close we are to being a desert here in Santa Barbara, despite the madrones and huckleberries in our canyon. (Joshua Tree, 21 March 2005)
CommonNames: Flower Fly; Basket Bush; Squaw Bush

File.Name: 5329e_5322-028.jpg
Notes:
9780279 Mass of creepy Tent Caterpillars in the desert - maybe the Western Tent Caterpillar (Malacosoma californicum)? Other native butterflies like Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona) also make web tents, so I'm not sure of the ID. I'm not sure of the native shrub either. It has rose-family flowers - maybe Desert Almond (Prunus fasciculata)? (Joshua Tree, 22 March 2005)

The 2+ inch long caterpillars collect on the sunny side of their communal nest in the morning to catch some rays. If it warms up, they spread over the branches. In the evening, they disappear inside their web. Creepy critters. I had "Invasion of the Body Snatchers" thoughts.
CommonNames: Tent Caterpillars; Western Tent Caterpillar; Chalcedon Checkerspot; Desert Almond
ScientificName: Malacosoma californicum; Occidryas chalcedona; Prunus fasciculata
associatedTaxa: Malacosoma californicum; Occidryas chalcedona; Prunus fasciculata

File.Name: 5329f_5323-130.jpg
Notes: 9780188 Gotta have at least one sunset shot after backpacking for a week in the desert. The intense color only lasted for a few minutes on the night after our rain storm. I grabbed my camera and ran up the wash to frame these native Joshua Trees (Yucca brevifolia). There are a couple of commercial jet contrails (approaching LAX) in the cloud to complicate the geometry. A few minutes later the sky was all gray. Only a few kids noticed. (Joshua Tree, 23 March 2005)

File.Name: 5401a_5401-040.jpg
Notes:
9801969 "I'm so blue..." Native Larkspur flower in our canyon - maybe Coast Larkspur (Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum but cp. D. parryi). Whatever it is, the beautiful flowers are about as blue as can be. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2005)

It must be spring!

File.Name: 5401b_5401-045.jpg
Notes: 9801747 Native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) flowers in the canyon. Some lupines have flowers in whorls and some don't. This one is in between. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2005)

File.Name: 5401c_5401-052.jpg
Notes: 9801702 Native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) starting to flower in the canyon. There's a lesson in this photo about pollination. Number the flowers left to right from 1 to 5. You can tell the age of each flower by the amount of brown on the lower spade-shaed petal. Flowers 2 and 3 are young, and the male stamens are held high, and there is no visible female pistil. In flower 4, the pistil is open and the stamens are moving down. In flowers 1 and 5, the female pistil is erect, and the male stamens are folded completely down. It's an elegant dance between stamen and pistil to avoid self-pollination and increase genetic diversity by receiving pollen from another plant. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2005)

File.Name: 5401d_5401-033.jpg
Notes: 9801633 Crab Spider (Thomisidae) poised to attack on a leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata). These spiders usually perch on flowers and match colors with the petals. Flowers are late this year after our record rainfall. (We've had 68.6 inches so far since September here on San Marcos Pass!) This spider is doing the best it can on a green leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2005)

File.Name: 5401e_5401-057.jpg
Notes:
9801550 Not quite in focus, but the action is perfect. This is a little bee-like Flower Fly (Syrphidae) laying eggs with its extended abdomen on native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the canyon. The eggs will be the doom of other insects who live on the plant. I guess they wait until eaten by larvae and then develop within. You know, like the movie "Alien". (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2005)

Pollinators (and flowers) are just starting to appear. It's interesting that these early insects are preditors. I have another photo of a Syrphid Fly laying eggs near (not ON) its prey at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8081872

File.Name: 5401f_5329-018.jpg
Notes:
9801302 Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly resting on a rock in our garden at home. This was my first sign of a remarkable migration of these butterflies that passed through the Santa Ynez Valley the next day. Hundreds - thousands - even more! - of these butterflies flew past school helped by east winds. My "California Butterflies" book describes them perfectly: "They fly a few feet above the ground, rising vertically over obstacles, rather than going around them." I collected many on the window of my truck driving to and from school. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2005)

I don't remember seeing such a migration at home before. But we drove through a swarm of these same butterflies in Cajon Pass on our drive home from Joshua Tree last week. Maybe the devil winds carried them all the way here and beyond! Today - a few days later - I didn't see any of these buterflies.

File.Name: 5402a_5402-034.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 10170489 Green Aphid (family Aphididae, order Homoptera) climbing over stamens in a flower of native Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa). I think it's aphid love. See my photos at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=9809435 and [Previous] to that. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2005)

File.Name: 5402b_5402-026.jpg
Notes:
9809555 Native Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) slurping up the sweet nectar from the green glands at the base of the petals of this Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) flower. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2005)

The stamens are arched over the glands like traffic lights, but they are too high to transfer pollen to this ant. Compare my photo of a Ladybug Beetle on the same flower at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=7669357

I took many photos of this ant moving around and around the flower. It didn't seem to be using its mandibles to feed, but look between the jaws and the right antenna to see how its dragging its palps over the flower. Is this how it feeds?
CommonNames: Ant; Chaparral Death Camas; Ladybug Beetle

File.Name: 5402c_5402-025.jpg
Notes:
9809435 Is this Aphid (family Aphididae, order Homoptera) love? I was trying to get a close shot of this native Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) wildflower. I didn't even notice the aphids until I got home and zoomed in to see them move in successive shots. It sure looks like the familiar green aphid at the bottom is heading for a rendezvous with that smaller dark critter above. Compare the [Previous] photo to see how it's climbing. Aphids have a complex life cycle with sexual and parthenogenic generations. I've read that males appear in fall, but this is a confusing year with so much rain. Is this a sexual encounter to exchange genetic material? (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2005)

The California Poppy you find in wildflower seed packets is Eschscholzia californica. Those flowers have a "receptacle disk" at the base of the petals. This photo shows the real "California Poppy" (E. caespitosa) that doesn't have the disk and doesn't come from seed packets. It's not common.
ScientificName: Aphididae; Homoptera; Eschscholzia caespitosa; Eschscholzia californica; E. caespitosa
associatedTaxa: Aphididae; Homoptera; Eschscholzia caespitosa; Eschscholzia californica; E. caespitosa

File.Name: 5402d_5402-052.jpg
Notes: 9809283 Is this Aphid (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) love? I was trying to get a close shot of this native Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) flower and didn't even notice the aphids until I got home. See [Next] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2005)

File.Name: 5402e_5402-044.jpg
Notes: 9809134 Little bee-like Flower Fly (Syrphidae) visiting a perennial Composite (Asteracea) flower in the garden. Look close to see that the reddish ray flowers around the rim have only stamens showing at the base, and the disk flowers are just starting to open around the outside edge of the center. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2005)

File.Name: 5402f_5402-010.jpg
Notes: 9809026 This might be my very favorite native plant in our mountains - the shrub Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis var. redivivus) in the Storax (Styracaceae) family. The flowers are fragrant and beautiful and elegant and different. It's related to a species from southern Europe and the Middle East, and "gum storax" is an ancient commodity with a familiar smell. This shrub has spotty distribution in our mountains and no other local relatives. I'm curious about these isolated single-species families and genera in our local flora with no obvious close relatives. Why are they here at all? They must say something about the history of our flora. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2005)

File.Name: 5402g_5402-024.jpg
Notes: 9808966 New flower and buds of native Wild Cucumber aka California Manroot (Marah fabaceus) - sometimes called "manroot" because it grows from a huge underground tuber that can get child size. It's a vine that uses tendrils to clamber over native shrubs and dies back to the root in winter. The flowers have a frosted but immature look. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2005)

File.Name: 5403a_5403-068.jpg
Notes: 9816207 Flower head of Pacific Sanicle aka Snakeroot or Gamble Weed (Sanicula crassicaulis or kin) in the Parsly (Apiaceae) family. We have several native sanicles in our mountains, and I always get them confused. They all have similar heads of tiny yellow flowers in characteristic round umbels. The flowers are like perfect origami envelopes holding the precious stamens. The stamens ripen and fall away one by one rather than all at once. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2005)
CommonNames: Pacific Sanicle; Snakeroot; Gamble Weed; Parsly

File.Name: 5403b_5403-067.jpg
Notes: 9816034 Elegant flowers of Woodland Star (Lithophragma affine) in the Saxifrage (Saxifragaceae) family. This native hides in the woods and is not common. It keeps its private parts hidden inside, but you get a glimpse here if you look close. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2005)

File.Name: 5403c_5403-061.jpg
Notes: 9815961 Minimal flowers of native California Plantain (Plantago erecta) in the Plantain (Plantaginaceae) family. It's a tiny plant that mostly grows in disturbed places like a weed, but it is native, unlike its weedy relatives. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2005)

File.Name: 5403d_5402-016.jpg
Notes: 9815889 This is the other native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia parviflora, not C. perfoliata) growing in a place near home that burned two years ago. Something strange is happening to the foliage. Whole populations of this plant are turning this mottled-brown color. They are still flowering. Que pasa? (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2005)

File.Name: 5403e_5402-027.jpg
Notes: 9815779 Pretty flowers of native Yellow-throated Phacelia (Phacelia brachyloba) telling the story of life from bud to fading. The old flower in the upper-left is not just fading - that's ripness, not decay. This is a fire-follower wildflower with clown colors growing in a place near home that burned two years ago. It will scatter its seed for next year and the next fire in years to come. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2005)

File.Name: 5403f_5403-064.jpg
Notes: 9815681 Another kind of native Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) making a move climbing the pitch between flower clusters of native Basket Bush aka "Squaw Bush" (Rhus trilobata). That spider web just happened to be there - it's not a belay line. (Sorry - I was hiking and rock climbing in Joshua Tree a week ago.) (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2005)

File.Name: 5405a_5405-321.jpg
Notes:
9834492 That`s not me. It`s another photographer trying to figure out how to get a shot of this incredible field of wildflowers where Shell Creek Road meets Highway 58 in the San Luis Obispso County backcountry about 20 miles from Santa Margarita. It`s not a secret place. There were 40+ cars and dozens of photographers, for good reason.

I like close shots and specimen photos, but Julie and I did a two-day road trip through the Santa Barbara and San Luis Obispso backcountry, and the masses of wildflowers in this wet rain year are spectacular. I took 600+ photos that I`ve barely begun to sort.

There`s not enough pixels in my camera for this many flowers. Monet would love this! (Shell Creek Road, San Luis Obispso, 5 April 2005)

File.Name: 5405b_5405-310.jpg
Notes: 9834485 Native California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) and Tidy Tips (Layia platyglossa) flowers in the mosh today. These are familiar flowers, but it was interesting watching the other serious photographers. Most had their tripods high. I'm more of a butt-up tripod-on-the-ground guy. (Shell Creek Road, San Luis Obispso, 5 April 2005)

File.Name: 5405c_5404-082.jpg
Notes:
9834477 Looking at a hillside in the Temblor Mountains on the east side of the upper Carrizo Plains - now part of the Carrizo Plain National Monument. Yellow composite flowers accent the landscape. Clouds are blowing towards me after a storm last night, and the fence (with tumbleweed) is still in the shadow. (Carrizo Plain, 4 April 2005)

See http://www.ca.blm.gov/bakersfield/carrizoplain.html

File.Name: 5405d_5404-155.jpg
Notes: 9834465 Like the road through OZ - we followed this track up into the Caliente Range on the west side of the Carrizo Plain and found endless wildflowers. The flower fields at Shell Creek and Soda Lake had endless flowers, but we found more diverity up this canyon. (Carrizo Plain, 4 April 2005)

File.Name: 5405e_5404-098.jpg
Notes: 9834456 Julie at a surprising little alkali pond and wetland in this almost desert place. The little yellow flowers that are too many to count are mostly Goldfields (Lasthenia - maybe L. Fremontii). There's still water in the pond and it's full of specled tadpoles - maybe toads? (Carrizo Plain, 4 April 2005)

File.Name: 5405f_5404-223.jpg
Notes: 9834444 Alkali flats at the upper (south) end of Soda Lake in the Carriza Plain. This is an alkali sink with so drainage, and all the plants are specially adapted to handle the salt in the soil. What a harsh landscape. And so many flowers! (Carrizo Plain, 4 April 2005)

File.Name: 5406a_5405-241.jpg
Notes:
9840793 Looking at the backside of a flower head of native Tidy Tips (Layia). I guess this is Layia munzii that favors alkaline soil, though it was growing a few miles away from Soda Lake. (Carrizo Plain, 4 April 2005)

Today's photos are all from our recent two-day trip to the Carrizo Plain National Monument. We saw endless yellow wildflowers. The rest of my photos today are some flowers that are not yellow.

File.Name: 5406b_5404-119.jpg
Notes: 9840648 I love the flowers of native Thistle Sage (Salvia carduacea). There is such a contrast between the thistle-like plant and the delicate flowers. The bright orange pollen is a perfect accent. It's having a good year with all the rain. (Carrizo Plain, 4 April 2005)

File.Name: 5406c_5405-167.jpg
Notes: 9840570 Elegant flower of native Parry's Mallow (Eremalche parryi ssp. parryi). (Carrizo Plain, 4 April 2005)

File.Name: 5406d_5404-145.jpg
Notes: 9840485 Strange flowers of native Owl's Clover (Castilleja densiflora - maybe ssp. gracilis?). Most of the color comes from the leafy bracts. Sometimes they color whole hillsides. (Carrizo Plain, 4 April 2005)

File.Name: 5406e_5405-210.jpg
Notes: 9840387 Surprising flowers of Byron Larkspur (Delphinium recurvatum) growing in the alkali flats near the south end of Soda Lake in the Carrizo Plain National Monument. The little yellow composite flowers are Alkali Goldfields (Lasthenia ferrisiae). The alkali-crusted soil is solid with these flowers. (Carrizo Plain, 4 April 2005)

File.Name: 5406f_5404-081.jpg
Notes: 9840182 Wild Rhubarb aka Canaigre (Rumex hymenosepalus) growing in the southern end of the Carrizo Plain. This surprising native looks, smells, and tastes like garden rhubarb. The Elkhorn Hills in the background are painted with yellow composite flowers. (Carrizo Plain, 4 April 2005)

File.Name: 5407a_5405-313.jpg
Notes:
9849996 Elegant Linanthus aka Babystars (Linanthus androsaceus or L. parviflorus?) flowers in the Phlox (Polemoniaceae) family. These little native flowers come in white or violet forms side by side. They are growing along a small dry creek through the huge flower fields. (Shell Creek Road, San Luis Obispso, 5 April 2005)

I should be getting new photos in the canyon at home today, but it was drizzly all morning and now it's windy. No problem. I took 650+ photos during our trip through the Carrizo Plains this week - it was windy, I tossed 3/4 of them - but I have lots more!

It's a good year for wildflowers in California. There might NEVER be another wildflower year like this. Julie and I first went to the Carrizo Plains in the mid-1970s - it's not the same now. Get out to see the flowers! There are several good "Wildflower Hotline" sites on the web to guide your trip - "Further"
   http://www.calphoto.com/wflower.htm
   http://www.theodorepayne.org/hotline.html
   http://www.natureali.org/

File.Name: 5407b_5405-318.jpg
Notes: 9849671 Beautiful native Larkspur (Delphinium parryi?) hiding in the mostly yellow wildflower fields. (Shell Creek Road, San Luis Obispso, 5 April 2005)

File.Name: 5407c_5405-295.jpg
Notes:
9849569 Native Sky Lupines (Lupinus nanus) with a field of (mostly) yellow composites behind. You can recognize these annual lupines by the intense smell as you walk past them. (Shell Creek Road, San Luis Obispso, 5 April 2005)

The background color is real. Check my [Recent Photos] archive for the last few days to see how intense these wildflower fields are!

File.Name: 5407d_5404-161.jpg
Notes: 9849437 We all know the royal California Poppies (Eschscholzia californica) that come in seed packs and line the roads, but there are several more small poppies (family Papaveraceae) in the local flora. I think this is the native Carnival Poppy (Meconella linearis). Great name - it has clown colors! (Carrizo Plain, 4 April 2005)

File.Name: 5407e_5405-296.jpg
Notes: 9849255 California Dandelion (Malacothrix californica or maybe M. glabrata). This native is another of the yellow composites making up the impressive wildflower displays in the backcountry this spring. (Shell Creek Road, San Luis Obispso, 5 April 2005)

File.Name: 5407f_5405-311.jpg
Notes: 9849059 Not all of the yellow flowers covering the backcountry fields are composites. This is a Sun Cup (Camissonia) in the a Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family - I guess this is the California Sun Cup (Camissonia campestris ssp. obispoensis?) with mostly decumbent stems. It was hiding in a field of yellow composites near where Shell Creek Road meets Highway 58, about 20 miles east of Santa Margarita. (Shell Creek Road, San Luis Obispso, 5 April 2005)
CommonNames: Sun Cup; Evening Primrose; California Sun Cup

File.Name: 5408a_5404-207.jpg
Notes:
9857824 Beep Beep! This is a Greater Roadrunner (Geococcyx californianus) bird running past me carrying a twig for its nest. It was persistent, and so was I! I followed it for a while before out in the field. (See [Previous] photo.) Then it ran past me into a bunch of Saltbush (Atriplex - maybe A. polycarpa?) shrubs. I didn't want to disturb it by following too close, so I circled around to peek. I did find a large hole in the ground with a bit of a twig at the entrance, but my book on "Western Birds' Nests" says that Roadrunners nest in "low trees or bush, rarely on ground". Wherever it's building its nest, this was a lucky shot! (Carrizo Plain, 4 April 2005)

I tried to get new photos at home today, but it was too windy. Then the winds slowed down and it started raining. *Sigh* I'm worried because we're scheduled to take a boat to Santa Barbara Island tomorrow morning with the botanist who wrote the island flora. Now there are gale warnings in the channel. I hope this trip happens!

UPDATE:
D'oh - our island trip is now officially canceled. Our UCSB ornithologist neighbor suggests the roadrunner dropped the stick at the hole just to confuse me. LOL - it worked!

File.Name: 5408b_5404-206.jpg
Notes:
9857574 Most of the Carrizo Plains is not covered by wildflowers. We had to hunt for those special places. Lots of short grasses - Cheat Grass/, Bromus, Rye - Filaree, Fiddleneck, and more that shows the grazing history when this was ranch land rather than a National Monument. Here's a view looking from the Goodwin Education Center towards the Caliente Mountains. The low shrubs are probably Saltbush (Atriplex - maybe A. polycarpa?). The big rock outcrop in the background is nearly 100 feet high and formed in a U-shape like a natural amphitheater. It contains the largest native American Chumash rock art site. Unfortunately it's been badly vandelized over the last 100 years. Access to the rock is closed now to protect nesting raptors.

I'm rambling for a reason. Did you notice the Roadrunner (Geococcyx californianus) bird in the foreground? (Carrizo Plain, 4 April 2005)

File.Name: 5408c_5404-091.jpg
Notes:
9857227 I reckon this is a Burrowing Bee (Andrenidae but maybe Halictidae) digging a new burrow. It was remarkably fast - I could watch it dig in. There were hundreds of these holes evenly spaced an inch or two apart on bare patches of ground near a small vernal pond on the south end of the Carrizo Plains. I believe these bees are communal, but not quite social in the way that honeybees share a nest. They must get some protection from nesting together. The air was loud with buzzing bees. I was careful where I walked! (Carrizo Plain, 4 April 2005)

I have a photo of the location at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=9834456
The bees are burrowing into the bare areas between the flower patches in the photo. I remember reading Niko Tinbergen about sand wasps - I was temped to move the little stones around to see if the bees get confused! (I didn't.)

File.Name: 5408d_5404-092.jpg
Notes: 9857075 A nest hole of a Burrowing Bee (I guess Andrenidae but maybe Halictidae). I don't think the rim of that hole is glued together, but I didn't poke it to find out. See my [Next] photo for more info. The corkscrew-twisted seed in front is from the introduced weed Filaree (Erodium) that grows everywhere. (Carrizo Plain, 4 April 2005)

File.Name: 5408e_5404-118.jpg
Notes: 9856920 A pair of mating Crane Flies (Tipulidae) hanging on a flower head of native Thistle Sage (Salvia carduacea). Their long delicate legs break off so easily, but they seem perfectly adapted to hanging from plants like this. They look like giant mosquitoes, and kids at school call them "Mosquito Hawks". As far as I know, the adults have reduced mouthparts and don't feed at all. Do Crane Fly pupae make naughty jokes about two-headed flies?! And I really wonder - which way did this pair take off? (Carrizo Plain, 4 April 2005)

File.Name: 5408f_5405-329.jpg
Notes: 9856793 Very large - quarter size? - Bumblebee (Bombus) working a flower of native Thistle Sage (Salvia carduacea). The bee has an orange abdomen that is almost the same color as the pollen of the flower. I also see the same color on its legs, and it sure looks like pollen collected from the flowers. You can see why in the photo. This bee is so large, it has to straddle neighboring flowers to hang on. Now that's an interesting pollination strategy! (Shell Creek Road, San Luis Obispso, 5 April 2005)

File.Name: 5409a_5409-018.jpg
Notes:
9866363 Pretty bee-like Flower Fly (Syrphidae) perched on a leaf in the woods. I like the bronze-colored thorax covered with yellow hairs - there are many more hairs than you can see on the far edge. These flies have a hard exoskeleton, so every single hair must have a special opening! Are there really enough genes in a fly's chromosomes to encode the info for every single hair? (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2005)

It was SO WINDY today that the flowers on top of the plants were moving more than the flies and wasps hugging the stems and leaves below. I looked for insects today. I did see a few butterflies (mostly Blues and Painted Ladies) blowing past at 20+ mph! These flies and wasps are mostly preditors or parasites that lay eggs to attack other insect larvae. I guess it makes sense that these are our early insects on the wing. They are ready to take advantage of the rest of the insect brood as they take that hazardous path from egg to adult. Beautiful nature - and tough.

File.Name: 5409b_5409-023.jpg
Notes: 9866107 I think this is our common native California Harvester Ant (Pogonomyrmex californicus or kin) working the odd flowers of this Euphorbia in our garden. I usually call these critters "piss ants" which is a pun. They do have a strong "ant" smell, and they sure can bite if you disturb a nest. (The worst is when you're climbing up a tree!) It looks like they are also pollinators, with those yellow pollen grains stuck on the ant's head. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2005)

File.Name: 5409c_5409-015.jpg
Notes: 9865988 Pretty little bug-eyed Wasp (Hymenoptera) hanging onto a leaf in the woods during the strong winds today. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2005)

File.Name: 5409d_5409-014.jpg
Notes: 9865934 Native Scavenger Fly (Sarcophagidae) resting on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2005)

File.Name: 5409e_5409-011.jpg
Notes: 9865909 My what big eyes you have! Little native Fly (Diptera) hanging on to a leaf in the woods today as the winds blow. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2005)

File.Name: 5409f_5409-027.jpg
Notes:
9865830 Cute little bee-like Flower Fly (Syrphidae) perched on a stem of Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis) in our garden. Its head looks way too big, like one of those bobblehead dolls. You can see the typical four seeds of all Mint (Lamiaceae) family plants developing in the calyx on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2005)

It was SO WINDY today that the flowers on top of the plants were moving more than the flies and other insects hanging onto the stems and leaves below.

File.Name: 5410a_5410-049.jpg
Notes:
9875009 I guess this is a female Silvery Blue (Gaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly resting on a shrub in the canyon. It was cavorting with the male in the [Previous] photo. It looks like a very plain butterfly with a bit of blue - but look closer. It has a tiny brilliant sky-blue window on its left upper forewing with astounding color, a bit up and to the right from the center of the photo. I thought it might be a stuck pixel, but it shows in every photo of this critter. None of my butterfly books mentions this. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2005)

It's my ambition to learn - and photograph - local butterflies this year. I need a longer lens than my favorite Nikkor 105mm micro that can still focus close enough to catch butterflies from a distance. Any advice?

File.Name: 5410b_5410-054.jpg
Notes: 9874887 I guess this is a male Silvery Blue (Gaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly resting on a weedy Bur Clover (Medicago polymorpha) in the canyon. A few minutes before this I got a photo of it feeding on the flower. My "Butterflies Through Binoculars" book says that it feeds on a "wide variety of legumes, especially lupines, deerweed, and astragalus." Both Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) and Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) were growing nearby. (See [Previous] photo.) My photos do not quite match the book, but this species runs to varieties. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2005)
CommonNames: Silvery Blue; Bur Clover; Deerweed; Collar Lupine
ScientificName: Gaucopsyche lygdamus; Medicago polymorpha; Lotus scoparius; Lupinus truncatus
associatedTaxa: Gaucopsyche lygdamus; Medicago polymorpha; Lotus scoparius; Lupinus truncatus

File.Name: 5410c_5410-042.jpg
Notes: 9874725 Flowers of Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the canyon. Many lupines have flowers in whorls, but the flowers of this annual are spread out one by one along the stem. The butterflies in the [next] two photos were interested in these flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2005)

File.Name: 5410d_5410-066.jpg
Notes: 9874662 Little native ant, maybe a California Harvester Ant (Pogonomyrmex californicus or kin) approaching a flower of Greek Sage (Salvia fructicosa?) in the garden. I'm a teacher at the end of spring break - back to work tomorrow. I know, for these ants it's always work work work. But what a beautiful workplace! (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2005)

File.Name: 5410e_5410-074.jpg
Notes: 9874518 Little Beetle (Coleoptera) slurping the sweet nectar on a flower of native Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) on the dry side of our garden. This lily-relative grows from a bulb, and we rescued this one from a slide on the road a few years ago after a heavy rain. The beetle is not quite big enough to rub against the stamens of the flower to transfer pollen. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2005)

File.Name: 5410f_5410-034.jpg
Notes: 9874450 Pretty Flower Fly (Syrphidae) visiting a flower of Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis) in the garden. This little fly doesn't quite have the mass to tip the scale and bring the stamens down onto its back. This is one of many ways that different insects cheat the plants to get something for nothing. Little Bumblebees (Bombus) are also working the flowers, so the plant does get some reward for the nectar it offers. In the long run, that's enough. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2005)

File.Name: 5417a_5416-004.jpg
Notes: 9930028 Native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) flowers in the canyon with the sun just right. We have several species of lupines in the canyon. This one is perennial with flowers in whorls. I don't see many insects visiting these handsome flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2005)

File.Name: 5417b_5416-007.jpg
Notes: 9929977 Elegant new buds of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) flowers on the dry side of our garden. It's a different kind of Mariposa Lily. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2005)

File.Name: 5417c_5417-061.jpg
Notes: 9929908 "You looking at me?" The woods must be full of insect larvae - all those critters I'll see next summer - but they usually stay hidden. I caught a glimpse of this little caterpillar spinning a web to pull together two leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) to make a secret coccoon. It must be producing the web from its mouth. It was very deliberately putting its head down, and then pulling a web to the other side and cinching it up tight. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2005)

File.Name: 5417d_5417-049.jpg
Notes: 9929738 These spiny caterpillars will become Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona) butterflies in a month or two. The communal web-building caterpillars spin webs over whole branches of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia), but they don't usually kill the shrubs. The larvae will come back to our garden as brilliant adult butterflies in summer. Creepy, but there will be beauty. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2005)

File.Name: 5417e_5416-014.jpg
Notes:
9929540 This is a common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis) flowers in our garden. The bee is resting on the lower petal of the flower. That's the fulcrum that brings the stamen down on its back to deposit pollen. The bee is carrying a big load of pollen, but the special "pollen baskets" on its rear legs are empty. Will other worker bees harvest the pollen back in the hive? The photo is not quite sharp, but it's an action shot! (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2005)

Rosemary is not a native plant in California, and Honey Bees are not native insects either. Are they are made for each other?
CommonNames: Honey Bee; Rosemary; Rosemary; Honey Bees

File.Name: 5417f_5416-051.jpg
Notes: 9929367 Little hump-backed Fly (Diptera) gazing at the riches in a native Fiesta Flower (Pholistoma auritum) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family. This is a party flower. The stems and leaves have backwards pointing "retorse bristles" that stick to your clothes like velcro to make an easy decoration for a hike or fiesta. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2005)

File.Name: 5428a_5423-015.jpg
Notes:
9980623 "You looking at me?" I crept up as close as I could to this Two-striped Garter Snake (Thamnophis hammondii) in the garden with my camera and ground-level tripod. The snake watched my every move. A moment later it took off one way and I jerked the other way! (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2005)

I have more good critter photos from the weekend. This is my "test shot" to see if the cranky new servers are working. More photos tomorrow I hope. The "server problem" is no big deal. It's happened before and it will happen again.

File.Name: 5429a_5423-004.jpg
Notes: 9983759 Drama in the throat of a Cymbidium orchid - it's in a pot, but outside in the garden. The little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on top has caught a tiny fly. What is that tiny white thing in the middle near the bottom that looks like a jumping trout? What a world for this little spider predator to explore... where did you go to work today? (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2005)

File.Name: 5429b_5423-042.jpg
Notes: 9983704 Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae) working a new composite flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon. What a schnozz! (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2005)

File.Name: 5430a_5423-012.jpg
Notes: 9987147 Close encounter with a Two-striped Garter Snake (Thamnophis hammondii) in the garden with my camera and ground-level tripod. This snake watched my every move as I inched in closer and closer - eye to eye, tete a tete, you and me brother! A moment later it took off. Julie saw it again a day later by the compost pile. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2005)

File.Name: 5430b_5423-017.jpg
Notes: 9987041 Beautiful West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly resting on a leaf in the canyon. The latin scientific names of butterflies and plants and other critters are scary at first, but this scientific name "vanessa annabella" is almost as pretty as the butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2005)

File.Name: 5430c_5423-036.jpg
Notes: 9986999 Life wears us down after a while. You can see it in this threadbare male Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly - probably the Southern Blue (G.l. australis). I caught this one resting on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera), but it was more interested in the Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2005)
CommonNames: Silvery Blue; Southern Blue; Black Sage; Collar Lupine
ScientificName: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Salvia mellifera; Lupinus truncatus
associatedTaxa: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Salvia mellifera; Lupinus truncatus

File.Name: 5430d_5423-031.jpg
Notes: 9986957 This spiny caterpillar will become a Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona) butterfly in a month or two if it evades all the wasp and fly preditor/parasites that prey on them. Groups of these caterpillars spin communal webs for protection over whole branches of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2005)

File.Name: 5501a_5501-013.jpg
Notes:
9990347 We don't usually think of grass having flowers, but here they are. This is a native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass showing its gangly yellow male stamens and feathery white female pistil. No petals, but the important sexy stuff is all here. We've been encouraging this native grass at home, and it's doing very well. Be very careful pulling weeds if you don't know exactly what they are - especially grasses! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2005)

Today's photos are all green. That's how it is this year after record rainfall here in central California.

File.Name: 5501b_5501-026.jpg
Notes: 9990328 This is the dance of life. I was trying to sneak up on the female butterfly on the right, when the bigger and bluer male landed on the left with his intent clear and wings aflutter. He's making his move. A moment later they were both fluttering away together in the air. I think these are Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterflies - probably the Southern Blue (G.l. australis). They are perched on a Bromus grass growing up into their native host plant Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2005)

File.Name: 5501c_5501-027.jpg
Notes: 9990300 Native Plume Moth (Lepidoptera, family Pterophoridae) resting on a stem of Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the canyon. It has the shape of a cross. How does it fold its wings like that? (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2005)

File.Name: 5501d_5429-248.jpg
Notes: 9990280 Bristly Tachnid Fly (Tachinidae) resting on a palmate leaf of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) flowers in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2005)

File.Name: 5501e_5501-018.jpg
Notes: 9990268 I think this spiny caterpillar will become a Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona) butterfly next month. These caterpillars spin communal silk webs like tent caterpillars that cover whole branches of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the canyon. They don't help the plants, but they don't kill them. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2005)
CommonNames: caterpillar; Chalcedon Checkerspot; tent caterpillars; Climbing Penstemon

File.Name: 5501f_5430-002.jpg
Notes: 9990246 Larva of a Ladybird Beetle aka "Ladybug" (Coleoptera, family Coccinellidae) on a leaf of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis). I reckon this adolescent is ready to pupate since there were many shed skins hanging on the willow leaves. It seems to be exuding tiny drops of some elixer from its back. Reminds me of one of those Japanese anime creatures with armor plates. We watched a new adult emerge nearby - creamy white with no color. I guess the red color comes later. (Figueroa Mountain, 30 April 2005)

File.Name: 5505a_5423-024.jpg
Notes:
10018592 Some kind of perennial Mountain Dandelion (Agoseris grandiflora?) in the woods. The composite flowers don't open much more than this. They develop large seed heads like danedlions, only more so. This looks pagen, with the green phyllaries shielding an ancient rite inside. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2005)

Todays photos all show plants with something to hide.

File.Name: 5505b_5424-054.jpg
Notes: 10018509 Elegant new flower of Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the canyon. It's a demure kind of Mariposa Lily that hides its private parts rather than showing them. That swollen reddish area at the base of the petal (on top) is the sweet nectar gland hidden inside to entice pollinators. Stiff hairs along the edge of the petals keep them there until finished. Nature is like that. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2005)

File.Name: 5505c_5424-053.jpg
Notes: 10018435 Pretty flower of native Sticky Cinquefoil (Potentilla glandulosa) in the canyon. The stamens look like little lollipops! (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2005)

File.Name: 5505d_5424-046.jpg
Notes: 10018410 English Plantain (Plantago lanceolata) is one of those darn weeds that's everywhere, even up here in the mountains. The flower head doesn't even look like a flower, but it has the important parts like these pollen-bearing male stamens on long anthers and the female pistils almost hidden in the flower head. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2005)

File.Name: 5505e_5501-025.jpg
Notes: 10018336 Scarlet Pimpernel aka Poor Man's Weather Glass (Anagalis arvensis) is a common non-native weed, but the humble flowers are beautiful up close. The common names are confusing. The color is salmon, not scarlet. The flowers do close under clouds and open in the sun. But it's a very poor man who would look at the ground rather than the sky to see if the sun is shining. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2005)
CommonNames: Scarlet Pimpernel; Poor Man's Weather Glass

File.Name: 5505f_5501-036.jpg
Notes: 10018232 I noticed these tiny ants moving in slow circles around the glandular base of this native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) flower in the canyon slurping up nectar. These ants are tiny. I hope they are not the invasive Argentine Ants (Iridomyrmex humilis) that are invading our buildings at school and the rest of California. We haven't seen these ants in our house, and I have seen very similar ants on other flowers. Compare my photo at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=8352074. There are so many ants (and plants and everything else) in nature, it's important not to jump to conclusions! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2005)

File.Name: 5508a_5406-333.jpg
Notes:
10038791 This native Blue-Eyed Grass (Sisyrinchium bellum) plant came up all by itself on the dry side of our garden. It's in the Iris (Iridaceae) family, and it grows wild just up the hill. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2005)

I never got out the door this weekend. I'm hard at work sorting through 1000s of my photos for the school end-of-the-year slideshow. I found these photos in my "Ready" folder - along with many more in the "Good Stuff" folder. Two weeks to go!

File.Name: 5508b_5501-031.jpg
Notes: 10038670 Chinese Houses (Collinsia heterophylla) in the woods. The whorled flowers look like lupines at first, but they are really quite different. They are in the Figwort Family (Scrophulariaceae), related to penstemon and paintbrush. The flower color varies from violet to almost white. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2005)

File.Name: 5508c_5417-062.jpg
Notes: 10038629 Some kind of Sage (Salvia sp.) starting to flower in the garden. I can't find my book on garden salvias right now, so that's all I know. Beautiful flowers on long stalks that lay down after a rain like we had last week. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2005)

File.Name: 5508d_5409-012.jpg
Notes: 10038489 Pretty little bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae) resting on a leaf in the garden. It's a good disguise for this beneficial fly that lays eggs that hatch into larvae that pray on aphids and other pests. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2005)

File.Name: 5508e_5423-002.jpg
Notes: 10038401 Dead female Anna's Hummingbird returning its atoms to the earth. We found it in our house, and I hope our cat didn't kill it. I have trouble getting within 10 feet of these hummers to catch a photo, let alone catching it in my hand. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2005)

File.Name: 5508f_5410-071.jpg
Notes:
10038308 Look close at this Blue Belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) in the garden and see that it has a Tick (order Acarina, suborder Ixodides) attached near it's ear opening on the side. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2005)

I've heard that these lizard ticks are immature forms of the adult ticks that plague us. There's some evidence that ticks that feed on lizards first are purged of Lyme disease bacteria later.
  See http://www.msmosquito.com/SF41798.html
CommonNames: Blue Belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Tick

File.Name: 5513a_5512-031.jpg
Notes: 10071257 Mud Dauber (Sphecidae) wasps hunt for spiders to provision their nests. But sometimes they need a nectar energy hit from a flower like this native California Figwort aka Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica). It's an intimate moment. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2005)

File.Name: 5513b_5512-027.jpg
Notes: 10071231 I love these elegant native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) flowers in the canyon. It's a kind of Mariposa Lily that hangs down to hide its private parts rather than stand up to show them. That swollen area at the base of the petal (on top) is the sweet nectar gland hidden inside to entice pollinators. The stiff hairs along the edge of the petals make a trap to hold insects inside until they finish what they start. Nature is like that. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2005)

File.Name: 5513c_5513-047.jpg
Notes: 10071207 These new flowers of Rock Phacelia (Phacelia imbricata) aren`t very colorful, but they have great geometry. This photo shows the typical "scorpioid inflorescence" of all Phacelia species in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family - curled up like a scorpion`s tail or a fiddle neck. (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2005)

File.Name: 5513d_5513-049.jpg
Notes: 10071194 Grasses do have flowers, but they are simplified since they are wind-pollinated and don't need petals to attract insects as pollinators. This is a native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass showing its gangly yellow male stamens and feathery white female pistils. We've been encouraging this native grass at home, and it's doing very well. (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2005)

File.Name: 5513e_5513-052.jpg
Notes: 10071176 This flower of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) shows the female style up high and the male pollen-bearing stamens down lower. The sexual parts of these flowers are usually hidden, but they are spring-loaded to "goose" a visiting insect to deliver or receive pollen. The female part seems to come first in this flower, then the male. (See [Previous] photo.) I reckon this flower has done its job. (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2005)

File.Name: 5513f_5513-051.jpg
Notes: 10071128 Flowers aren't just pretty things to admire and photograph. They are the sexual parts of plants, and they often take an active role. These flowers of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) are typical Pea Family (Fabaceae) flowers with a banner petal on top to attract insects, and two wing petals on the sides that make a platform to clasp the fused keel petals inside that hold the "goodies" - the male stamens and female pistil. When an insect lands on this platform, it triggers the spring-loaded goodies inside to poke up and deliver or recieve pollen. This flower seems to be ladies first, with the female stigma popping up before the male stamens. (Compare [Next] photo.) Sometimes you can tap one of these flowers with a twig to see the action! (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2005)
ScientificName: Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; banner; wing; keel
associatedTaxa: Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; banner; wing; keel

File.Name: 5528a_5526-074.jpg
Notes:
10168620 Ladybird Beetle aka "Ladybug" (Coleoptera, family Coccinellidae) skewered on a spine of Silver Cholla (Opuntia echinocarpa) cactus. I reckon it was an ill wind that blew this beetle to its fate. I've been "cholla'd" myself, and needed pliers to get free. (Owens Valley, 26 May 2005)

I've been too busy with school - sequencing my 2000+ photo school slide show and leading the 8th grade graduation trip last week to our cabin in the desert Alabama Hills near Lone Pine, California on the eastern side of the Sierra Nevada Mountains. I found some time for photos while the kids were doing their sunrise solo meditation. Just one week to go...

File.Name: 5528b_5526-027.jpg
Notes: 10168463 Tiny bright red Mites (Acari) on a composite flower head of Cooper's Goldenbush (Ericameria cooperi). I don't usually think of these spider-relative Mites as pollinators, but here they are working the tiny florets and dusted with yellow pollen. I guess small flowers need small pollinators. (Owens Valley, 26 May 2005)

File.Name: 5528c_5526-036.jpg
Notes: 10168416 Pretty little flowers of Desert Calico (>Loeseliastrum matthewsii) with tiny red Mites (Acari) exploring the flowers. You have to get down on your knees to notice this tiny desert plant that only grows an inch or two high on bare ground. Note the little Mite hanging on the stamens in the upper-left. These tiny spider-relatives are pollinators! (Owens Valley, 26 May 2005)

File.Name: 5528d_5526-039.jpg
Notes: 10168297 A little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working the beautiful composite flowers of this native Mojave Aster (Xylorhiza tortifolia). Look close and see the tiny orange Thrips (order Thysanoptera) on the petals. (Owens Valley, 26 May 2005)

File.Name: 5528e_5526-051.jpg
Notes: 10168237 A flower of native Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua) with insect visitors. There is a small hump-backed fly (order Diptera) resting on a stigma on the right, and many tiny orange Thrips (order Thysanoptera) at the base of the composite florets that make up this flower head. (Owens Valley, 26 May 2005)

File.Name: 5528f_5524-079.jpg
Notes: 10168190 It's a beetle (Coleoptera) party inside this flower of Beavertail Cactus (Opuntia basilaris). (Owens Valley, 24 May 2005)
ScientificName: Coleoptera) party inside this flower of Beavertail Cactus (Opuntia basilaris
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera) party inside this flower of Beavertail Cactus (Opuntia basilaris

File.Name: 5531a_5524-087.jpg
Notes: 10193291 Looking into a flower head of native Mojave Thistle (Cirsium mohavense) growing at the base of a rock outcrop in the desert east of Mount Whitney. There are too many weedy thistles, but the natives are beautiful and rare. Don't damage them by mistake. (Owens Valley, 24 May 2005)

File.Name: 5531b_5524-090.jpg
Notes: 10193246 Beautiful flower of native Mojave Thistle (Cirsium mohavense) growing at the base of a rock outcrop in the Alabama Hills in the desert east of Mount Whitney. This is not a weed! (Owens Valley, 24 May 2005)

File.Name: 5603a_5602-009.jpg
Notes:
10213780 This new flower of native Soap Plant (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) just opened in the ten minutes it took to walk past and return, about 5:00 in the afternoon. It's an interesting plant in the Lily (Liliaceae) family with many traditional uses including food, soap, and fiber. (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 June 2005)

I've been way too busy with my school slide show - 1,928 photos in 24 segments, with different music for each part, sequenced to video at just under one hour total. I slipped away for a break to get these new photos while the DVD burned, even though it was windy. The slideshow was great, we had our school graduation ceremony today. It's almost summer!

Notes: 10213550 Pretty flowers of native Canchalagua (Centaurium venustum or maybe C. davyi) in the Gentian (Gentianaceae) family. It's a pretty little wildflower in an unusual plant family with an ancient name that suggests use as a medicinal herb. It still has herbal and homeopathic uses. (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 June 2005)

File.Name: 5603d_5602-013.jpg
Notes: 10213465 New flowers of native composite California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia, aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia). (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 June 2005)

File.Name: 5603e_5526-089.jpg
Notes: 10213426 Ripe seeds of Desert Needlegrass (Achnatherum speciosum aka Stipa speciosa) in the desert. The long hairy, bent, once-twisted awns remind me of trout fishing flies. (Owens Valley, 26 May 2005)

File.Name: 5603f_5526-030.jpg
Notes: 10213354 Ripe seeds of native Indian Ricegrass (Achnatherum hymenoides aka Oryzopsis hymenoides) in the desert. The grass is good forage, and Native Americans collected the seeds for food. Because of overgrazing, it's not as common as it used to be. (Owens Valley, 26 May 2005)

File.Name: 5604a_5604-056.jpg
Notes: 10220780 Native Checker Bloom (Sidalcea malviflora) flower catching a late afternoon sunbeam just right. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2005)

File.Name: 5604b_5604-020.jpg
Notes: 10220749 Pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae) working a flower of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata) in the canyon. The flower has a deep throat, so I wonder what this fly is getting with its not-so-long proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2005)

File.Name: 5604c_5604-043.jpg
Notes: 10220691 I guess this is a male Acmon Blue (Icaricia acmon aka Plebejus acmon) butterfly resting on a branch of its native larval food plant Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2005)
ScientificName: Icaricia acmon; Plebejus acmon; Lotus scoparius
associatedTaxa: Icaricia acmon; Plebejus acmon; Lotus scoparius

File.Name: 5604d_5604-032.jpg
Notes: 10220617 Dandelion-like seeds of native Mountain Dandelion (Agoseris heterophylla) still hanging on. It's graduation time for many school kids, and these seeds are also ready to move on. I find the pretty seeds everywhere in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2005)

File.Name: 5604e_5604-037.jpg
Notes: 10220579 Flower spikes of Red Brome (Bromus rubens) grass catching the sun just right. It's another introduced weedy grass growing along the road, but it doesn't spread as much as some of the other alien weeds in our woods. It sure is pretty with the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2005)

File.Name: 5604f_5604-008.jpg
Notes: 10220534 New flower of Borage (Borago officinalis) in our garden. It's an ancient herb that has naturalized and reseeds itself every year in different places. I like to add the pretty flowers to our salads. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2005)

File.Name: 5605a_5605-072.jpg
Notes: 10228278 I've been chasing this critter for a week. I was sure it's a Flower Fly (Syrphidae) that mimics a bee. Now I'm sure it's a bee that mimics a fly that mimics a bee! It hovers with a high-pitched whine and flies forwards and backwards like a fly. It's very territorial on its patch of native Rock Phacelia (Phacelia imbricata) flowers. When I try to approach, it hovers and faces me head-on and tries to drive me off. It's spooky to make eye contact with an insect! But this photo gives it away as a real Bee (Apoidea) with chewing mouthparts. It seems to have pollen-collecting hairs under its abdomen. I guess it is a Mason or Resin Bee in the family Megachilidae that includes the Leafcutter Bees. The phacelia flowers have long exerted stamens that seem a perfect fit to tickle its underside to achieve pollination. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2005)
CommonNames: Flower Fly; Rock Phacelia; Bee; Mason or Resin Bee; Leafcutter Bees

File.Name: 5605b_5605-062.jpg
Notes: 10228156 Bristly little spider resting on a leaf in the graden - maybe a Jumping Spider (Salticidae)? It's in "relaxed" position. Spiders and other arthropods have a hard exoskeleton. They have flexor muscles to pull their legs in, but no extensor muscles to push them out again like we have. Instead, they use hydraulic pressure, pumping "blood" into each limb to push it out, like turning on the faucet unrolls the hose. That's why spiders always curl their legs in when they relax or die. Sounds funky, but it sure works well for these critters! (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2005)

File.Name: 5605c_5605-064.jpg
Notes: 10228048 Native male Timema insect hiding with perfect camouflage along a leaf of White Sage (Salvia apiana). These critters are unusual walkingstick (order Phasmatodea) insects. They seem pretty slow-moving. I rarely notice them, but with that camouflage they could be everywhere. I don't know what they do in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2005)

File.Name: 5605d_5605-073.jpg
Notes: 10227945 We had so much rain this year in Central California, and everything is late. Insects are lagging behind the flowers, but they are doing their thing. This is an immature long-horned Katydid (Tettigoniidae grasshopper resting on a leaf of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea). You can see new wing-buds on the side. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2005)

File.Name: 5605e_5604-047.jpg
Notes:
10227858 Inside the Colossal Cave, there is a spider...

This "cave" is deep in the throat of a native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Diplacus longiflorus) flower. It's a natural light tent. I didn't "photoshop" the little spider. I don't know why it shows those two big black eyes when all spiders have 6 or more eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2005)

File.Name: 5605f_5604-022.jpg
Notes: 10227742 I know it's not quite in focus, but the action is perfect. This little wasp-like Flower Fly (Syrphidae) is laying eggs with its extended abdomen on a Sage (Salvia sp.) in the garden. The eggs will be the doom of other insects who live on the plant. I guess they wait until eaten by larvae and then develop within, like the movie "Alien". This fly only perched for a moment, so it must spread its eggs around. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2005)

File.Name: 5606a_5606-091.jpg
Notes: 10232915 New flowers of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula var. vacillans) in the canyon, catching a beam of morning sunlight just right, like a candelabra lit from within. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2005)

File.Name: 5606b_5606-092.jpg
Notes: 10232890 Looking close at new flowers of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula var. vacillans) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2005)

File.Name: 5606c_5604-006.jpg
Notes: 10232835 Like a clown puppet with frilled collar and a hat made from shag carpet - this is really a flower of Shirley Poppy aka Corn Poppy or Field Poppy (Papaver rhoeas) in the garden. Are those brown spots on the future seed pod Scale Insects (Coccoidea)? (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2005)
CommonNames: Shirley Poppy; Corn Poppy; Field Poppy; Scale Insects

File.Name: 5606d_5606-078.jpg
Notes: 10232590 This looks like something living on a coral reef under the sea. It's the center of a flower of Oriental Poppy (Papaver somniferum). The raised white lines radiating from the center are the receptive female stigmas of the flower, surrounded by male stamens releasing pollen. How do these flowers avoid self-pollination? (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2005)

File.Name: 5606e_5606-085.jpg
Notes: 10232508 After the party - looking inside a night-blooming flower of native Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri) early the next morning. This flower looks barren. No pollen has been transfered from the male stamens (right) to the female pistil (left). It's been an odd year in central California with so much rain. The usual insect pollinators are late. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2005)

File.Name: 5606f_5606-081.jpg
Notes: 10232357 Mature Band-wing Grasshopper (family Acrididae) trying to look like a piece of oak bark on the ground. Note the tan color bands across its back that match the fallen oak leaves on the ground - and the colors line up with the tan patches on its hind "drumstick" leg. Now that's camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2005)

File.Name: 5607a_5604-011.jpg
Notes:
10242528 Looking close at flowers of Inyo Bush Lupine (Lupinis excubitus var. excubitus). (Owens Valley, 26 May 2005)

I've been finishing school grades and comments today. No time for new photos, but I found these in my "Ready" folder. Now it really is almost summer!

File.Name: 5607b_5604-039.jpg
Notes: 10242493 Ripening seeds of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) made visible with the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2005)

File.Name: 5607c_5604-052.jpg
Notes: 10242477 An approaching bee's view of the inviting landing decks of a pair of Chinese Houses (Collinsia heterophylla) flowers in the woods. Looks like those flowers are wearing shades! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2005)

File.Name: 5607d_5604-053.jpg
Notes: 10242433 Beautiful flower of native Fringed Indian Pink (Silene laciniata ssp. major) in the canyon. The species name "laciniata" is from the Latin for "deeply cut", as with scissors. It's a perfect name for this flower with these lacerated petals. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2005)

File.Name: 5607e_5526-084.jpg
Notes: 10242372 I've been noticing dandelion-like composite seeds everywhere in the woods. The bottom two seeds are from Mountain Dandelion (Agoseris heterophylla), but I'm not sure about the top one. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2005)

File.Name: 5608a_5608-106.jpg
Notes: 10247758 Insect love - part one. (See [Previous] photo.) This pair of little Rove Beetles (family Staphylinidae) are conceiving new baby beetles on a composite flower of native Green Everlasting (Gnaphalium californicum). I guess that's the male on the left arching its abdomen over its head like a scorpion and holding the female's abdomen with his brown "claspers". It's a brief encounter that only lasts a minute or less. These Rove Beetles are easy to recognize by their short wing covers that leave the soft abdomen completely exposed. There are many different kinds. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2005)
CommonNames: Rove Beetles; Green Everlasting; Rove Beetles

File.Name: 5608b_5608-110.jpg
Notes: 10247615 Insect love - part two. (See [Next] photo.) After disengaging, the lower male Rove Beetle (family Staphylinidae) is either being tender to the female or inspecting his work. Note the brown-colored "claspers" exposed at the tip of his curved abdomen. The flower is a composite head of native Green Everlasting (Gnaphalium californicum). (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2005)

File.Name: 5608c_5608-099.jpg
Notes: 10247518 Little wasp-like Flower Fly (Syrphidae) working a composite flower of Common Sow Thistle (Sonchus oleraceus). Even this all-too-common weed flower has charms for man and fly up close. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2005)

File.Name: 5608d_5608-096.jpg
Notes: 10247432 One of the last native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) flowers in the canyon, next to a ripening seed pod. Little bees crawl under that fringed petal to get to the nectar glands at the base of the petals inside. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2005)

File.Name: 5608e_5604-033.jpg
Notes: 10247343 Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly resting on the ground. We had a huge migration of these butterflies pass by a couple of months ago. This one either stayed behind or came back. This lady looks pretty tattered. Fly on little wing! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2005)

File.Name: 5608f_5604-041.jpg
Notes: 10247268 California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly resting on a leaf of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) by the creek in the canyon. These handsome butterflies patrol their "flyways" through the woodland, but they rarely land except to "puddle" on damp soil. Do they feed? (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2005)

File.Name: 5609a_5609-145.jpg
Notes:
10256086 What we do for science! This is our worst biting fly working on that place where my leg meets my foot. (Is there a name for that flexible place between shin and foot?)

I let the fly work for a while and got some photos, resting camera on leg. I believe it's a Biting Snipe Fly (order Diptera, family Rhagionidae, genus Symphoromyia). It has a painful bite that you notice. I believe they make an incision to draw blood and then slurp it up - kind of like vampire bats. You can see a bit of my blood behind the head in this photo. These orange-brown snipe flies are fast and painful. We also have a larger dark snipe fly that is slower and easier to swat. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2005)

I'm almost glad to swat these pesky biting snipe flies this year. When we built our house 20+ years ago, these flies drove us off the scafolding because they were so nasty. I remember swatting five at a time. I've hardly noticed them for many years since. It's spooky when something natural disappears from the woods, even a pest, like a silent spring.

File.Name: 5609b_5609-154.jpg
Notes:
10255856 The moth that ate our Peruvian blanket! This is not the moth, but the pupal case of one single larva out of the many that did a good job of destroying our blanket and infest our house. It's the larval stage/caterpillar that did the damage. Maybe it's a Webbing Cloths Moth (Tineola bissella, order Lepidoptera) or one of its pesky kin. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2005)

Reminds me of Caddisflies (Trichoptera) and the Wicker Man.

File.Name: 5609c_5609-136.jpg
Notes:
10255742 The first Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona) butterfly I seen this year, lying spread-eagle (weird phrase) on a new flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon. It was cool and cloudy today, so I reckon this butterfly was trying to "soak up some rays" until I bothered it enough to move on. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2005)

Now this is strange. I believe the caterpillars of this butterfly usually spin communal web-tents on the native shrubs of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=7958213
and more in the archive. This year I've seen caterpillars on the penstemon, but I haven't seen any webs. We've had near-record rain this year. Why would that make a difference in how these critters live?

Keckiella cordifolia). See my photo at:

File.Name: 5609d_5609-134.jpg
Notes: 10255523 Native succulent Lanceleaf Liveforever aka Rock Lettuce (Dudleya lanceolata or kin) starting to flower on a rocky bank in the canyon. The flowers look sculpted from colored wax. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2005)

File.Name: 5609e_5609-123.jpg
Notes: 10255467 Beautiful new flower of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the canyon. This native thistle (with many varieties) can vary from white to red to purple, and the invading weedy Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare) can fool you. Be sure you know what you're doing before you pull any thistles as weeds in the back country. When in doubt, WAIT! But please DO kill the bad ones. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2005)

File.Name: 5609f_5609-115.jpg
Notes:
10255367 Maybe like a ninja-monkey-funny-hat-clown climbing up a giant bean stalk to rescue the princess and restore order to the known universe? Nah, it's a native wild orchid flower almost ready to open in the native part of our garden. I believe this is Purple-Flowered Piperia (Piperia michaelii

) - or maybe P. elongata? The plant looks more like an asparagus shoot than an orchid, but note the twisted ovary that turns the flower upside down like most orchids. We have at least three of these plants starting to flower on the hillside. I reckon it's one of those "environmental indicators" - orchids on the hill, interesting flies in the garden, strange mushrooms, trout in the creek - still OK! (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2005)

) - or maybe P. elongata

File.Name: 5610a_5610-185.jpg
Notes:
10261965 Push, lady, push! I think this is an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly depositing her eggs on a branch of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius). (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2005)

All of today's photos are from a place on West Camino Cielo road near home that the Forest Service Hot Shot crew cleared last summer and then burned. It's not a real wildfire area, but many fire-follower plants are doing well.

File.Name: 5610b_5610-186.jpg
Notes: 10261895 Probably an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly resting on a branch of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius), with the sun behind. I know she's a female, because I just watched her lay eggs on the same plant, see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2005)

File.Name: 5610c_5610-190.jpg
Notes: 10261807 New Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona) aka Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a new Dahlia flower in the garden. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2005)

File.Name: 5610d_5610-159.jpg
Notes: 10261772 Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a flower of native Giant-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora). Look at the flower for a moment to get your bearings. It has five violet male stamens with yellow/brown tips and hairy bottoms. It also has a two-part female style that comes from the yellow-green ovary at the bottom in the center of the flower. I'm sure this flower produces nectar from glands around the base of the ovary. Now note how the bee's tongue (aka proboscis) wraps around the ovary to get all the sweet nectar! (Maybe the proboscis only goes halfway around?) (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2005)

File.Name: 5610e_5610-165.jpg
Notes: 10261689 Little Plant Bug (order Hemiptera, maybe family Miridae) resting on a gall on native composite California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2005)

File.Name: 5610f_5610-169.jpg
Notes: 10261648 I first thought California Chicory aka California Plumseed (Rafinesquia californica), but maybe this is Malacothrix saxatilis var. tenuifolia? Either way, it's a composite with milky juice in the leaves. I hunkered down to get this shot against the sky. Notice the little insect in the upper left. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2005)
CommonNames: California Chicory; California Plumseed; Malacothrix saxatilis

File.Name: 5611a_5611-030.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 10709191 Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly depositing her eggs on a branch of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius). (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2005)

File.Name: 5611b_5611-026.jpg
Notes:
10269080 Beautiful Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly working a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon. I don't see these butterflies very often, so I was excited to capture the light from this one. My book says they feed on Nettles (Urtica dioica etc.) which don't grow up here in the mountains, but they do grow a few miles down the canyon where the creek from Cold Springs Tavern joins. I reckon this buterfly was blown up the canyon by the strong off-shore winds we've been having this week. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2005)

By the way, this Admiral is a Lady! It's related to the Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) and West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterflies that I've photographed recently.
CommonNames: Red Admiral; Canyon Sunflower; Nettles; Painted Lady; West Coast Lady
ScientificName: Vanessa atalanta; Venegasia carpesioides; Urtica dioica; Vanessa cardui; Vanessa annabella

File.Name: 5611c_5611-019.jpg
Notes: 10268980 Pretty Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly working a flower cluster of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the canyon. You can see the half-moon crescent shapes at the rear of the hindwings. My book says their food plant is thistles, and native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) is growing nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2005)

File.Name: 5611d_5611-003.jpg
Notes: 10268922 Another little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly, resting on a leaf of Small-Flowered Lotus (Lotus micranthus) along the road in the canyon. Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) is flowering nearby. Both plants are natives in the Pea Family (Fabaceae) that this pretty little butterfly favors. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2005)
