File.Name: 5611e_5611-010.jpg
Notes: 10268805 A new Skipper (Hesperiidae) butterfly on a flower of Pincushion Flower (Scabiosa atropurpurea?) in our garden. I reckon it's a Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae), probably genus Hesperia. It's very dark colored. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2005)

File.Name: 5611f_5611-001.jpg
Notes: 10268709 I guess this is a Western Tussock Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Lymantriidae, maybe Orgyia vetusta or kin) caterpillar on this leaf of native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in our canyon. The females emerge without wings to mate, lay eggs, and die. Some related species defoliate conifer trees up north. Evolution ... creation - as long as you admit that nature has a sense of humor and endless surprises, I won't argue! Well, not here anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2005)
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Lymantriidae; Orgyia vetusta; Rubus ursinus
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Lymantriidae; Orgyia vetusta; Rubus ursinus

File.Name: 5611g_5610-184.jpg
Notes: 10268557 Two "True Bugs" (order Hemiptera) mating on a flower head of Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata) in the garden. The critter on the left seems to be pulling the other along. Is that a male or female thing?? (Nah - I won't touch that one.) (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2005)

File.Name: 5612a_5604-055.jpg
Notes:
10277241 The fire hydrant in our canyon catching the afternoon light just right. Not my usual photo, but look closer to spot the Western Tussock Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Lymantriidae, maybe Orgyia vetusta or kin) caterpillar crawling over the upper valve! (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2005)

No time for photos today. My son is driving down tonight from Portland. We've been cleaning house for all the good company starting to converge for the Live Oak Music Festival next weekend:
   http://www.liveoakmusic.org/

File.Name: 5612b_5611-007.jpg
Notes: 10277118 Seedpod of Oriental Poppy (Papaver somniferum) with a new flower behind. It looks like a new world, and for some people, it really is. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2005)

File.Name: 5612c_5610-177.jpg
Notes:
10277090 Little flowers of native California Figwort aka Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica?) in a place that was cleared and burned last summer. These plants are colored deep maroon, almost black, different from he plants in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2005)

Despite the common name, I believe the flowers are adapted for large wasps rather than bees. They have stamens and stigma at the mouth of the flower rather than hidden inside. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10071257

File.Name: 5612d_5610-167.jpg
Notes: 10276921 Looking into a flower of our native composite Malacothrix saxatilis var. tenuifolia - or maybe California Chicory aka California Plumseed (Rafinesquia californica). The male stamens form a sheath around the fertile female pistil. It sure looks like there is pollen on the stems. How do they avoid self-pollinating? (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2005)
CommonNames: Malacothrix saxatilis; California Chicory; California Plumseed

File.Name: 5612e_5610-173.jpg
Notes: 10276838 Foot-long flower heads of native Giant-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora). They have the typical "scorpioid inflorescence" of all Phacelia species in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family - curled up like a scorpion`s tail or a fiddle's neck, making new flowers as they keep unrolling new buds on top. After such a rainy winter, they might just keep going and going! (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2005)

File.Name: 5612f_5610-162.jpg
Notes: 10276739 New flower of native Giant-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora). If you look close in the lower left, you might notice the orangish insect egg on a stalk. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2005)

File.Name: 5622a_5622-032.jpg
Notes: 10351987 Small native Bee (Apoidea) working a Dahlia flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2005)

File.Name: 5622b_5622-043.jpg
Notes: 10351751 Little native Bee (Apoidea) harvesting a golden flower of California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2005)

File.Name: 5622c_5622-036.jpg
Notes:
10351701 New flower head of this healing composite Purple Coneflower (Echinacea purpurea) in the garden. You can see spirals of flowers going to the right and left. If you count the spirals right and left, you should get a pair of Fibonacci Numbers with a ratio that approaches the Golden Number Phi, 1.61803399... (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2005)

For more info, check:
   http://www.mcs.surrey.ac.uk/Personal/R.Knott/Fibonacci/fibnat.html#seeds

File.Name: 5622d_5622-047.jpg
Notes: 10351565 New flower of a little Cactus (Cactaceae) in the garden. The male stamens are at the base of the flower. It's the female pistil that stands up - like a hand with a fertile glove. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2005)

File.Name: 5622e_5622-039.jpg
Notes: 10351422 These are the barren female stigmas of native Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri) flowers in the garden. It's been an odd year in central California with so much rain. The usual Sphinx Moth pollinators are late. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2005)

File.Name: 5622f_5620-027.jpg
Notes:
10351281 Live Oak Music Festival 2005 - it's just down the road from home. There's fine music on the festival stage, but my favorite event is our late night campground music jam. My kids and many old and new friends come together for this event year after year. (Santa Ynez Valley, 20 June 2005)

Check http://www.liveoakmusic.org/

File.Name: 5623a_5623-072.jpg
Notes: 10358676 Cactus (family Cactaceae - maybe Mammillaria?) starting to flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2005)

File.Name: 5623b_5623-058.jpg
Notes: 10358635 I walk or drive past these flowers of native Farewell-to-Spring aka Speckled Clarkia (Clarkia cylindrica) along our road almost every day - but they sure are amazing when I stop to look up close. They have eight male stamens divided into different inner and outer series, and a single female pistil laying down to the lower-right. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2005)

File.Name: 5623c_5623-060.jpg
Notes: 10358459 I don't usually think of caterpillars as pollinators, but this one is working a flower of California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica). Note the yellow pollen on its spines. It even seems to have a golden line on the side to match the stamens of the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2005)

File.Name: 5623d_5623-069.jpg
Notes: 10358359 Flower of native Fringed Indian Pink (Silene laciniata ssp. major) in the canyon. I didn't notice the insect hiding under the petal on the right until I got home. D'oh! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2005)

File.Name: 5623e_5623-049.jpg
Notes:
10358334 I believe this is the Green Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata

) - or maybe P. michaelii? It looks more like an asparagus than an orchid, but note the twisted ovary that turns the flowers upside down like most orchids. It has green flowers, a long spur, and a triangular lip with straight edges. I reckon it's one of those "environmental indicators" of where I want to live, like the canary in the coal mine - orchids on the hill, interesting flies in the garden, strange mushrooms in the woods, trout in the creek - still OK! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2005)
ScientificName:
Piperia elongata

) - or maybe P. michaelii
associatedTaxa:
Piperia elongata

) - or maybe P. michaelii

File.Name: 5623f_5623-052.jpg
Notes: 10358159 Not-quite-mature long-horned Katydid (Tettigoniidae) grasshopper resting "butts up" on a Potato (Solanum tuberosum) plant in the garden. It was in the same pose two hours later when I came back from my walk. Maybe it's ready for the final molt to turn those buds into wings? (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2005)

File.Name: 5624a_5624-099.jpg
Notes:
10365789 This fat mature caterpillar of native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly is munching the introduced and invasive Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) weeds along our road. Chow down! (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2005)

See my [Previous] photo, and the good caterpillar series last year starting at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=7976165

I just noticed I've been f'logging these photos at fotolog for 2+ years now, starting with this one from 21 June 2003:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=251716

I now count 2,000+ photos. This is still so much fun. Like the Grateful Dead sing, "We are the eyes of the world"!

File.Name: 5624b_5624-095.jpg
Notes:
10365527 Tiny caterpillar of our native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly happily munching the introduced and invasive Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) weeds along our road. At this stage, the caterpillers look like bird poop, which is pretty good camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2005)

See my [Next] photo, and more starting at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=7976165

File.Name: 5624c_5624-082.jpg
Notes: 10365415 This looks so much like a prancing pony with jewelled finery! I reckon it's an immature Katydid or Cricket (order Orthoptera) stepping over a leaf in the garden. This beautiful adolescent is grasshopper-like, but it must be something different with those long antennae. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2005)

File.Name: 5624d_5624-084.jpg
Notes: 10365362 Immature Katydid (Tettigoniidae) or maybe a Cricket (order Orthoptera) on a leaf in the garden. This beautiful critter is grasshopper-like, but it must be something different with those long antennae. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2005)

File.Name: 5624e_5624-079.jpg
Notes: 10365331 Immature Katydid (Tettigoniidae) or maybe a Cricket (order Orthoptera) with long antennae, on a leaf in the garden. What remarkable colors, especially the blue on the thorax above the front leg. Is the critter in the [Next] photo an earlier molt/instar of the same insect? (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2005)

File.Name: 5624f_5624-075.jpg
Notes: 10365241 Looking eye-to-eye (and eye and eye and eye...) with a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in the woods. We made eye contact. The spider followed my every move as I tried to sneak in closer. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2005)

File.Name: 5625a_5625-001.jpg
Notes:
10372501 This "bee" hovering in the air is really a Fly (order Diptera)! My first thought for any bee-mimic fly is Flower Fly (Syrphidae) or maybe Bee Fly (Bombyliidae), but the antennae and mouthparts are all wrong. I guess this is a Soldier Fly (family Stratiomyidae)?

It was breezy this morning, so I was looking for anything standing still when I noticed these "bees" hovering over the wood pile at home, always pointing into the wind and holding position. They are the size and shape and color of Honey Bees (Apis mellifera), but they hover in place and never rest. Then I noticed their amazing green-blue eyes. I took 80+ photos of these critters hovering in the air - with manual focus, using my monopod for support, trying to get a keeper. I deleted all but four, and cropped in close. (Gotta love digital ;=) (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2005)

Look how this fly holds its legs in flight - like body surfing the wind!
I'm really proud of this photo.
CommonNames: Fly; Flower Fly; Bee Fly; Soldier Fly; Honey Bees
ScientificName: Diptera; Syrphidae; Bombyliidae; Stratiomyidae; Apis mellifera
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Syrphidae; Bombyliidae; Stratiomyidae; Apis mellifera

File.Name: 5625b_5625-003.jpg
Notes: 10372224 This "Bee" hovering in the air is really a Fly (order Diptera, maybe a Soldier Fly (family Stratiomyidae). See my [Next] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2005)

File.Name: 5625c_5613-034.jpg
Notes: 10372122 Pretty little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly, resting on the weed Mouse-ear Chickweed (Cerastium glomeratum) in the garden. These butterflies are usually more interested in plants in the Pea Family (Fabaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2005)
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; Mouse-ear Chickweed; Pea Family

File.Name: 5625d_5623-064.jpg
Notes: 10372095 American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly in the canyon. We have American and West Coast and Painted Lady butterflies in the canyon, and I can never tell them apart until I study the photos with my books. All these pretty ladies have four or more white spots against the dark upper corner of their upper-forewing. This lady has an additional white spot in the orange cell behind, and the next orange cell above it does not have a dark bottom. It's easy to see this in a photo, but I sure can't catch it on the wing. I'm glad I can catch these butterflies with my camera to study instead of a net and killing jar. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2005)
CommonNames: American Lady; American; West Coast; Painted

File.Name: 5625e_5622-040.jpg
Notes: 10371949 Some kind of Fly (order Diptera) with handsome colors resting on a leaf in the garden. Getting these insect photos in the woods at home is like casting a net into the sea. I get some photos, but learn little about each critter and how it lives as egg, larva, pupa, and adult. The challenge is to discover the stories of these insects! (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2005)

File.Name: 5625f_5624-087.jpg
Notes: 10371795 Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus) - or some close kin in the Vespidae family. They have a nasty "Imperial Storm Trooper" look that makes me nervous, but they build nests under the roof of our porch and rarely bother us. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2005)

File.Name: 5626a_5626-011.jpg
Notes: 10379223 Native succulent Lanceleaf Liveforever aka Rock Lettuce (Dudleya lanceolata or kin) flowers on a rock outcrop in the canyon. The elegant flowers and stems look sculpted from wax. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2005)

File.Name: 5626b_5624-102.jpg
Notes: 10379179 This looks so exotic - weird mushrooms or under the sea - Nah, it's just looking into a Summer Squash (Cucurbitaceae) flower in the garden, with the petals acting as a natural "light tent" to bring up the glow. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2005)

File.Name: 5626c_5626-016.jpg
Notes: 10379115 Pretty little Damselfly (Zygoptera) perched in the woods near the creek. If the big dragonflies are "darners" - like knitting needles - then these delicate critters are just little pins. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2005)

File.Name: 5626d_5626-025.jpg
Notes: 10379049 Face to face with a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working composite flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the canyon. It looks pissed, but these butterflies are really wusses - they are weak fliers that I can usually get close to. Last summer we had dozens of these butterflies by now. This year I've only seen a few. All insects are scarce this year - strange after our record rains. I hope they are just late. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2005)

File.Name: 5626e_5626-014.jpg
Notes: 10378871 Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly in the canyon with wings open to show its colors, though this old-timer is already tattered and worn. Compare the [Previous] photo of the same butterfly with wings closed. They usually have wings open when I spot them. D'oh - I guess that's the point! (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2005)

File.Name: 5626f_5626-013.jpg
Notes: 10378795 Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly in camouflage mode with wings folded together. Compare the [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2005)

File.Name: 5627a_5627-033.jpg
Notes: 10387400 Pretty bee-like Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) depositing eggs in this furry flower head of Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata) in the garden. The eggs will hatch into slug-like larvae that consume aphids and other pests. These are good flies. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2005)

File.Name: 5627b_5627-032.jpg
Notes: 10387336 Incoming Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) approaching a flower head of Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata) in the garden. She is coming to lay eggs not to feed. See [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2005)

File.Name: 5627c_5627-038.jpg
Notes: 10387245 Little Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) resting on a Yarrow (Achillea) leaf in the garden. She has been depositing eggs on the leaves. It sure looks like the fly in the [Next] two photos, but I wouldn't bet they are the same. The color bands on her abdomen are more white than yellow, and the shaded cells in the wing look different. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2005)

File.Name: 5627d_5627-042.jpg
Notes: 10387111 Immature Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) in the garden. Baby is growing up. You can see the first little wing bud under the thorax in front of its hind leg. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2005)

File.Name: 5627e_5627-028.jpg
Notes: 10387045 This Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly is a tattered veteran who deserves a moment of rest. Maybe a bird took out that piece of its wing? You can see the crescent moon shapes along the hind wings that give it its name. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2005)

File.Name: 5627f_5627-037.jpg
Notes: 10387007 Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a flower of Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata) in the garden. Look at the other flower to see how it works. The bee hugs the lower petal and pushes its head against the goodies on the top petal to get to the nectar and transfer pollen. The bees working these flowers tend to have white foreheads - dusted with pollen from the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2005)

File.Name: 5628a_5628-057.jpg
Notes:
10396375 This is 3/3 of a series. This is a blow-up of a deep 180x180 pixel zoom into my original 3004x2000 very close (near 1:1) photo shown two clicks [Previous]. The tiny fly has a monkey on its back! I reckon the red critter is a spider-relative Mite (Acari). It don't know if the mite is hitching a ride or sucking its host dry. The setting is a flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca). (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2005)

I love these nature dramas I only notice after I take the photo and get it on my computer screen. Like the movies "Blow Up" and "The Conversation".

File.Name: 5628b_5628-057.jpg
Notes: 10396198 This is 2/3 of a series. When I got home, I zoomed into the [Previous] photo of a flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca). I wanted to check out the little black & red critter in the throat of the flower up front. I thought it was an aphid when I took the photo. Nope - it's a tiny fly, with luggage! (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2005)

File.Name: 5628c_5628-057.jpg
Notes: 10396158 This is 1/3 of a series. It's a close shot of flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca). It's an uncommon perennial mint-relative with a musty smell that's just starting to flower in our canyon. Notice that little black & red critter in the throat of the flower up front? Check the [Next] two photos! (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2005)

File.Name: 5628d_5628-045.jpg
Notes: 10395870 Remarkable iridescent colors on this tiny Fly (order Diptera) resting on a leaf of Summer Squash (Cucurbitaceae) in the garden. It has long antennae and "pictured wings" with distinct dark patterns. Maybe it's a True Fruit Fly (family Trypetidae)? (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2005)
CommonNames: Fly; Summer Squash; True Fruit Fly

File.Name: 5628e_5628-044.jpg
Notes: 10395843 This tiny iridescent green Fly (order Diptera) is resting on a leaf of Summer Squash (Cucurbitaceae) in the garden. It has long antennae and "pictured wings" with a distinct pattern. Maybe it's a True Fruit Fly (family Trypetidae)? (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2005)
CommonNames: Fly; Summer Squash; True Fruit Fly

File.Name: 5628f_5628-046.jpg
Notes: 10395769 Native Plume Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Pterophoridae) resting on a leaf in the garden. It has the shape of a cross. Moths (and butterflies) have no muscles in their wings. How does it fold and unfold its wings like this? (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2005)

File.Name: 5629a_5629-073.jpg
Notes: 10403928 Pretty little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly delicately working a flower of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum). I usually find these butterflies on lupines or lotus plants in the Pea Family (Fabaceae). (Santa Ynez River, 29 June 2005)
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; California Buckwheat; Pea Family

File.Name: 5629b_5629-083.jpg
Notes:
10403872 Classic native hummingbird flowers of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). These are the flowers the hummingbirds *should* be visiting at home - see [Previous] photo. (Santa Ynez River, 29 June 2005)

I got this photo down by the river today. The Penstemons are not doing so well at home, and neither are the Canyon Sunflowers (Venegasia carpesioides) - these are our most common late-season flowers. Insects are scarce this summer, and so are the usual summer flowers. Strange for this cycle to fail after so much rain this year.
Locality: Venegasia carpesioides) - these are our most common late-season flowers. Insects are scarce this summer

File.Name: 5629c_5628-062.jpg
Notes:
10403778 Female Hummingbird working a Dahlia flower in the garden. What a cool seal-like body shape with wings like helicopter blades! I reckon the hummers don't read the books that say they should visit long tubular red flowers. I guess this is a resident female Anna's Hummingbird, though we also have Black-Chinned and visiting Allen's and Rufous hummers in the garden right now. They fight like cats! (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2005)

After I got this photo, the hummingbird told me to fill the feeder. Really. We put out a sugar-water feeder last summer for a few weeks and then stopped because the hummers (and wasps) got so aggressive and nasty about it. But this lady hummer flew right up to my face - then to the empty feeder still hanging in the same place - then back to my face. I tried to bring my camera up and of course it was too late. Was that real??

File.Name: 5629d_5628-052.jpg
Notes: 10403274 The first flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family that I've seen in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2005)

File.Name: 5629e_5629-067.jpg
Notes: 10403229 One of the last flowers of native Farewell-to-Spring (Clarkia unguiculata) down by the river. The petals are reduced even more than usual, but the colors are intense. Spring wildflowers - even farewell-to-spring flowers - are hanging on late after our very wet winter/spring. (Santa Ynez River, 29 June 2005)

File.Name: 5629f_5629-066.jpg
Notes: 10403164 Flowers of Yellow Star Thistle (Centaurea solstitialis) look pretty, but it's a noxious weed that takes over whole acres of wildland in pure stands. Kill it when you can. Don't let it get established. (Santa Ynez River, 29 June 2005)

File.Name: 5630a_5630-090.jpg
Notes: 10411294 The first Crab Spider (Thomisidae) I've seen this summer - poised to attack on a native flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon. Usually these spiders color-shift to match the flower. I've never seen one look so pale. Maybe this spider is still learning how? (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2005)

File.Name: 5630b_5630-092.jpg
Notes: 10411226 Diurnal (aka daytime) Moth (lepidoptera) resting on a green leaf near the creek, trying to look like an oak leaf with handsome antennae. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2005)

File.Name: 5630c_5628-059.jpg
Notes: 10411176 Distinctive Buprestid Beetle (Buprestidae) on a flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon. They don't have the clumsy flight of most beetles. I always confuse them with bees or flies in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2005)

File.Name: 5630d_5626-019.jpg
Notes: 10411095 Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly on the ground in the canyon with wings open - here's looking at you! They have good camouflage if they keep their wings closed, but they usually rest on the ground with wings open. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2005)

File.Name: 5630e_5629-079.jpg
Notes: 10411052 Colonial web-spinning caterpillars on a flowering branch of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). I believe these are the larvae of beautiful Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona) butterflies. (Santa Ynez River, 29 June 2005)

File.Name: 5630f_5630-094.jpg
Notes: 10411027 We had so much rain this spring that we still have large patches of late spring wildflowers in the woods. This is native Farewell-to-Spring aka Speckled Clarkia (Clarkia cylindrica). The drooping buds and the folded sepals below the flowers are distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2005)

File.Name: 5702a_5701-098.jpg
Notes: 10424206 Tiny gray Damselfly (Zygoptera) perched in the garden, about as thick as a pencil lead. (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2005)

File.Name: 5702b_5702-001.jpg
Notes: 10424183 Pretty little Plant Bug (order Hemiptera, maybe family Miridae) resting in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2005)

File.Name: 5702c_5702-099.jpg
Notes: 10424166 Flower spikes of Red Brome (Bromus rubens) grass catching the sun just right. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2005)

File.Name: 5705a_5704-017.jpg
Notes:
10449539 Ripe stamens and stigma of a Lily (Lilium) flower in Grammy's garden. (Santa Monica Mountains, 4 July 2005)

My Dad is home from the hospital today after serious heart surgery. Vital signs are good. It did me good to get out with my camera and find life in Grammy and Pop Pops' garden. Rock on, Dad!

File.Name: 5705b_5704-020.jpg
Notes: 10449178 Ripe stamens and stigma of a Lily (Lilium sp.) flower in Grammy's garden. The stamens look like rich eclairs. (Santa Monica Mountains, 4 July 2005)

File.Name: 5705c_5704-013.jpg
Notes: 10449135 I believe these are the strange flowers of Pokeweed (Phytolacca americana). It's not native in California, but here it is on the edge of Grammy's garden. (Santa Monica Mountains, 4 July 2005)

File.Name: 5705d_5704-031.jpg
Notes: 10448968 The real flowers of a tropical Bromeliad (Bromeliaceae) in Grammy's garden. They look like hungry baby birds! These Bromes have overlapping leaves that form a water-tight cup at the base like a little pond. There are tropical frogs that only breed in the little pools of these plants. (Santa Monica Mountains, 4 July 2005)

File.Name: 5705e_5704-028.jpg
Notes: 10448858 I think these are flower heads of native Sticky Baccharis aka Salt-marsh Baccharis (Baccharis douglasii). It mostly grows near the creek, but I found this plant higher up in the chaparral. (Santa Monica Mountains, 4 July 2005)

File.Name: 5705f_5704-021.jpg
Notes: 10448808 Looking into a Matilija Poppy (Romneya sp.) flower in Grammy's garden. There are two native species in the Southern California backcountry, but I don't know what this garden variety is. (Santa Monica Mountains, 4 July 2005)

File.Name: 5706a_5705-040.jpg
Notes: 10457929 This Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly looks more like a moth - but look closer and see that it has swollen club-like tips on its antennae. Interesting color pattern on the wings - like pages in a water-damaged book. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2005)

File.Name: 5706b_5706-057.jpg
Notes: 10457436 It's hard to sneak up on these little tan-colored Grasshoppers (Acrididae) trying to hide in the dry grass and herbs. I don't think this one is quite mature. It hopped away instead of flying - only adults have wings. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2005)

File.Name: 5707a_5707-069.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 10937590 Mating pair of small Syrphid Flower Flies (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) in the garden. They make a perfect biplane! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2005)

File.Name: 5707b_5707-075.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 10937573 Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on a flower head of Purple Coneflower (Echinacea purpurea) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2005)

File.Name: 5707c_5706-066.jpg
Notes: 10465988 Mating pair of small Syrphid Flower Flies (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) in the garden. What can I say? Capturing a photo like this is the only way I know to distinguish male and female. Their faces really are different. They make a perfect biplane! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2005)

File.Name: 5707d_5706-065.jpg
Notes: 10465938 I think of a prancing pony with jewelled finery. I reckon this is a tiny immature Katydid or Cricket (order Orthoptera) on a golden California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2005)

File.Name: 5707e_5706-055.jpg
Notes: 10465802 Looking over the shoulder of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly working a composite flower head of Purple Coneflower (Echinacea purpurea) in the garden. Despite the common name, this is a native butterfly that lives on native plants in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family in its caterpillar stage. I guess the "Anise" in the name refers to that danged invasive weed Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) that's trying to take over our mountains. I count this butterfly as an ally in our ongoing effort to stop this weed. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2005)
CommonNames: Anise Swallowtail; Purple Coneflower; Parsley; Fennel
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Echinacea purpurea; Apiaceae; Foeniculum vulgare
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Echinacea purpurea; Apiaceae; Foeniculum vulgare

File.Name: 5707f_5706-044.jpg
Notes: 10465668 This native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly has been a busy pollinator. Here it is on a flower head of the composite Purple Coneflower (Echinacea purpurea) in the garden. Look close to see yellow pollen on the end of its proboscis. It also has orange pollen deposits on the ribs of its hind wings which I'm sure are from native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) flowers in the canyon. (See my photo at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=7999731.) I wish I knew were its been foraging. ALL the Humboldt Tiger Lilies I know of have been parasitized this year and have no flowers. I guess I have to explore further down the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2005)
CommonNames: Anise Swallowtail; Purple Coneflower; Humboldt Lily
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Echinacea purpurea; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Echinacea purpurea; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum

File.Name: 5707g_5706-058.jpg
Notes: 10465634 Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly perched in an Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) tree by the creek. Feisty little critter! It left its perch to chase away all other butterflies, including a look-alike California Sister (Limenitis bredowii). It never rested less than 8 feet above ground. I got as close as I could, and held my camera out at arm's length and tried to line up the viewfinder for autofocus, and then cropped in tight. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2005)
CommonNames: Lorquin's Admiral; Arroyo Willow; California Sister
ScientificName: Basilarchia lorquini; Salix lasiolepis; Limenitis bredowii
associatedTaxa: Basilarchia lorquini; Salix lasiolepis; Limenitis bredowii

File.Name: 5707h_5707-067.jpg
Notes: 10463525 "Them there eyes..." This furry critter with amazing blue eyes is the size and shape of a bee, but I'm pretty sure it's a Deer Fly (order Diptera, family Tabanidae) visiting new flowers of Toyon aka Christmas Berry (Heteromeles arbutifolia) in the canyon. These pretty flies have a nasty bite. They need a blood meal for protein to make eggs, but they visit flowers to get energy nectar for themselves. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2005)

File.Name: 5708a_5706-087.jpg
Notes: 10473421 Big Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis or kin) working a garden flower of Clary Sage (Salvia sclarea). This photo is not quite sharp, but it tells a good story. This bee landed on the lower white petal that acts like a fulcrum to bring the stamens and style down onto its back, and it is carrying a load of white pollen from other flowers. You know - pollination happens. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2005)

File.Name: 5708b_5706-047.jpg
Notes: 10473366 Magenta flower of native Checker Bloom (Sidalcea malviflora) with a perfect orange accent of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus. I think of these as spring flowers, but they are both still going strong in the canyon after all the rain this year. It's odd that our usual summer flowers are not doing so well. Summer flowers and insects are confused this year! (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2005)

File.Name: 5708c_5706-053.jpg
Notes: 10473329 Pretty leaf galls on native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the canyon. The bright red abnormal growth is caused by colonies of tiny aphids (maybe Tamalia coweni?) that live on secretions within the folded leaf margins. If you unfold a leaf, you can see the tiny aphids inside. What a life. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2005)

File.Name: 5708d_5706-051.jpg
Notes: 10473290 New flowers of the native shrub Chaparral Pea (Pickeringia montana) in the canyon. The pretty flowers sure look fertile, but they mostly spread by runners and rarely make seeds. They have gnarly spines, and it's painful to bushwack past them. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2005)

File.Name: 5708e_5707-077.jpg
Notes: 10473230 First flowers of our native summer California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2005)

File.Name: 5708f_5704-010.jpg
Notes: 10473210 Looking into a wild hive of Honey Bees (Apis mellifera) in a crack in a Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree. The sun just broke through on a foggy morning, so the bees are not quite awake yet. (Santa Monica Mountains, 4 July 2005)

File.Name: 5713a_5710-020.jpg
Notes:
10510004 I believe this little butterfly is a Marine Blue (Leptotes marina). The tawny zebra-striping and twin eye spots on the hind wings are distinctive. It's resting on a flower of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Pea Family (Fabaceae). (Santa Monica Mountains, 10 July 2005).

I've been visiting my parents - my father is recovering from serious heart surgery. Today's photos are all from the nearby trails at Will Rogers State Historic Park near the city of Pacific Palisades.

File.Name: 5713b_5710-017.jpg
Notes: 10509929 Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly perched on a Eucalyptus leaf in the chaparral. This butterfly landed on my head! They like to perch and chase away other butterflies (and people), but they don't like to pose. I don't see them at home in the mountains. (Santa Monica Mountains, 10 July 2005)

File.Name: 5713c_5710-015.jpg
Notes: 10509869 Immature Katydid (Tettigoniidae) grasshopper resting on a flower of the native composite Cliff Desertdandelion (Malacothrix saxatilis var. tenuifolia. (Santa Monica Mountains, 10 July 2005)

File.Name: 5713d_5709-005.jpg
Notes: 10509814 Remarkable summer flower of Plummer's Mariposa Lily Calochortus plummerae. I found this native lily growing in the dry chaparral along a dusty trail. Expect the unexpected! (Santa Monica Mountains, 9 July 2005)

File.Name: 5713e_5709-007.jpg
Notes: 10509755 Pretty flowers of the native shrub Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus or kin). (Santa Monica Mountains, 9 July 2005)

File.Name: 5713f_5710-023.jpg
Notes: 10509732 Pretty flowers of Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala). This is an unusual composite (Asteraceae) plant with heads of two-lipped mint-like flowers that are unlike any other composite I know. (Santa Monica Mountains, 10 July 2005)

File.Name: 5714a_5714-061.jpg
Notes: 10519445 This is an Aphid (family Aphididae) Horror Movie! The drama is taking place on a single flower stalk/ovary of a night-blooming yellow Evening Primrose (Oenothera sp.) flower in our garden. That slug is about 1/4 inch long - much bigger than the aphids. Look close and see that it's sucking up a live aphid on the left. Follow the curve of the slug's body and you can see the rest of the aphid and more unfortunate aphids entombed within. The slug has no legs, so I reckon it's a fly larva aka maggot (order Diptera). Spooky - but think what it would be like without these natural preditors and parasites to patrol the woods. (If you use pesticides in your garden, maybe you already know!) (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2005)

File.Name: 5714b_5713-043.jpg
Notes: 10519146 This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) is moving counterclockwise following the new pollen-bearing stamens around a flower head of composite Purple Coneflower (Echinacea purpurea) in the garden. You can see the magenta female stigmas (with some dusted pollen) down lower on the flower. The left side of the bee is covered with pollen. That's funny because the outside right-side of the bee has no pollen at all! I'll start watching to see if they always go in the same direction. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2005)

File.Name: 5714c_5713-054.jpg
Notes: 10519059 Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a composite flower head of Purple Coneflower (Echinacea purpurea) in the garden. You can see that its proboscis is formed of two separate channels zipped together to make a tube. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2005)

File.Name: 5714d_5714-098.jpg
Notes:
10518992 Female California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly working a Dahlia flower in the garden. This is the official California State Butterfly because of the remarkable iridescent colors on the upper wings of the males. I saw both males and females for the first time today. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2005)

I have a photo of the male California Dogface at
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=8477823

File.Name: 5714e_5714-084.jpg
Notes: 10518882 Remarkable Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) hovering at a flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the canyon. It's an unusual kind of Sphinx Moth (order lepidoptera, family Sphingidae) related to "tomato worms". This uncommon native moth works the day shift. It really does have clear wings without scales, it's hirsute and colored like a bumblebee, and it hovers like a hummingbird! This is one of my favorite critters. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2005)
CommonNames: Snowberry Clearwing Moth; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Sphinx Moth
ScientificName: Hemaris diffinis; Monardella hypoleuca; lepidoptera; Sphingidae
associatedTaxa: Hemaris diffinis; Monardella hypoleuca; lepidoptera; Sphingidae

File.Name: 5714f_5714-072.jpg
Notes: 10518804 A little "inchworm" or "looper" moth caterpillar (order lepidoptera, maybe family Geometridae) navigating a landing on a flower head of Yarrow (Achillea) in the garden. If you know the plant, you can appreciate how small this critter really is. I wouldn't recognize the adult moth. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2005)

File.Name: 5715a_5714-078.jpg
Notes: 10527273 Immature Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) resting on a Zinnia flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2005)

File.Name: 5715b_5713-040.jpg
Notes: 10527246 Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly visiting new flowers of well-named Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. This butterfly is also a kind of "weed" - it's introduced from Europe and probably the most ubiquitous butterfly in North America. The caterpillars feed exclusively on plants in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family, but I find the adults visiting many different flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2005)

File.Name: 5715c_5710-011.jpg
Notes: 10527185 Ripe stamen and stigma of a Lily (Lilium) flower in Grammy's garden. (Santa Monica Mountains, 10 July 2005)

File.Name: 5715d_5713-045.jpg
Notes: 10527173 Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a composite flower head of Purple Coneflower (Echinacea purpurea) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2005)

File.Name: 5715e_5714-092.jpg
Notes: 10527159 Here's why we don't see many nesting birds - they are too well hidden! (Look for the head in the upper-right corner and work backwards.) I think this is a Lesser Goldfinch (Carduelis psaltria) on its nest in native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) by the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2005)

File.Name: 5715f_5714-068.jpg
Notes: 10527125 There's a lot going on in this close photo of Aphids (family Aphididae) on a single flower stalk/ovary of a night-blooming yellow Evening Primrose (Oenothera sp.) in the garden. The large tan-colored aphid on the left is shedding its skin. There is a slug-like fly larva aka maggot (order Diptera) that has been eating aphids - see [Previous photo] - but now it's withdrawn because of the tiny parasitic Wasps (order Hymenoptera). Are the wasps looking to lay eggs on the aphids? Or the maggot? Or maybe on the aphids to be eaten by the maggot?? It's a jungle out there! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2005)
CommonNames: Aphids; Evening Primrose; fly larva; Wasps

File.Name: 5716a_5716-037.jpg
Notes: 10533580 Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly working a Zinnia flower in the garden. Note how the front edge of the wing is black - very different from the Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly a few photos [Previous]. (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2005)

File.Name: 5716b_5716-024.jpg
Notes: 10533537 This is one of those photos you hope for (as a naturalist), but don't really expect to get. That's an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly working a Zinnia flower in the garden. Up front you can see an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) caterpillar on a Dill (Anethum graveolens) herb nearby. Don't expect adult butterflies to feed near their babies - but it's pretty cool when they do! (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2005)
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Zinnia; Papilio zelicaon; Anethum graveolens
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Zinnia; Papilio zelicaon; Anethum graveolens

File.Name: 5716c_5716-028.jpg
Notes: 10533503 Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) caterpillar on a Dill (Anethum graveolens) herb in the garden. Which end is the engine and which is the caboose? Heh - the head is on the right and the tail is left. Both "faces" are just painted on as an adaptation to distract preditors. With so many active parasitic wasps and flies on the wing, I'm surprised any of these big caterpillars survive to become adult butterflies. (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2005)

File.Name: 5716d_5716-034.jpg
Notes: 10533393 Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed flowers in the garden. Look how the black bands across the front wing are all perpendicular to the edge, and compare how the Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterflies in the next few photos are different. These color differences look so obvious with digital photos and books, but it's hard to get it right on the wing. (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2005)
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Butterfly Weed; Anise Swallowtail
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Asclepias tuberosa; Papilio zelicaon
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Asclepias tuberosa; Papilio zelicaon

File.Name: 5716e_5716-012.jpg
Notes: 10533253 I reckon this little butterfly is a Marine Blue (Leptotes marina). The tawny zebra-stripes and twin eye spots on the hind wings are distinctive. The caterpillars feed on Legumes in the Fabaceae family. This adult was mostly interested in the Lavender (Lavandula officinalis or kin) flowers in the Mint Family (Labiatae) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2005)
CommonNames: Marine Blue; Legumes; Lavender; Mint Family
ScientificName: Leptotes marina; Fabaceae; Lavandula officinalis; Labiatae
associatedTaxa: Leptotes marina; Fabaceae; Lavandula officinalis; Labiatae

File.Name: 5716f_5716-021.jpg
Notes: 10533198 California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly on the ground in the garden. They patrol their "flyways" through the oak woodland, but they rarely land except to "puddle" on damp soil like this. Do they ever feed? (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2005)

File.Name: 5717a_5717-061.jpg
Notes:
10540993 Spider love - Sheet Web Spiders (Linyphiidae) in the act of love, so many legs entwined, transfering genetic material from the male's palps to female's epigyne. It's a tender moment.. and complicated! (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2005)

[Spoiler: Explicit Sexual Details Follow!]

Mating is difficult for these preditors who are ready to attack anything that moves... I guess it's always difficult. The smaller male spider is low on the left, and the larger female is higher behind. Both are hanging upside down. The male deposits tiny drops of sperm on a special "sperm web" and then inflates his spermaphores on the end of his leg-like palps to pick it up. You can see the dark inflated spermaphores on the end of the leg-like palps over his head in this photo. Above the male's palps, you can see the tiny crescent-shaped epigyne opening of the female that is ready to accept the sperm package. And then stuff happens... This is mysterious!
ScientificName: Linyphiidae; palps; epigyne; spermaphores; palps; epigyne
associatedTaxa: Linyphiidae; palps; epigyne; spermaphores; palps; epigyne

File.Name: 5717b_5716-033.jpg
Notes: Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) caterpillar munching a flower stem of Dill (Anethum graveolens) herb in the garden. That clown face is just painted on to confuse predators! Despite the name and host, this is a native butterfly that favors plants in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2005)

File.Name: 5717c_5717-057.jpg
Notes: 10540784 This morning I saw three different species of Swallowtail butterflies in the garden at the same time. This is the Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed flowers. I also saw Anise Swallowtails (Papilio zelicaon) and Pale Swallowtails (Papilio eurymedon). Check my [Previous] photos for the last few days to see the others. The garden is like a water hole in the woods for much native life. (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2005)
CommonNames: Swallowtail; Western Tiger Swallowtail; Butterfly Weed; Anise Swallowtails; Pale Swallowtails
ScientificName: Papilio rutulus; Asclepias tuberosa; Papilio zelicaon; Papilio eurymedon
associatedTaxa: Papilio rutulus; Asclepias tuberosa; Papilio zelicaon; Papilio eurymedon

File.Name: 5717d_5716-009.jpg
Notes: 10540475 I got the blues... at least I'm getting confused by the local Blues - the (family Polyommatinae) butterflies in our canyon. Maybe this a Square-spotted Blue (Euphilotes battoides) butterfly working native flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum). None of my books mention orange antennae. (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2005)

File.Name: 5717e_5717-053.jpg
Notes: 10540376 Aphid (family Aphididae) Horror Movie - part 3! This tiny "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) on a flower stalk/ovary of a night-blooming yellow Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri or kin) flower in the garden sucked that little aphid on the right dry, leaving another husk among many. The little aphid on the left reminds me of a Reindeer in the Christmas tableau my Mom always puts up - Yipes, that's a wolf not Santa! (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2005)

File.Name: 5717f_5716-001.jpg
Notes: 10540264 Aphid (family Aphididae) Horror Movie - part 2! I noticed this black spot among the aphids on a single flower stalk/ovary of a night-blooming yellow Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri or kin) flower in the garden. It doesn't look alive - but look closer. I can almost make out eyes and legs, and it seems to be slowly dragging its bloody antenna across the stem on the bottom! (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2005)

File.Name: 5718a_5718-074.jpg
Notes: 10548213 Mating pair of Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterflies on a flower head of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the canyon. I think. I'm getting really confused by these "Blue" butterflies. I guess these are Acmon Blues because of the iridescent blue spots on the hind wing spots. Catching a pair like this "in the act" is the only way I know to tell male from female - and I still don't know which is which! I crept closer, and they finally flew off to the right, still joined together. So which is which? (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2005)

File.Name: 5718b_5718-082.jpg
Notes: 10548135 This photo is not quite sharp - and maybe that's perfect! Did you spot the Ambush Bug (order Hemiptera, family Phymatidae) looming motionless above this flower cluster of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in our canyon? Maybe this is the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica. This preditor has perfect brown and white colors to match the buckwheat flowers as they mature. It's curious that all our summer flowers are late this year after the very rainy spring. These buckwheat flowers have not yet started to turned brown. I spotted this critter because its brown colors were not right! (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2005)
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; California Buckwheat; Pacific Ambush Bug
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Phymatidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Phymata pacifica
associatedTaxa: Hemiptera; Phymatidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Phymata pacifica

File.Name: 5718c_5718-065.jpg
Notes: 10548022 This Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly looks and flies like a moth - but look closer and see that it has swollen club-like tips on its antennae, the mark of a butterfly. They don't seem interested in flowers. I always lose them when they land on dead oak leaves on the ground. These plain butterflies have perfect camouflage. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2005)

File.Name: 5718d_5718-091.jpg
Notes: 10547888 This pretty yellow butterfly in the garden is NOT a female California Dogface (Zerene eurydice). The pretty dogface ladies have a pointed apex on their front wings, but this butterfly has rounded wings. You can also see the dark shadow of the upper wings, and this is not a dogface shape. I guess this is an Orange Sulphur (Colias eurytheme). Maybe the garden flower is Gaillardia? (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2005)

File.Name: 5718e_5718-068.jpg
Notes: 10547802 I reckon this is a tattered American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly in the canyon. I'm looking up from below in this photo, but I can still make out two big eyespots on the hind wings, and that's distinctive. I see these pretty ladies every day, but they rarely land and never(?) feed at flowers. They flutter around many plants, but they seem most interested in composite Everlasting (Gnaphalium sp.) flowers. Are they just "running out the clock"? (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2005)

File.Name: 5718f_5717-064.jpg
Notes:
10547731 More spider love - These are Sheet Web Spiders (Linyphiidae) caught in the act of love. See my [Previous] photo for more info. Courtship is over - this photo shows the moment of consummation. The male spider (front-left) has deposited sperm on his "sperm web" and picked it up with his swollen dark spermaphores on the end of the leg-like palps on its head. He's laying on his back, and so is the female behind him. He has managed to engage the embolus tip of his spermaphore into the female's epigyne opening. The female has responded by inflating her crescent-shaped structure you can see in this photo to guide the genetic material into one of her spermathecae sacs to hold it until needed to fertilize her eggs. The structure of spider spermaphores and epigynes is extremely complex. It's one of those lock and key things. It's hard enough to get two spiders this close without attacking each other - they also have to fit! (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2005)

Hah - what would those spiders on the wall write about us!

File.Name: 5719a_5719-113.jpg
Notes:
10557094 Pretty American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working a Lavender (Lavandula officinalis or kin) flower in the garden. The large lavender "petals" on top are just leafy bracts. The butterfly has found the small real flowers down below. The beautiful underwings are distinctive among the butterfly "Ladies" - two eyespots on the hind wing (rather than four), white cobweb pattern on hind wings, pink patch on the front wing, and that white spot in the orange cell top right. I know all these features, but I still need my books and a close photo like this to see them. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2005)

Julie and I spent the afternoon paddling our kayaks in the ocean. I'm tired. I'm glad there are still a few places I can't take my camera!
ScientificName: Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working a Lavender (Lavandula officinalis

File.Name: 5719b_5719-112.jpg
Notes: 10556961 I've been chasing these pretty American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterflys all week hoping one would land to feed and pose. This one finally settled on a flower it liked - Lavender (Lavandula officinalis or kin) in the garden. But it was tricked. The lavender "petals" on top are just pretty leaves (bracts) and the real flowers are small and down below. This butterfly figured it out. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2005)

File.Name: 5719c_5716-022.jpg
Notes:
10556898 This California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly is "puddling" on damp ground in the garden, taking up water with its orange proboscis. These butterflies are easy to recognize when you can see the upper side of the wings:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10533198
But the undersides of their wings are so beautiful and mysterious. And you gotta love those eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2005)

File.Name: 5719d_5719-101.jpg
Notes: 10556813 Tiny Damselfly (Zygoptera) perched on a California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the canyon. It's about as thick as a pencil lead. What prey does it hunt? (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2005)

File.Name: 5719e_5718-094.jpg
Notes: 10556793 Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) caterpillar munching a flower stem of the invasive weed Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the canyon. Another caterpillar is devouring the Dill in our garden. Go for it little bro! Despite the common name, this critter is not an introduced biological control. It will become a beautiful native swallowtail butterfly that has lived here before the introduced fennel (aka anise). I guess the caterpillars originally fed on other natives in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family like Sanicula and Lomatium and Tauschia. But there is so much fennel invading the mountains - I hope to see THOUSANDS of these beautiful caterpillars munching away! (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2005)
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Sanicula; Lomatium; Tauschia
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Sanicula; Lomatium; Tauschia

File.Name: 5719f_5719-099.jpg
Notes:
10556701 Aphid (family Aphididae) Horror Movie - part 4! This is a different slug-like preditor working the aphids on a flower stalk/ovary of a night-blooming yellow Evening Primrose (Oenothera sp.) flower in our garden. I guess it's another fly larva aka maggot (order Diptera), but the only way to know for sure is to rear it in a cage until it pupates and emerges as an adult. No thanks. This larva has just about sucked its victim dry and is about to spit out the husk. (A different slug-like larva I found swallows them whole.) (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2005)

It's remarkable how many different preditors there has been on this one plant infested with aphids. I'll keep checking every morning. Of course poison kills everything - no fun, no life, no future.

File.Name: 5720a_5720-128.jpg
Notes:
10564896 Oh my... four hatchling Lesser Goldfinch (Carduelis psaltria) birds in the nest. They look so embryonic. Maybe they just hatched this morning? (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2005)

I have a photo of Mama Goldfinch at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10527159

I know I shouldn't get too close to nesting birds, but I couldn't resist. I walked past the nest on my hike and Mom was there. I came back an hour later and Mom was gone, so I took a quick peek and photo. I backed off and waited. Mom returned in a few minutes.

File.Name: 5720b_5720-143.jpg
Notes:
10564804 Our Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) caterpillar that has been munching the Dill (Anethum graveolens) herb in the garden has moved on. It moved to a nearby chili plant to make its cocoon and (hopefully) pupate to become a beautiful adult butterfly. Now it's motionless. Look close and you can see it has made a silk sling below the 5th segment so it is hanging from the stalk as it get ready to metamorphose. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2005)

Yesterday this critter was munching leaves. Today it's hanging from a sling like a rock climber, facing an uncertain future over an abyss of preditors. Here's one of my favorite quotes, from Anais Nin:
  "It takes courage to push yourself to places that you have never been before... to test your limits... to break through barriers. And the day came when the risk it took to remain tight inside the bud was more painful than the risk it took to blossom.

File.Name: 5720c_5720-126.jpg
Notes: 10564648 Already tattered California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly tangled up and blue in a spider web. Yeah... much as I love spiders, I did set it free - to be eaten by some other critter! (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2005)

File.Name: 5720d_5720-122.jpg
Notes: 10564600 I guess this is a little Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae - probably genus Hesperia) butterfly perched on a leaf in the woods. It's sitting in classic "jet-plane" posture, with hindwings wider open than forewings. There are many species that look alike, so it's easy to "skip her" - at least I don't worry about the species name. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2005)

File.Name: 5720e_5720-115.jpg

Pretty tattoo pattern on her butt! Her eggs will hatch into larvae that love to eat aphids. I reckon this fly is much too small to be the parent of the slug-like larva in the [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2005)

File.Name: 5720f_5720-117.jpg
Notes: 10564489 Every morning before the breeze kicks up I go out to the garden with my camera and check this particular night-blooming yellow Evening Primrose (Oenothera sp. - maybe Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri) plant in our garden. I've been watching these little slug-like preditor larvae eat aphids for a couple of weeks. I guess this is a Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) larvae. This one is starting to show some internal structure. Is it larva or pupa? (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2005)

File.Name: 5721a_5721-176.jpg
Notes:
10573192 I almost failed this naturalist/camouflage test. Sure I noticed the little Ambush Bug (order Hemiptera, family Phymatidae) perfectly hiding in the flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum). I moved my "ground-level" tripod as close as possible and got a few photos. I didn't get the rest of the story until I returned home to my computer - and I still almost missed it!

Now look very close at this photo. There are TWO Ambush Bugs mating, male and female, upper and lower. It looks nasty. All of today's photos are shots of pretty gnarly preditors.

File.Name: 5721c_5721-152.jpg
Notes:
10573042 Awesome Tarantula Hawk (order Hymenoptera, genus Pepsis) wasp getting a nectar energy boost from native flowers of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. This is our largest wasp, one of the biggest in the world. They dine on California Tarantula (Aphonopelma reversum) spiders for breakfast - at least their larvae do. (Santa Ynez River, 21 July 2005)

It was windy at the river this morning and hard to get photos. This big wasp only had one orange antenna. I'll get back for more photos as soon as I can.

File.Name: 5721d_5721-153.jpg
Notes:
10572910 This Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens) has captured an unfortunate butterfly - maybe an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon)? - on a native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub along the river. The butterflies' posture and unrolled proboscis say it all - blah - even better than little X's through the eyes. It's interesting to think that EVERY critter in nature - except maybe us? (do bacteria and viruses etc. count?) - will finally die in the grasp of a preditor. (Santa Ynez River, 21 July 2005)

I've been looking close at spiders lately, and I notice that this one surely has a swollen dark spermaphore on it's lowest leg - that must really be a palp. I'm sure this spider has been mating. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10540993

File.Name: 5721e_5721-155.jpg
Notes: 10572761 This Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens) has captured a bee. I'm sure it's not a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) - maybe it's a Leafcutter Bee (family Megachilidae)? The plant is native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) which is just starting to flower on the dry flats along the river. (Santa Ynez River, 21 July 2005)

File.Name: 5721f_5721-192.jpg
Notes: 10572675 "How about those chompers?" I guess this is a larva of Brown Lacewing (order Neuroptera family Hemerobiidae) on a flower head of Yarrow (Achillea) in the garden. Said to be predaceous, but look how it's dusted with pollen, and I have another photo of it sticking those chompers into a flower. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2005)

File.Name: 5722a_5722-215.jpg
Notes: 10580636 Mating couple of male and female Cabbage White (Pieris rapae or maybe P. napi?) butterflies. They don't look very white in this photo, but they were trying to get away from me and my camera and hid among the big green leaves of a native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) shrub - like a natural green light tent. I did notice the butterfly on the left was white, and its partner had a definate yellowish cast. The butterfly on the left did the flying for both of then when I disturbed them. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2005)
ScientificName: Pieris rapae; P. napi; Venegasia carpesioides
associatedTaxa: Pieris rapae; P. napi; Venegasia carpesioides

File.Name: 5722b_5722-217.jpg
Notes:
10580545 Our native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) caterpillar has become a chrysalis. It crawled off the Dill (Anethum graveolens) plant in the garden and moved to a nearby chili plant where it spun a silk girdle to hang from the stem like a rock climber trying to sleep half way up El Capitan in Yosemite. Maybe the perils are similar. This chrysalis/pupa is so vulnerable to birds and lizards and parasitic wasps and flies - like a perfectly packaged snack! Did it change its old skin or shed to a new one? Looking at this photos, I can almost make out face and features - is it facing outward with head on top? I'll try to get a closer shot tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2005)

Maybe you saw my other photos of this same caterpillar in the garden:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10564804
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10556793
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10540784
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10533503

Notes:
10580445 Wild orchids! It's been so hot and muggy for the past week - near or past 100 degrees every day - but we still have native Green Rein Orchids (Piperia elongata

- or maybe P. michaelii?) flowering in the summer-dry woods. The plants look more like asparagus stalks than the orchids you get from a florist, but they are real native orchids. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2005)

File.Name: 5722d_5721-198.jpg
Notes: 10580366 Pretty Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working the Yarrow (Achillea) flowers in the garden. I also saw these butterflies near the river today. Note the distinctive "tails" on the hindwings. It likes to "saw" them back and forth when it perches with wings together. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2005)

File.Name: 5722e_5721-157.jpg
Notes: 10580292 I've been going through my books, and I believe this is a female Northern White Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working native flowers of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the dry banks of the river. Look close and you can see distinctive inward pointing "chevon" shapes (like "^") along the wing margins. It's said to breed on shrubby plants in the Mallow (Malvaceae) family, and Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) grows nearby. (Santa Ynez River, 21 July 2005)
CommonNames: Northern White Skipper; Scale Broom; Mallow; Chaparral Mallow
ScientificName: Heliopetes ericetorum; Lepidospartum squamatum; Malvaceae; Malacothamnus fasciculatus

File.Name: 5722f_5721-186.jpg
Notes: 10580237 Like ships passing in the night... I was trying (and failed) to get a sharp photo of this little bee on the Yarrow (Achillea) flowers in the garden. I didn't notice the Crab Spider (Thomisidae) underneath until I got back to my computer. That little bee is dusted with pollen on its legs and mostly under its abdomen. I guess it's a Leafcutter Bee or kin (family Megachilidae). (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2005)

File.Name: 5723a_5723-009.jpg
Notes:
10587100 Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae - probably genus Hesperia) butterfly working Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed flowers in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2005)

Look how this butterfly is directing its very long proboscis into the flower - under the right leg, left leg holding it down, and how the heck does it get that sharp bend at the bottom to enter the flower? This is a real stumper since butterflies - like all insects and spiders and arthropods - have an exoskeleton, like a suit of armor, with organs inside. So how can these critters move at all when their muscles only attach on the inside of their external frame, with all pull and no push?

File.Name: 5723b_5723-003.jpg
Notes: 10587031 Tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) working Yarrow (Achillea) flowers in the garden. It seems to be gathering pollen rather than nectar. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2005)

File.Name: 5723c_5721-194.jpg
Notes: 10587011 Pretty little fly on the Yarrow (Achillea) flowers in the garden. Several of these golden flies are here every day, and they don't like to pose. Maybe this is a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but it's not very bristly. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2005)

File.Name: 5723d_5723-013.jpg
Notes: 10586971 Pretty Woodland Cicada (family homoptera, family Cicadidae - maybe genus Platypedia?) in the woods. Our local cicadas don't sing. They just "click click click" by rattling their wings together, kind of like a samba Guiro instrument. They are really hard to find, because they stop clicking whenever you get close. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2005)

File.Name: 5723e_5723-021.jpg
Notes:
10586867 This critter hovers and buzzes like a bee-fly or flower-fly, but it's a real Bee (Apoidea) with chewing mouthparts. It has pollen-collecting hairs under its abdomen rather than its hind legs. I guess it is a Mason or Resin Bee in the family Megachilidae that includes the Leafcutter Bees. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2005)

This bee is working the native flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the canyon. The bee is very territorial. I found a similar bee on flowers of Rock Phacelia (Phacelia imbricata) in the garden a month ago. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10228278

I think it's so interesting how similar these flowers are even though one flower is in the Mint Family (Labiatae) and the other is in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family. Both flowers have long exerted stamens ready to deposit pollen under the abdomen of this particular kind of bee. This is nature that works, not design.
Locality: Hydrophyllaceae) family. Both flowers have long exerted stamens ready to deposit pollen under the abdomen of this particular kind of bee. This is nature that works

File.Name: 5723f_5723-014.jpg
Notes:
10586710 Looking close at the Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) chrysalis in our garden. It's three days old now. That sure looks like a face to me - but maybe Godzilla more than a butterfly! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2005)

Look back two photos to http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=10580545.

File.Name: 5724a_5724-065.jpg
Notes:
10599070 Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) with proboscis unfurled approaching a flower head of Cleveland Sage (Salvia clevelandii) flowers in the garden. It's an odd native moth with clear wings that works the day shift, hovers like a hummer, and looks like a huge bee. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2005)

I took at least 50 photos to get this one keeper. You gotta love digital!

File.Name: 5724b_5724-051.jpg
Notes: 10594548 Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) among the flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum). It looks sort of kindly and paternal - with a hint of menace, like a parent or thesis advisor or politician! We all know insects have six legs - but what about those mouth parts!? (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2005)

File.Name: 5724c_5724-053.jpg
Notes: 10594482 Always beautiful American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working Cleveland Sage (Salvia clevelandii) flowers in the garden. The shrub is a California native, but not native here. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2005)

File.Name: 5724d_5724-040.jpg
Notes:
10594334 Milkweed flowers like this Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) in the garden can be a trap for pollinators like this Honey Bee (Apis mellifera). Bees get their legs stuck as they pick up the pollinia or pollen sacks hidden between the cup-like "petals" (really part of the corona). Bees can die in the trap, or lose a limb escaping. I watched this particular bee get stuck several times - she knows the secret to just crawl over the other flowers until she pulls loose. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2005)

See my photo with explanation at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8108247

File.Name: 5724e_5724-035.jpg
Notes:
10594254 This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) has learned (again) that milkweed flowers like this Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) in the garden are a trap. Both left and right middle legs of this bee have slipped between the cup-like hoods into the stigmatic slits of the flower and picked up pollinia or pollen sacks. You can see the dark branching pollinia hanging on the bee's legs. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2005)

See my photo with explanation at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8108247
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Asclepias tuberosa; hoods; stigmatic slits; pollinia
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Asclepias tuberosa; hoods; stigmatic slits; pollinia

File.Name: 5724f_5724-048.jpg
Notes:
10594040 "Never eat anything bigger than your head."

Sure enough, butterfly coccoons are vulnerable. This tiny parasitic wasp is checking out the Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly chrysalis in our garden. The tiny wasp has been walking around on this critter for two days. I don't think it managed to lay any eggs through the thick skin, but it's sure been trying. I saw the chrysalis kick a few times today - made me jump! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2005)

More photos and links of this caterpillar/chrysalis at
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=10580545

File.Name: 5726a_5726-073.jpg
Notes: 10611005 Pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) working a Marigold (Tagetes sp.) flower in the garden. The orange petals are like a natural light tent. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2005)

File.Name: 5726b_5726-074.jpg
Notes: 10610983 Pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) slurping the pollen from its foot, while resting on a petal of a Marigold (Tagetes sp.) flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2005)

File.Name: 5726c_5726-084.jpg
Notes: 10610946 This must be an immature "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) on a leaf in the garden. You can see its straw-like proboscis sucking the plant stem. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2005)

File.Name: 5726d_5726-077.jpg
Notes: 10610892 A clutch of tiny "True Bugs" (order Hemiptera) on a leaf in the garden. My first impression is "lots of eyes!" They've been together for two days now. I'm sure these critters will soon go their own ways. I wonder if they will retain a vague memory of being together? (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2005)

File.Name: 5726e_5726-083.jpg
Notes: 10610836 A Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae - probably genus Hesperia) butterfly eye to eye with a Buprestid Beetle (Buprestidae) on a flower of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the canyon. I wonder what they think of each other. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2005)
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; Buprestid Beetle; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: 5726f_5724-067.jpg
Notes: 10610805 This is the same native Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) approaching a flower of Cleveland Sage (Salvia clevelandii) with proboscis unfurled that I show in the [Previous] photo. I almost deleted this one, but being sharp isn't everything! (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2005)

File.Name: 5727a_5727-113.jpg
Notes: 10618932 A pair of Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflys on a flower of the native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrubs along the river. They are either fighting or feeling frisky... Hah - no doubt this encounter is about love! I won't try to guess which is male or female. The butterfly "on top" is physically bigger and it's feeding on the flower. It never stopped sucking on that flower no matter how frisky the other smaller butterfly "on the bottom" got, even through a dozen photos. The little one kept fluttering around and around to attract the bigger one, but I saw no interest or consummation in this relationship. (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2005)

File.Name: 5727b_5727-150.jpg
Notes:
10618781 Awesome Tarantula Hawk (order Hymenoptera, genus Pepsis) wasp working native flowers of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. This is our largest wasp - dragonfly size - one of the biggest in the world. They "sting" and paralyze California Tarantula (Aphonopelma reversum) spiders and drag them to a burrow for the wasp larvae to consume. Their growing kids need a protein meal, but the adults get their nectar energy boost from these flowers. (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2005)

These huge wasps are said to have a fearsome sting. I wouldn't get close - except to get a photo!
CommonNames: Tarantula Hawk; Scale Broom; California Tarantula
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Pepsis; Lepidospartum squamatum; Aphonopelma reversum
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Pepsis; Lepidospartum squamatum; Aphonopelma reversum

File.Name: 5727c_5727-117.jpg
Notes: 10618691 Tiny Damselfly (Zygoptera) perched on a flower of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the dry banks of the river. What amazing eyes! Look closer on the underside to see several bright red spider-relative Mites (Acari) hitching a ride. I've seen this before. Maybe the mites have their own "Hitchhiker's Guide to the Insects" book! (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2005)

File.Name: 5727d_5727-124.jpg
Notes: 10618619 This Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens) has captured a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) on a plant of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the dry banks of the river. Look close where the bee's right eye should be (screen left) to spot a tiny Fly (order Diptera). The closer I look, the more I don't understand. (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2005)
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Honey Bee; Scale Broom; Fly
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Apis mellifera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; Apis mellifera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Diptera

File.Name: 5727e_5727-138.jpg
Notes: 10618571 This is a brutal photo. The Ambush Bug (order Hemiptera, family Phymatidae) on the left is clutching a little Moth (Lepidoptera) with its claw, getting ready to suck it dead. It's interesting to think that just about every critter alive will meet its end in the grasp of a predator. Are humans really different? (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2005)

File.Name: 5727f_5727-119.jpg
Notes: 10618487 Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) on a plant on the dry river bed. Most grasshoppers I find have tan "earth colors", but maybe this gray color is better camouflage on the dry gravel along the river bed? (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2005)

File.Name: 5728a_5728-191.jpg
Notes:
10626713 American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working a composite flower (maybe Gaillardia?) in the garden - at least this is most of the butterfly. Maybe a bird got the rest of it? Butterfly wings are a big target, but the vital parts are smaller. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2005)

TV pundits talk about the American spirit (substitute any country or affiliation). Poets write about the human spirit. But this photo shows the universal spirit of life. We do what we can and make the best of it. If there was a Callahan's Bar for all critters, I'd buy a cold one for this butterfly survivor and hope to hear its story.

File.Name: 5728b_5728-173.jpg
Notes: 10626438 Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) on a yellow form of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) in the garden. I chased this butterfly all morning. It would spend a minute at the flowers, then flutter around and maybe lay an egg on the milkweed plant - and then disappear for minutes or an hour. These butterflies converge from all across the western states to overwinter in the Eucalyptus groves on the coast just 10 miles away. But they are not common here in the mountains during summer. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2005)

File.Name: 5728c_5727-136.jpg
Notes: 10626286 I believe this is a female Checkered White (Pontia protodice) butterfly working native flowers of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the dry banks of the river. This butterfly has bulbous clubs on its antennae - compare the [Previous] photo. (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2005)

File.Name: 5728d_5727-103.jpg
Notes: 10626242 The curved apiculus tip of the antennae identifies this as a Skipper. I guess it's a female Northern White Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working native flowers of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the dry banks of the river. There are many kinds of white butterflies - compare the [Next] photo. (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2005)
CommonNames: Skipper; Northern White Skipper; Scale Broom

File.Name: 5728e_5728-182.jpg
Notes: 10626131 Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly resting on a leaf in the garden. What a name. My book says the caterpillars feed on Seepwillows and Clematic. That's all I know about this little butterfly, except that I found this one resting with its proboscis unrolled in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2005)

File.Name: 5728f_5727-112.jpg
Notes:
10626058 Another shot of the frisky Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies I found on a flower head of the native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrubs along the river - see my [Previous] photo. The smaller butterfly in the air kept fluttering around and around to attract the larger one on the flower, but s/he was more interested in feeding. Is that just part of their mating game? Maybe EVERY critters' mating game?? (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2005)

With all insects, I expect the female to be larger than the male. She must nourish eggs, but his job is soon done. I guess the female is feeding on the flower, and the male fluttering away trying to get her attention. I reckon we've all been on one side or the other!

File.Name: 5729a_5729-234.jpg
Notes:
10634092 Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly standing tippytoe on a Zinnia flower in the garden. The sun is behind the butterfly and the flower is way overexposed - but look how the butterfly's eye is picking up the violet color. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2005)

The garden was a'flutter with these butterflies this morning - as many as eight at one time. Maybe a new brood has matured in the canyon? These butterflies are almost tame. I couldn't resist taking *lots* of photos.

File.Name: 5729b_5729-232.jpg
Notes: 10634040 This Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly is working a Zinnia flower in the garden. You can barely make out the butterfly's head on top. Follow the proboscis down and you can see it inside the translucent flower tube at the bottom! (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2005)

File.Name: 5729c_5729-208.jpg
