Notes: 10634013 Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly two-fer photo in the garden! They are working flowers of an Iris/Gladiolus relative that I believe is Crocosmia masonorum or kin. We had eight of these butterflies in the garden at one time this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2005)

File.Name: 5729d_5729-218.jpg
Notes: 10633977 Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly getting as far into this garden flower as it can. I believe the pretty flower is an Iris/Gladiolus relative Crocosmia masonorum or kin. I've seen this plant growing as an invasive weed in Northern California. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2005)

File.Name: 5729e_5729-220.jpg
Notes: 10633873 Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) flowers in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2005)

File.Name: 5729f_5729-213.jpg
Notes: 10633853 Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) working a flower of the prolific hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the garden. The background color of these native swallowtails varies from almost white to fairly yellow like this. Our Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterflies have a deeper yellow color. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2005)
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Waverly Sage; Western Tiger Swallowtail

File.Name: 5730a_5729-240.jpg
Notes:
10641714 Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) laying her eggs on leaves of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. It's an exclusive gig between monarchs and milkweeds. I'll be watching for caterpillars to come later this summer. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2005)

You know: First comes love, then comes marriage - then come eggs and several instars of larvae and pupae and maybe a chrysalis and... round and round in the circle game. Click [Back] to see my [Previous] photos today

File.Name: 5730b_5730-014.jpg
Notes: 10641645 A mating pair of Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterflies in the garden - at least they are both in the mood for mating. If you look close at the stripes on their abdomens, you can see that both butterflies are facing forward. Look very close, and I'm sure they are not "doing it" - not yet anyway. They fluttered away together moments after this photo. Be fruitful and multiply, little wings! (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2005)

File.Name: 5730c_5730-015.jpg
Notes: 10641539 A mating pair of Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterflies in the garden. Note that the butterflies are both facing the camera, so they are approaching back to back. See my [Next] and [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2005)

File.Name: 5730d_5730-013.jpg
Notes: 10641512 This Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly in the garden is ready for mating with its partner fluttering nearby. This must be love. It looks so strange to see this butterfly facing towards the camera like this. See [Next] and [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2005)

File.Name: 5730e_5730-022.jpg
Notes: 10641479 Sometimes it's hard to distinguish true love from anger - in ourselves as well as critters as alien as these butterflies. I'm sure this photo shows a love bump between male and female Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterflies. I witnessed this courtship drama several times in the garden today. The smaller and whiter butterfly would feed on the bright Zinnia or Cosmos flowers. The larger and yellower mate would suddenly fly down and bump it like this. Then away they would both fly around each other like a butterfly tornado. After a while, the larger and yellower mate would land in open view and wait for the mate to return. Then they would either take off together to do it again or start over. I guess that doesn't sound so strange! (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2005)

File.Name: 5730f_5730-023.jpg
Notes: 10641385 So that's why they call it Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii)! This shrub in the garden attracts many butterflies like this Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) and American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) - sometimes to the same flower cluster. Sometimes they get aggressive and only one comes back. The [Next] few photos are something different. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2005)
CommonNames: Butterfly Bush; Pale Swallowtail; American Lady

File.Name: 5731a_5731-045.jpg
Notes:
10655868 Looking into a remarkable flower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) in the dry summer chaparral. This is a native Mariposa Lily, definately not a weed. Those colors and hairs are amazing - but it's also remarkable that it flowers in mid-summer when most native plants are already done and gone in central California. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2005)

Today's photos are all flowers and plants rather than my usual summer insects. It's appropriate that "mariposa" means butterfly en espaÒol!

I couldn't connect to fotolog yesterday, but it's just as well. We're heading off to Oregon soon. I went to town today and bought a 300 GB external hard drive to back up all my photos. I also bought a USB2 adapter card for my ancient (but well maintained) Win98 computer. Everything works. I'll leave that drive in a safe place when we take off. This sure feels more secure than 100+ burned CDs!

File.Name: 5731b_5731-042.jpg
Notes: 10655720 Remarkable flower of native Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) in the dry summer chaparral. It's a native Mariposa Lily, definately not a weed. See my [Next] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2005)

File.Name: 5731c_5727-160.jpg
Notes: 10655658 This is not a sea anemone in some tropical ocean. Looking close at a flower of native Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. occidentale) growing on the dry wash along the river. See my [Previous] photo. (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2005)

File.Name: 5731d_5727-158.jpg
Notes: 10655607 Striking flower of native Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. occidentale) growing on the dry wash along the river. It's easy to confuse the native thistles with weedy invaders like Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare) before they flower. Please DO kill the bad ones, but when in doubt, WAIT! (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2005)

File.Name: 5731e_5730-009.jpg
Notes: 10655517 Tiny white flowers of native Yerba Buena (Satureja douglasii). This is a native mint-relative that grows here and there in the woods according to its own rules that I can't quite figure out. It has a wonderful mint aroma and spicy taste. It does well on the native side of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2005)

File.Name: 5731f_5731-058.jpg
Notes: 10655493 Native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the canyon catching the last bit of sunlight just right. The glow only lasted for a minute or so. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2005)

File.Name: 5802a_5731-036.jpg
Notes:
10664003 All four of our nestling Lesser Goldfinch (Carduelis psaltria) baby birds are still in the nest, but they're growing up and getting wary. They used to open their mouths when I made a clucking noise. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10564896

Now I get this "Who are you?" look. Good for them. I don't like to intrude, but I can't resist peeking now and then when Mom is away. (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2005)

File.Name: 5802b_5802-071.jpg
Notes: 10663925 Like a kitten in a mitten - this little Wild Bee (Apoidea) - maybe a Burrowing Bee (family Andrenidae)? - is wrapped up inside a flower of Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) going round and round inside the flower to collect the golden pollen. The bee seems to be filling its baskets! (Santa Ynez River, 2 August 2005)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Burrowing Bee; Chaparral Mallow

File.Name: 5802c_5802-062.jpg
Notes: 10663855 Beautiful Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly working native flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum). The tawny zebra-stripes and twin eye spots on the hind wings with that iridescent blue border are distinctive. It's always a surprise to find this handsome butterfly in the woods. (Santa Ynez River, 2 August 2005)

File.Name: 5802d_5802-092.jpg
Notes: 10663797 The orange abdomen shows that this is a male Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working native flowers of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the dry banks of the river. It was so hot under the sun on the bleached white dry river wash today - I'm surprised any critter was working, but I guess I was too! (Santa Ynez River, 2 August 2005)

File.Name: 5802e_5731-051.jpg
Notes:
10663724 My [Previous] photo shows a fly (order Diptera) that mimics a wasp. Here's a wasp (order Hymenoptera) that mimics a fly - at least it hovers and buzzes like one of those flies that mimic wasps! It's working a bright Zinnia flower in the garden. Compare my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10586867

Judging from the look and behavior of this critter, I think it might be a Mason or Resin Bee in the family Megachilidae that includes the Leafcutter Bees. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2005)
CommonNames: fly; wasp; Mason or Resin Bee; Leafcutter Bees

File.Name: 5802f_5731-032.jpg
Notes: 10663580 This bee-sized critter sure looks like a wasp, but I know it's a Fly (order Diptera) - maybe a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae), but I really don't have a clue. It's colored and sculpted like a wasp, but it has two wings ("di-ptera") instead of four, and you can see the stubby little "haltere" behind the front wing where the hind wing should be. This fly is working Yarrow (Achillea) flowers in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2005)

File.Name: 5803a_5803-161.jpg
Notes: 10671985 It was so hot hiking in the chaparral today... but the temperature seemed to drop when I spotted these pretty flowers of native Canchalagua (Centaurium venustum) growing in a wet place in the chaparral. It's a beautiful wildflower in the unusual (for us) Gentian (Gentianaceae) plant family. It has an ancient name that suggests medicinal uses. It's not common - I hope no one harvests it here. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2005)

File.Name: 5803b_5803-158.jpg
Notes: 10671902 I'm sure this is a native Monkeyflower - maybe Mimulus floribundus? It's growing in a wet place in the chaparral. The leaves are distinctly slimy when you grasp them. I'm amazed that there still is a wet place in the chaparral in the month of August! (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2005)

File.Name: 5803c_5803-114.jpg
Notes: 10671828 Flowers of Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata). This is a local endemic variety in our mountains with narrow leaves rather than the wider leaves of the more common Chaparral Honeysuckle (L. subspicata var. denudata) that is common more inland. It's these differences between here and there that make the local flora so interesting. I watched a Bumblebee (Bombus sp.) visit one of the flowers. It hung down under the flower, and the stamens and pistil pressed tight against its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2005)
CommonNames: Santa Barbara Honeysuckle; Chaparral Honeysuckle; Bumblebee
ScientificName: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; L. subspicata; denudata; Bombus
associatedTaxa: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; L. subspicata; denudata; Bombus

File.Name: 5803d_5803-129.jpg
Notes: 10671654 Toasted native Land Snail (Gastropoda) with a flat shell in a chaparral area that was cleared and burned last year. This is NOT one of those big common introduced pesky Garden Snails with a vertical shell that are so common in town and at school. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2005)

File.Name: 5803e_5803-123.jpg
Notes:
10671562 There are official signs in our mountains warning about bears and mountain lions and rattlesnakes. I'm sure the greatest danger is stumbling on an illicit Cannabis garden like this. Look out for the telltale signs like hoses, PVC pipe, nursery pots, chicken wire, water bottles, etc. - and leave right away! I found this illicit garden on the edge of an area that the NFS Hot Shot crew cleared for a firebreak last summer - in fact I found the remains of two more illicit gardens nearby along the same clearing! (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2005)

Fotolog is for photos not politics, but I can't help wondering... after the "War on Drugs", marijuana is now more expensive and much more potent that it used to be. So what about the "War on Terror"?

File.Name: 5803f_5803-106.jpg
Notes: 10671304 My shoelace after my morning hike yesterday to look for butterflies and native thistles on some abandoned ranch land along the river. I picked up these &%$# "hitchhiker" seeds on my shoelaces and socks - mostly Hedge Parsley (Torilis arvensis ssp. purpurea). It's so easy to pass these weed seeds around to spread even further, and dogs are worse than socks! It helps to wear gaiters - like condoms for weeds. Be sure to clean (or toss) your shoes and socks after any hike through invasive weeds! (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2005)

File.Name: 5804a_5804-203.jpg
Notes: 10679905 Flowers of native Dodder (probably Cuscuta californica) entwined around a seedling shrub of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus). Dodder is a parasitic plant with orange stems and no leaves or roots that sucks life from its host plant. It's related to Morning Glory (Convolvulaceae), but it's now placed in its own Dodder Family (Cuscutaceae). The bright orange stems seem to lose color when it starts to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2005)
CommonNames: Dodder; Greenbark Ceanothus; Morning Glory; Dodder Family
ScientificName: Cuscuta californica; Ceanothus spinosus; Convolvulaceae; Cuscutaceae
associatedTaxa: Cuscuta californica; Ceanothus spinosus; Convolvulaceae; Cuscutaceae

File.Name: 5804b_5803-143.jpg
Notes:
10679899 Love play between female (right) and male (left) Harford's Sulpher (Colias harfordii aka Colias alexandra harfordii) butterflies. You have to appreciate that these butterflies NEVER land with wings open - and rarely land at all. Lepidopterists can study museum specimens pinned in collection boxes. I just take lots of photos and hope for a keeper like this. You can see that the male has thin dark bands on its hind wings. Clouded Sulphurs (Colias philodics) have wider bands. The bright male was at a Lotus flower when the pale female flew in and knocked it away. They chased and tussled (I could hear their wings hitting!) and took off together straight up. I reckon that's when consummation happened. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2005)

There were many of these butterflies "hilltopping" on the cleared and burned area I've been showing in my previous photos. They rarely land to feed, so this is a lucky shot.
ScientificName: Colias harfordii; Colias alexandra harfordii; Colias philodics; Lotus
associatedTaxa: Colias harfordii; Colias alexandra harfordii; Colias philodics; Lotus

File.Name: 5804c_5729-226.jpg
Notes: 10679733 This is the bright yellow Colias Sulphur butterfly in the garden on a Zinnia flower that is NOT a California Dogface (Zerene eurydice). I've been confused, but now I believe this is a female Harford's Sulpher (Colias harfordii or Colias alexandra harfordii) butterfly. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2005)
CommonNames: Colias Sulphur; California Dogface; Harford's Sulpher
ScientificName: Zinnia; Zerene eurydice; Colias harfordii; Colias alexandra harfordii
associatedTaxa: Zinnia; Zerene eurydice; Colias harfordii; Colias alexandra harfordii

File.Name: 5804d_5804-170.jpg
Notes:
10679663 Developing acorns on an interesting native oak. I guess it is a "Shreve Oak" - which may be Quercus parvula; or Quercus parvula var. shrevei; or maybe Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens. It's related to an endemic oak on Santa Cruz Island and maybe a tree in the sierra foothills. I found it as a small evergreen tree 15-20 feet tall in a small grove with 20+ trees. The leaves are 1-3 inches long, some spines or none, shiny green on top and bottom with no hairs. The acorns have thin papery scales and a pretty deep cup. There is another grove of these trees a bit further along West Camino Cielo road that is mentioned in every edition of the "Flora of the Santa Barbara Region" book with several confusing names. (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2005)

If you have issues with "evolution" and "intelligent design" - come take a walk and help me with our local oaks Quercus and bedstraw (Galium) species. Trying to identify these plants with a book is like trying to fit square pegs into fractal holes. I have to think this is "evolution in action".
ScientificName: Quercus parvula; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Quercus; Galium
associatedTaxa: Quercus parvula; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Quercus; Galium

File.Name: 5804e_5803-139.jpg
Notes: 10679437 Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare) is a bad weed that is invading our mountains. Look at this photo from right to left. The young flowers and plants are spiny but attractive, and easy to confuse with native Cirsium thistles. Swallowtail butterflies like them. The thistles get creepy with age and cover the ground with seeds like spider webs. This thistle is growing on the edge of an area that the Forest Service Hot Shot crew cleared for a firebreak last summer. They burned most of the ground last spring, and native fire-follower plants are thriving even in mid-summer - see my [Previous] few photos. There are no thistles on the burned area, but they start right along the line that wasn't burned - and the damn seeds blow everywhere. Wildland management in our chaparral mountains takes more than a chain saw! (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2005)

File.Name: 5804f_5804-183.jpg
Notes: 10679119 Perfect flowers of native Giant-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) flowering NOW in mid-summer in the mountains! This plant is growing in an area that the Forest Service Hot Shot crew cleared for a firebreak last summer and (I guess) burned last spring. This plant is a classic "fire follower". I expect these flowers after fires, but not in mid-summer when it's so hot and dry. But here it is. I didn't see many insects visiting except a few bees. That's the problem keeping your own schedule. (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2005)

File.Name: 5805a_5805-255.jpg
Notes:
10686689 Male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly working a composite flower (maybe Gaillardia?) in the garden. You can see his "dog face" in silhouette through his wings in the upper left. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2005)

I hike every day and sometimes travel, but I think most of the local butterfly action is in our garden at home! I reckon it's like a water hole in the woods for much native life. All of today's butterfly photos are from the garden this morning. We live in the mountains on the edge of the National Forest. It's spooky to visit parks and whole cities where there are no interesting insects except pests. Are cities that deadly for nature?

File.Name: 5805b_5805-256.jpg
Notes: 10686592 Beautiful Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly perched on a blackberry leaf this morning. It likes to perch on a high plant and then chase away any other butterflies - of any species - who fly too close. It was funny to watch because this Admiral perched near the equally feisty little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly I show two photos [Previous]. They were chasing each other all morning! I thought the little Crescent had the edge, and definately more mates. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2005)

File.Name: 5805c_5805-258.jpg
Notes: 10686545 American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working a Pincushion Flower (Scabiosa atropurpurea?) in the garden. These pretty ladies have been our most common butterfly this summer. Look close and see that this disabled veteran is missing its entire camera-side hind wing. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2005)
ScientificName: Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working a Pincushion Flower (Scabiosa atropurpurea
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working a Pincushion Flower (Scabiosa atropurpurea

File.Name: 5805d_5805-236.jpg
Notes: 10686480 Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly perched on a leaf. It has an attitude! Now and then it would visit a flower, but mostly it perched on the same high leaf or twig and chased away any other butterfly that came near. It chased me too - which makes it hard to get a photo! The secret is to crawl up close to the ground. Several times I saw another Mylitta Crescent butterfly wander by and love happens - they chase each other and tussle some, then take up straight into the air fluttering around and around together. You know - like cruising on State Street! (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2005)

File.Name: 5805e_5731-049.jpg
Notes: 10686425 Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly on a flower of Purple Coneflower (Echinacea purpurea) in the garden. I'm sure that "face" with such big eyes might spook a predator! (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2005)

File.Name: 5805f_5805-220.jpg
Notes: 10686395 Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly snuffling deep into a Dahlia flower in the garden. All of today's photos are butterflies in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2005)

File.Name: 5806a_5806-015.jpg
Notes: 10694040 Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) approaching a flower of Cleveland Sage (Salvia clevelandii) with proboscis unfurling. Why do I think of Dumbo and Pegasus?! (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2005)

File.Name: 5806b_5806-024.jpg
Notes: 10693900 Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched to kill on a composite flower (maybe Gaillardia?) in the garden. The yellow and brown colors of the spider are a good match with this flower. But look closer at how the spider's legs/claws match the stamens in the center of the flower. The legs are even dusted with yellow pollen. Now that's camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2005)

File.Name: 5806c_5806-025.jpg
Notes: 10693852 Gnarly wasp with beautiful golden fur and bright orange legs and abdomen visiting the yellow form of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. It is larger than our common social wasps, and it seems to be on its own. I guess this is some sort of Digger Wasp (family Sphecidae). (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2005)

File.Name: 5806d_5806-009.jpg
Notes: 10693813 Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) deeply involved with some kind of "Mexican Sage" (Salvia sp.) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2005)

File.Name: 5806e_5805-223.jpg
Notes: 10693798 Face to face with a Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) on a Dusty Miller (Senecio cineraria) plant in the garden. Hah... I believe that is an eye in the middle of its face between and below the antennae! (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2005)

File.Name: 5806f_5805-265.jpg
Notes:
10693745 Female Harford's Sulpher (Colias harfordii) trying to figure out what to do with this huge dinner plate sized Dahlia flower in the garden. This is the same butterfly I show in my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10679899
This is a lucky shot despite the blur. These yellow butterflies almost always keep their wing folded when they land. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2005)

File.Name: 5807a_5807-051.jpg
Notes: 10701843 Cantaloupe-sized swarm of Honey Bees (Apis mellifera) hanging from a branch in the woods near the river. I'm sure this is just a temporary rest stop. The bees were not aggressive. I moved in pretty close for a look- see my [Previous] photo - they got a little agitated, but they never attacked. (Santa Ynez River, 7 August 2005)

File.Name: 5807b_5807-046.jpg
Notes: 10701793 This is looking close at the top of the Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) swarm in the [Next] photo. I was curious how it was attached to the branch. It seems like it isn't - it's just bees hanging from each other. I wouldn't want to be one of those bees on top! (Santa Ynez River, 7 August 2005)

File.Name: 5807c_5807-031.jpg
Notes: 10701767 I don't have a clue what - or why - this tiny critter is! Look close and you can see a couple of legs sticking out of the top-right. It's less than 1/8 inch long - maybe 2 mm - and it moves pretty quick. I only saw this one on a Yarrow (Achillea) stalk in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2005)

File.Name: 5807d_5805-245.jpg
Notes: 10701705 Insect galls on leaves of Squaw Bush (Rhus trilobata). It's a poison oak relative, but the middle leaf does not have a separate stalk, it has a distinctive "skunk" smell, and it does not make you itch. I believe these galls are caused by Leaf Gall Psyllid (Pachypsylla) insects. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2005)

File.Name: 5807e_5802-061.jpg
Notes: 10701662 Strange insect galls on a leaf of native White Sage (Salvia apiana). Galls are abnormal plant growths caused by insects that protect and nourish their larvae within. There are many different kinds of galls and gall insects. The familiar "oak apples" are caused by tiny wasps. I believe these galls on the sage are caused by the Sage Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia salviae). This is a special kind of tiny fly (order Diptera) in the family Cecidomyiidae. The gall goes completely through the leaf with a round end and a long pointy end shaped like a volcano. I don't notice these galls very often, even when I look for them. (Santa Ynez River, 2 August 2005)
ScientificName: Salvia apiana; Rhopalomyia salviae; Diptera; Cecidomyiidae
associatedTaxa: Salvia apiana; Rhopalomyia salviae; Diptera; Cecidomyiidae

File.Name: 5807f_5802-073.jpg
Notes: 10701662 Here are real flowers (left) and a large leafy gall (right) on a native Tarragon (Artenisia dracunculus). Our native Tarragon has the same species name as the real French tarragon uses as a kitchen herb, but our plant doesn't have much flavor, even when dried. Galls are abnormal plant growths caused by insects. This one is not in my gall book, but I guess it is caused by Gall Midge (order Diptera, family Cecidomyiidae) flies that also infect other Sagebrush shrubs in the same genus with similar bud-like growths. (Santa Ynez River, 2 August 2005)

File.Name: 5808a_5808-092.jpg
Notes:
10709316 (Daisy sequence, 1 of 6) - This flower is a native summer-blooming Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus)in the woods. I don't know what kind of tiny fly (order Diptera) this is - but I'm sure it's NOT a mosquito. Big eyes, humpback thorax, long legs and proboscis, - seems distinctive, I'll try to track it down. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2005)

This photo is the first of a series of photos I took today on this same flower within a few minutes. No particular story, no life or death struggle, but it was exciting how one critter followed another.

I wondered why these photos look so grainy and checked my camera. D'OH - somehow it got set to ISO 800. I did bump it up one stop yesterday to get a flower in the shade. I think when I went to undo it, I went the wrong way and made two stops. Oh well, it's like pushing Tri-X. Now I think of it, that's why these photos look so sharp despite the breeze.

The Fotolog database is getting cranky. One of the photos I posted last night never showed up, though I still see it in my "Archive". I can't delete it. I now have FOUR "active photos". Today I posted a series of photos, but they are not in order on my page. The "Recent Photos" link does seem to have these photos in order. Go there instead of using the [Previous] button. All these photos should have that same yellow/purple daisy in the picture.

File.Name: 5808b_5808-095.jpg
Notes: 10709274 (Daisy sequence, 2 of 6) - This interesting fly (order Diptera)is probing for nectar at the base of this native summer-blooming Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) flower in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2005)

File.Name: 5808c_5808-090.jpg
Notes: 10709251 (Daisy sequence, 3 of 6) - A little Wild Bee (Apoidea) showed up on this Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) right next to the fly. Instead of taking off, the little humpbacked fly Diptera) politely moved to the side and waited. The bee then flew away and the fly returned to the flower. But the bee came back... (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2005)

File.Name: 5808d_5808-098.jpg
Notes: 10709225 (Daisy sequence, 4 of 6) - This little Wild Bee (Apoidea) came back to this Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) and the fly took off. Notice that the bee is collecting yellow pollen in the special "pollen baskets" on its rear legs. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2005)

File.Name: 5808e_5808-100.jpg
Notes: 10709187 (Daisy sequence, 5 of 6) - This little Wild Bee (Apoidea) is still working the native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) flower, but something wicked this way comes from underneath the flower! (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2005)

File.Name: 5808f_5808-102.jpg
Notes:
10709182 (Daisy sequence, 6 of 6) - This spider-like critter is some kind of "True Bug" (order Hemiptera). It really is a gnarly predator - check that dark needle-like proboscis folded under its head ready to suck its victims dry. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2005)

This photo is the last of a series of photos I took today on this same flower within a few minutes. There's no story, no life or death struggle. Bug, bee, and fly overlapped for a moment and then moved away for something else. I've seen movies like that.

File.Name: 5809a_5808-069.jpg
Notes:
10715223 This Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly just emerged from its chrysalis a few inches away. I've been checking the cocoon in the garden every day for 19 days, but I missed its coming out this morning. See my photos (and links) at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10580545

Remember how it feels - opening your wings for the very first time, the world so new and delicious... A moment later it flew away into the world. Be fruitful and multiply, little wing! (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2005)

Jerry Garcia died 10 years ago today. Right now I'm listening to "Bird Song" - words by Robert Hunter, music by Jerry Garcia, written "...for Janis". This is a good song for Jerry, and I want my photos today to be right:


   All I know is something like a bird
   within him sang
   All I know he sang a little while
   and then flew on...

   Sleep in the stars
   don't you cry
   dry your eyes
   on the wind
   lalalada...

BTW - There are 2600+ Grateful Dead concerts spanning 30 years (and many more bands) available for free (and legal) download in the Etree/Internet Archive Live Music Archive:
   http://www.archive.org/audio/etree.php

File.Name: 5809b_5808-081.jpg
Notes: 10715216 This Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly just emerged from its chrysalis a few inches away. I got very close for a photo. A moment later it flew away into the world to unroll its proboscis for the very first time. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2005)

File.Name: 5809c_5808-117.jpg
Notes:
10715211 What an amazing transformation from caterpillar to butterfly! This is the cast-off chrysalis skin of the brand-new Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly featured in the [Next] two photos. The exit opening seems so neat. The bottom of the chrysalis is dark brown. I reckon that's where the waste collects. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2005)

Here's a real stumper. I haven't see many of these Anise Swallowtail butterflies in the garden for several weeks, but I've seen many in the last few days, and here's one more. Do these butterflies synchronize their emergence from cocoons to make it easier to find mates? I notice we're just past a new moon.

File.Name: 5809d_5809-132.jpg
Notes: 10715190 This pair of Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterflies in the garden are either fighting or mating. Maybe they're not sure either! I notice both butterflies are facing the same way instead of facing each other. I could hear their wings bumping together. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2005)

File.Name: 5809e_5809-129.jpg
Notes: 10715167 These are dark white-fringed Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis - or kin) skipper butterflies in the garden. At least they know what species they are. I can guess their gender. I reckon the female on the right is wiggling her abdomen - aka "shake your booty" - and the male is definately interested and moving in. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2005)

File.Name: 5809f_5808-110.jpg
Notes:
10715130 Our Lesser Goldfinch (Carduelis psaltria) baby birds have left their nest. It seems so empty now. I missed the going away party, but I'm sure that's not frosting on the bed! (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2005)

See my photo (and link) at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10664003

File.Name: 5810a_5810-149.jpg
Notes: 10723254 Pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) hovering in the last ray of sunlight. I was in LA today (looking good Dad!), and got home just as the sun was setting. I noticed this little fly hanging in a sunbeam. Pretty cool how it holds it's legs like sled runners. This is cropped tight, handheld, manual focus, no flash - and good luck! (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2005)

File.Name: 5810b_5809-129.jpg
Notes:
10723169 White-fringed dark Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis - or kin) skipper butterfly in the garden. I reckon this is the female on her perch wiggling her abdomen to attract a male. It worked! (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2005)

See my [Previous] photo and my photo yesterday at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10715167

File.Name: 5810c_5809-130.jpg
Notes: 10723165 A tender moment between mating Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis - or kin) skipper butterflies in the garden. The female on top is wiggling her abdomen to attract the male - it's definately working. Look how their antennae are touching. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2005)

File.Name: 5810d_5808-089.jpg
Notes:
10723158 The little humpbacked fly Diptera) in the back was working this native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) flower first. When this little Wild Bee (Apoidea) landed on the flower, the little fly politely moved to the side and waited. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2005)

See my "Daisy sequence" of photos starting at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10709316
and [Previous] to that.

File.Name: 5811a_5809-137.jpg
Notes:
10729453 Today - August 11 - is the last of the "Dog Days of Summer" - at least that's what wikipedia says that the Old Farmer's Almanac says ;=)
   http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dog_days

I figure that means "Dog-face days". I've been trying to get a photo of a male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly showing its beautiful upper wings. That blue color in this photo is not real. It's an iridescent color, like the rainbow colors on a CD, or spilled gas at the corner station. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2005)

This Dogface is the official California State Butterfly - for good reason - because of the remarkable iridescent colors on its upper wings. But the males always close their wings when they land. You can only capture them in the air. The butterflies take off so quick - I've been clicking away at 3 frames per second for 50-100 photos at a time hoping for a keeper - and it still takes off between frames! Gotta love *digital* to delete all those misses and try again! I'll try again tomorrow.

File.Name: 5811b_5809-135.jpg
Notes: 10729438 Male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly showing the beautiful iridescent "dog face" pattern on its upper wings. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2005)

File.Name: 5811c_5811-159.jpg
Notes: 10729427 Male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly in the garden showing the beautiful iridescent "dog face" pattern on its upper wings. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2005)

File.Name: 5811d_5811-157.jpg
Notes: 10729420 I was trying to get another photo of the beautiful iridescent "dog face" pattern of this male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly working a Zinnia flower in the garden, but the angle was wrong. Still pretty cool. It is flying AWAY from the flower, to the right. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2005)

File.Name: 5811e_5809-133.jpg
Notes: 10729408 Here's an action shot - this Jumping Spider (Salticidae) has captured a fly. I hope its one of those pesky "oak gnat" flies that keep buzzing in my eyes and ears when I'm trying to focus. This happened in the blink of my eye and the click of my shutter. Not quite in focus, but I love the shadows. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2005)

File.Name: 5811f_5808-125.jpg
Notes: 10729404 I was trying to get a photo of this native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium) wildflower with flower (right) and seeds (left). I didn't notice those two spider-like "True Bugs" (order Hemiptera) until I got back to my computer. I like the little "flags" on their antennae! What very tiny critters are these tiny predators waiting to capture? Are they more interested in making more predators for next year? (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2005)

File.Name: 5812a_5812-168.jpg
Notes: 10737966 Look close and it's obvious that this is a moth rather than a bee or wasp, but you really do have to look close! This is a Clear-winged Moth (order lepidoptera, family Aegeriidae) working flowers of Thyme (Thymus vulgaris or kin) during the day-shift in the garden. It has perfect wasp colors and even flies like a wasp, but the fat antennae and long proboscis give it away. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2005)

File.Name: 5812b_5812-164.jpg
Notes: 10737966 This is a Clear-winged Moth (order lepidoptera, family Aegeriidae) working flowers of Thyme (Thymus vulgaris or kin) during the day-shift in the garden. It has perfect wasp colors and even flies like a wasp, but the fat antennae and long proboscis give it away. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2005)

File.Name: 5812c_5809-136.jpg
Notes: 10737961 Large Bumblebee (Bombus sp.) working a composite Zinnia flower in the garden - it has trouble balancing on the flower head! (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2005)

File.Name: 5812d_5812-172.jpg
Notes: 10737942 Leaf-like Katydid (Tettigoniidae) - maybe the Broad-winged Katydid (Microcentrum rhombifolium)? - on Yarrow (Achillea) flowers in the garden. They are attracted to lights at night. Last night I jerked awake in my chair with one of these in my face! Disturbing, not dangerous. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2005)

File.Name: 5812e_5811-154.jpg
Notes: 10737936 Immature Long-Horned Grasshopper (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) - maybe a Katydid ([Next] photo) or Cricket ([Previous] photo)? Whatever it is, it's getting the elixer of life from this tiny Dill (Anethum graveolens) flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2005)

File.Name: 5812f_5811-153.jpg
Notes: 10737923 Not quite mature Snowy Tree Cricket (Oecanthus fultoni or kin) perched on a leaf in the garden. It sure looks like a California Lobster! I'm just starting to hear these crickets at night. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2005)

File.Name: 5813a_5813-007.jpg
Notes: 10745714 I don't know what this insect is. It's resting on a native White Sage (Salvia apiana) leaf. It's maybe 3/16 inch long - just a few millimeters. It sure looks distinctive with those "bug eyes" and paddle wings and neat whiskers. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2005)

File.Name: 5813b_5813-010.jpg
Notes:
10745746 This large bee - maybe a Bumblebee (Bombus) or a lost Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa)? - is curled up for the night in this white Dahlia flower in the garden. I used flash for this photo, it's really almost dark. I bumped around with my tripod and it didn't budge. I'm sure this bee is here for the night. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2005)

Anyone else remember Robert Heinlein's book "Red Planet" when the boys survive the frigid Martian night inside the cabbage-like plant that closes up to protect them?!

File.Name: 5813d_5805-271.jpg
Notes: 10745569 I guess this is a wasp-like Bee (Apoidea) rather than a bee-like wasp working these Yarrow (Achillea) flowers in the garden - but I'm really not sure. I sure don't understand that "thing" it uses for a proboscis to feed from these flowers. How does all that fit into its head? (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2005)

File.Name: 5813e_5806-007.jpg
Notes: 10745567 This is one of those almost-black skipper butterflies hanging on a Dahlia flower in the garden. Maybe it's a Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis - or kin). Great name for this Goth critter that sure looks like a bat! (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2005)

File.Name: 5813f_5809-139.jpg
Notes: 10745564 Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly working a Zinnia flower in the garden. Of course I've photographed these butterflies many times! (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2005)

File.Name: 5814a_5727-166.jpg
Notes:
10754316 American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working a flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides). (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2005)

Despite the "eyes" on the wings looking back, this butterfly is looking away - me too. I'm off to Oregon for a week on the North Umpqua River. Gone fishing - and maybe a few photos!
ScientificName: Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working a flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working a flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides

File.Name: 5823a_5819-233.jpg
Notes:
10823427 This is the nastiest critter I've ever encountered. That white wasp on top with the red demon eyes and gnarly jaws is subduing the Yellowjacket (Vespula) wasp or hornet on the bottom. Wow - imagine eating yellowjackets for breakfast! At first I thought the white wasp was a bee - in other photos it seems to have flattened rear legs like pollen cages, but this critter never visits flowers. I also saw it attack honey bees and skippers and even big butterflies, sometimes in the air. Just by body type, I guess this wasp is order Hymenoptera and family Vespidae, but I can't find anything like it in my books. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 August 2005)

I've been in Oregon for the last week exploring the North Umpqua River drainage. I had some chores at my parents' house, but I managed to get a few photos every day. Today's photos are all about love and death.

File.Name: 5823b_5819-238.jpg
Notes: 10823181 Life and death struggle between the white-and-black wasp and the yellow-and-black Yellowjacket (Vespula) wasp. The white wasp flew off with its prey after a short but intense battle. I guess both wasps are order Hymenoptera and family Vespidae, but that black and white one is really gnarly! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 August 2005)

File.Name: 5823c_5818-081.jpg
Notes: 10823083 Mating Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae - probably genus Hesperia) butterflies joined to transfer genetic material. You can see how different male and female are... as if learning butterfly species isn't hard enough! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2005)

File.Name: 5823d_5818-137.jpg
Notes: 10823074 Mating Robber Flies (order Diptera, family Asilidae). It's almost tender how the fly on top is stroking its partner's head with its forelegs. These flies are perfect predators always hunting for prey. It must be hard for them to get together like this. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2005)

File.Name: 5823e_5818-099.jpg
Notes: 10823053 Mating Grasshoppers (family Acrididae) on a leaf of Corn Lily aka Hellebore (Veratrum viride or kin). By themselves, I would never connect these two critters. Learning insects is hard enough without males and females being so different! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2005)

File.Name: 5823f_5819-206.jpg
Notes: 10823042 Maybe this native Pine White (Neophasia menapia) butterfly slipped while it was getting a drink in this puddle. That's it for this butterfly. Culture lets us pass these hard lessons on to warn our kids! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2005)

File.Name: 5824a_5816-029.jpg
Notes:
10830619 This West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly landed on my thumb and didn't want to leave even after I got the photo. I figure it was after salt from my sweat. I moved the butterfly to the sweaty bandana on my head and it stayed there happily for at least half an hour as I continued hiking. Of course, I couldn't see it up there, but whenever I leaned forward I could see its shadow. I was alone in the woods - sorry, no photo. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 16 August 2005)

Today's photos are all butterflies from my trip to Oregon last week.

File.Name: 5824b_5818-144.jpg
Notes: 10830521 What rich colors - and a clown mask! - on this "Greater Fritillary" (genus Speyeria) butterfly working the flowers in a mountain meadow. I won't try to guess what species this is. I'm sure the fritillary butterfly in the [Previous] photo is different. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 16 August 2005)

File.Name: 5824c_5820-007.jpg
Notes: 10830517 Another "Greater Fritillary" (genus Speyeria) butterfly working late flowers of Himalayan Blackberry (Rubus discolor). There are many kinds of fritillary butterflies, so I won't even try to guess what species this is. I'm sure it's different form the butterfly in the [Next] photo. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 August 2005)

File.Name: 5824d_5818-160.jpg
Notes: 10830504 I guess this is a Lilac-bordered Copper (Lycaena nivalis) butterfly (male?) working flowers of Pearly Everlasting (Anaphalis margaritacea) in a mountain meadow. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2005)

File.Name: 5824e_5819-188.jpg
Notes: 10830495 California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly "puddling" at a small water seep. This butterfly is shockingly brilliant on top with the sun behind, but it is very dark with wings closed - compare my [Previous] photo. They always land with their wings closed, but they flex them open every so often. I took many photos - and deleted them - to get this shot. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 August 2005)

File.Name: 5824f_5819-187.jpg
Notes: 10830483 California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly with wings closed. Even with the sun behind, these are pretty good camouflage colors. Compare my [Next] photo of the same butterfly with wings open! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 August 2005)

File.Name: 5825a_5818-172.jpg
Notes:
10837446 Perfect flower of native Scarlet Gilia aka Skyrocket (Ipomopsis aggregata) in the Oregon mountains at about 5,000 feet elevation above the river. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2005)

No creepy insects today - just beautiful wildflowers I found on my trip to Oregon last week. What a treat to find flowering mountain meadows when the flowers at home are just about done.

File.Name: 5825b_5817-049.jpg
Notes:
10837213 Perfect flower of Red Columbine (Aquilegia formosa) in an Oregon mountain meadow at about 5,000 feet. It's a classic hummingbird flower with sweet nectar hidden in the bulb at the top of the long spurs (top of photo) and no place to land. This same species grows deep in the Santa Barbara backcountry. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2005)

The name "columbine" comes from the latin "columbina" which means "dove-like". The arched petals and spurs of the flower maybe resemble a quintet of doves around a bowl. The flower (different species) appears in classic paintings of Mary as a symbol of the dove of the Holy Spirit. These days the beautiful name has a very different meaning.

File.Name: 5825c_5819-216.jpg
Notes: 10837050 New flowers of Pacific Bleeding Heart (Dicentra formosa). This native is still flowering in the Oregon mountains at about 5,000 feet elevation where it still feels like spring. Can you spot the green aphid? (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 August 2005)

File.Name: 5825d_5818-178.jpg
Notes: 10836978 Fireweed (Epilobium angustifolium) is a native in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family. It has weedy habits, mostly growing along roads and after clearcuts and fires. I love it! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2005)

File.Name: 5825e_5818-091.jpg
Notes: 10836851 I believe this odd flower is Western Monkshood (Aconitum columbianum) growing in a marshy mountain meadow, about 5,000 feet elevation. All Harry Potter fans should know that "Monkshood and Wolfbane... are the same plant, which also goes by the name of Aconite." With names like that, I wouldn't want to taste it. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2005)

File.Name: 5825f_5817-072.jpg
Notes: 10836738 Composite Aster flowers - maybe California Aster (Aster chilensis), native despite the name, though there are many different aster species in the Oregon mountains. This is growing along the North Umpqua River near one of my favorite fishing spots. The river and sun behind are making pretty patterns in the background. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2005)

File.Name: 5826a_5818-182.jpg
Notes:
10845241 So many kinds of wild blackberries - see my [Previous] photos today - but I think this creeping blackberry high in the mountains at 5000+ feet elevation is my favorite. Maybe it is Dwarf Bramble (Rubus lasiococcus), or maybe it's a stunted Blackcap Raspberry (Rubus leucodermis). (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2005)

Today's photos are all ripe berries from my trip to Oregon last week. Some are edible and some are not.

File.Name: 5826b_5821-036.jpg
Notes: 10845156 The wild blackberries you pick along the roads in Oregon are probably this Himalayan Blackberry (Rubus discolor). *Sigh* It's introduced and considered a noxious weed. It sure does have tasty berries on those huge brambles. We also have these blackberries in the Santa Barbara mountains. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2005)

File.Name: 5826c_5818-085.jpg
Notes: 10845053 Tasty fruit of native Blackcap Raspberry (Rubus leucodermis). The dark berry is ripe. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2005)

File.Name: 5826d_5818-180.jpg
Notes: 10845009 Berries of native Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus). We have these plants back home in the mountains behind Santa Barbara, but they rarely set fruit. The berries are thin but tasty. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2005)

File.Name: 5826e_5818-169.jpg
Notes: 10844853 We have blue elderberries (Sambucus mexicana) at home, so it's interesting to find this beautiful Pacific Red Elderberry (Sambucus racemosa) shrub in the Oregon mountains. The berries here and there are EDIBLE - ONLY IF COOKED! They do taste pretty funky if you're foolish (like me) and nibble the raw berries. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2005)

File.Name: 5826f_5818-104.jpg
Notes: 10844759 These beautiful berries are Baneberry (Actaea rubra) - they look too red to be real. They also come in a white form that looks too white to be real. Red and white forms grow together, with the red more common. The berries look beautiful, but heed the name - your "bane" is your downfall - something that brings misery or death, like these berries. I reckon that's the stumper. Most plants want their berries to be eaten to (literally) pass the seeds. What critters eat Baneberries? Why would plants produce poisonous berries? (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2005)

File.Name: 5827a_5816-031.jpg
Notes: 10853041 This perfect little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly looks like its posing in a photo studio. It's a small frittilary relative that we also have at home. I rarely see them feed, but they like to perch up high to attract a mate. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2005)

File.Name: 5827b_5819-219.jpg
Notes: 10852884 Orange Sulphur (Colias eurytheme) butterfly with the sun behind to show the dark patterns on the upper wings - it's not quite a dog-face, but there is a hint. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 August 2005)

File.Name: 5827d_5821-030.jpg
Notes: 10852869 Little Wild Bee (Apoidea) collecting pollen inside a flower of common Chicory (Cichorium intybus). This is an introduced weed establshed along the roads in Oregon. The flowers only open in the morning, so it's very easy to not notice how pretty and strange they are. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2005)

File.Name: 5827e_5817-064.jpg
Notes: 10852798 This Robber Fly (order Diptera, family Asilidae) let me creep up close, but it watched my every move until I got too close. This large preditor is at least an inch (25 mm) long. You can see the bristles and knobs on it's front legs that it uses to catch prey in the air and hold them while it sucks them dry with that gnarly spike-like proboscis below the eyes. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2005)

File.Name: 5827f_5816-013.jpg
Notes:
10852715 Here's what happens if you eat too many of those tasty wild blackberries I show in the [Previous] few photos! Some Black Bear (Ursus americanus) dropped this load on a Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii) "pine cone" on the woods. At least now we know that the bear does do it in the woods... (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 16 August 2005)

Interesting that blackerry fruit and leaves can cause and cure "the runs" - like Homer Simpson says about beer, "the cause of - and solution to - all life's problems".

File.Name: 5828a_5816-040.jpg
Notes:
10861014 Perfect Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly perched on a blackberry leaf in Oregon. I occasionally find these butterflies at home too. They don't often feed at flowers, but they perch like this in a prominent place to command a flyway and chase away other butterflies. The very similar California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterflies at home do the same thing. It's been fun watching them chase falling leaves! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 16 August 2005)

I've been posting photos from my recent Oregon trip all week, and here are more butterflies from Oregon. I have been taking new photos at home, but it's just "too darn hot" as the song goes. It still feels 100+ degrees in my study as the sun goes down, like it's been all day. Maybe tomorrow. School starts on Wednesday. Despite the heat, I know the seasons and my commitments are starting to change. "Life goes on.

File.Name: 5828b_5819-191.jpg
Notes: 10860874 Pine White (Neophasia menapia) butterfly working flowers of Purple Star-thistle (Centaurea calcitrapa). (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 August 2005)

File.Name: 5828c_5819-207.jpg
Notes: 10860861 I believe this pretty creature in the Oregon woods is a Western Tailed Blue (Everes amyntula) butterfly. You can see a hint of blue between the upper and lower wings. Swallowtails and hairsteaks should have tails, not blues. If this is "intelligent design" then nature is out to get us! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 August 2005)

File.Name: 5828d_5818-074.jpg
Notes: 10860819 Tattered West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly working composite flowers of Tansy Ragwort (Senecio jacobaea. This plant is an all-too-common invasive weed, but the butterflies like it. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2005)

File.Name: 5828e_5819-205.jpg
Notes: 10860697 This pretty Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae - probably genus Hesperia) - butterfly is tasting everything with its long proboscis. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 August 2005)

File.Name: 5828f_5819-218.jpg
Notes:
10860688 This handsome butterfly in an Oregon mountain meadow at about 5,000 feet really confused me. It refused to pose in the sun - I was lucky to get this shot. At first I thought Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) - nah. Maybe a dark Swallowtail (Papilio sp.) - nah. Now I'm pretty sure this is a female Great Spangled Fritillary (Speyeria cybele) butterfly. She doesn't look anything like the typical orange male butterflies. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 August 2005)

I have a lot to learn about butterflies, and many more photos to get!
CommonNames: Mourning Cloak; Swallowtail; Great Spangled Fritillary

File.Name: 5829a_5829-076.jpg
Notes:
10868808 Pretty little Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo - probably Apodemia mormo virgulti) butterfly working flowers of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the dry river wash. I also have new photos of this critter working California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) flowers at home last week. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2005)

I've been posting photos from my Oregon trip because it's so nice to think of those mountain meadows when it's so hot and dry here at home. Despite the 100+ degree heat, there's a hint of fall in the air - something about the light and the critters. Today's photos are all from the last week back home (sweet home).
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Scale Broom; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum; Eriogonum fasciculatum
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum; Eriogonum fasciculatum

File.Name: 5829b_5826-141.jpg
Notes: 10868614 Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly sucking moisture from the wood chips in our garden after watering. These butterflies have brilliant eyespots above, but they almost disappear when they close their wings to show the underside like this. I was laying flat on the ground trying to wiggle closer without spooking it. I love those speckled eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2005)

File.Name: 5829c_5823-106.jpg
Notes: 10868606 Pretty little Bee Fly (order Diptera, family Bombyliidae) with proboscis extended getting ready to work a composite Zinnia flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2005)

File.Name: 5829d_5829-070.jpg
Notes: 10868598 Little gray Damselfly (Zygoptera) perched on a flower head of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the dry banks of the river. Look close - it's munching a little fly that it caught in the air! (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2005)

File.Name: 5829e_5829-081.jpg
Notes:
10868588 First I thought this large very green "grasshopper" was a Katydid. But it definately has short antennae, so it must be a Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae). It was acting cranky and trying to hide under leaves, but it didn't hop away when I nudged it. Now I understand - it's starting to molt! You can see its new face with yellow and black stripes through the translucent green skin it's about to shed! (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2005)

Sorry to hassle you buddy. I guess I didn't get time off for puberty either!

File.Name: 5829f_5824-123.jpg
Notes:
10868578 Mating Grasshoppers (family Acrididae) on a Dusty Miller (Senecio cineraria) plant in the garden. Are males and females usually different size? (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2005)

Compare my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10823053

Notice that strand of hair across the photo? I guess it's from me or Julie or Mojo - I keep finding hairs in my photos at home. I guess we all shed all the time like our dog Mojo! That's why hairs and fibers are so important in court cases.

File.Name: 5830a_5830-099.jpg
Notes:
10876805 Is this the mouth of Leviathon or the entrance to hell? Nope, this photo is looking into a flower of San Pedro Cactus (Trichocereus Pachanoi or kin) in the garden. The flower is the size of my shoe, native to the Andes, I'm surprised to find it flowering now. "San Pedro" is Saint Peter who holds the keys. Try a web search for more info, it's an interesting story. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2005)

It's been a good summer with lots of good photos to show for it. I'm a teacher - I go back to school tomorrow for meetings and planning, new classes next week. It's good timing. The woods are getting quiet. It's still hot, but there's change in the air... at least that stillness before the change comes.

File.Name: 5830b_5828-057.jpg
Notes:
10876626 The woods are stranger than we think. Here are brilliant insect galls on a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) leaf in the woods. Galls are tumor-like growths of plant cells initiated by an insect or other critter that "stings" the plant and lays eggs to develop inside the abnormal growth. Most of these oak galls are started by tiny Gall Wasps (Cynipidae). This photo is extra weird. I don't know if this is two forms of gall from one wasp or different galls from two different wasps. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2005)

I have no time to look up the details now. But I really wonder why these insect galls are so pretty!

File.Name: 5830c_5828-054.jpg
Notes: 10876618 Strange fuzzy tumor-like gall under a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the woods - like cotton candy! Tiny Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) initiate these galls so their eggs can develop inside. Many mysteries. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2005)

File.Name: 5830d_5828-056.jpg
Notes: 10876602 Beautiful tumor-like gall on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the woods. Tiny Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) initiate these galls so their eggs can develop inside. There's an older gall on the left that has lost its color. But why do these insect galls have any color at all since they are not designed to attract insects? I guess it's the same reason why men have nipples - it's easier to adapt than change! (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2005)

File.Name: 5830e_5829-066.jpg
Notes: 10876595 You sure can't sneak up on one of these Damselflies (Zygoptera) - those eyes see everything! This one is perching on flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) near the river. We're playing an "IC-UC" ("I see - You see") game to see how close I can get. This is about it. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2005)

File.Name: 5830f_5828-039.jpg
Notes: 10876585 The wasps love this old teapot for a drink - even though there is a modest 4x4 foot "pond" nearby. These wasps all seem to be Golden Paper Wasps (Polistes fuscatus) - or kin in the Vespidae family, but sometimes I see several species of wasps and hornets shouldering together at the same font - a truce drawn from need, like an African waterhole. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2005)

File.Name: 5831a_5831-102.jpg
Notes:
10885313 I never noticed how brilliantly *spotted* these butterflies are until I started sneaking up on them to get photos! This male Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) is working the yellow-form Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed flowers in the garden. This photo is a crop from my [Previous] photo. The photo [Previous] to that shows how I know it's a male. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2005)

Monarch butterflies east of the Rockies migrate all the way to Mexico for the winter. Western Monarchs come to my house! Well, not quite, but they have a standing invitation. Western Monarchs all winter along the central/southern California coast, including some sites just 10 miles away in Goleta/Ellwood. Maybe that's why I don't see many Monarchs at home during the summer - only those apples that didn't roll far from the tree.

File.Name: 5831b_5831-102.jpg
Notes:
10885198 Perfect male Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) working the yellow-form Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed flowers in the garden. See my [Next] photo for a closer look at its head. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2005)

Notice the almost-white highlight running along the hind wing from left-middle to lower-right, and then notice the thicker spot towards the lower end of the black vein just above. I think that's the sign of a male. See my [Previous] photo.

File.Name: 5831c_5831-107.jpg
Notes:
10885190 Top view of the same perfect male Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) featured in the [Next] two photos, but now he's working a Dahlia flower in the garden. Milkweeds are the exclusive larval plant for the caterpillars, but the adults are more cosmopolitan. Notice that black spot along the vein of the hind wing. That's the mark of a male. It looks lumpy like a gland, not just a spot. I hope it finds a mate and fathers prodigious progeny. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2005)

I've been chasing this particular butterfly for a few days, but it's photo shy and always takes off when I get close. I think it helps to stand around every day and be visible. Let it feed for a while before you approach. When you do move in, keep your lens pointed right at the butterfly. Raising my lens up when I'm close spooks them every time!

File.Name: 5831d_5831-100.jpg
Notes: 10885117 This seedpod of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed is opening in the garden. The fertile seeds are leaving the pod and drifting away. It's a perfect image for this time of year when our kids are heading back to - or away to - or beyond - school. Teachers too! (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2005)

File.Name: 5831e_5828-046.jpg
Notes: 10885113 The acrobatic seed pods of Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) are starting to open to drop seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2005)

File.Name: 5831f_5824-128.jpg
Notes: 10885091 Empty seed pods of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) - a kind of Mariposa Lily. I hope your seeds will live long and prosper! (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2005)

File.Name: 5901a_5901-122.jpg
Notes:
10893221 Little Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working a Yarrow (Achillea) flower in the garden - a lucky shot with wings open, they usually close their wings while they take nectar. You can see its tails in the back. The filigree of orange along forewings and crown of head is wonderful. I believe this is a female with gray rather than orange abdomen. Be fruitful and multiply, pretty lady! (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2005)

This butterfly looks very different with wings folded. See my [Previous] photo.

File.Name: 5901b_5901-120.jpg
Notes: 10893188 Pretty Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working a Yarrow (Achillea) flower in the garden. It sure gets remarkable curves in its proboscis/tongue! This photo shows the wings folded like usual. Check my [Next] photo to see the wings open. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2005)

File.Name: 5901c_5901-125.jpg
Notes:
10893172 Handsome orange wasp - maybe a Digger Wasp (family Sphecidae)? - visiting the yellow form of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. Look at all those little black "berries" on its front legs. Those are milkweed "pollinia" or pollen sacks that the wasp has picked up by visiting these flowers. You can see two more pollinia waiting to be snagged on the flower below the wasp's abdomen - if the wasp steps in the right place. Sometimes wasps and bees get stuck. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2005)

See my [Previous] photo. I have more info at: http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8108247

File.Name: 5901d_5901-132.jpg
Notes:
10893131 This orange wasp - maybe a Digger Wasp (family Sphecidae)? - is visiting the yellow form of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed flowers in the garden. It's been trapped by a flower! The wasp's two rear legs are stuck in the "horns" of the flower. It was strong enough to pull free, but it probably picked up more milkweed "pollinia" or pollen sacks by doing so. You can see some of those little black "berries" on its front legs. Milkweed flowers offer sweet nectar to pollinating insects, but they expect them to carry their pollen from plant to plant! (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2005)

See my [Next] photo. I have more info at: http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8108247

File.Name: 5901e_5901-113.jpg
Notes: 10893117 Now this gets weird. This tiny Damselfly (Zygoptera) isn't any bigger than a sewing needle with a pinhead. It wasn't flying much and let me get closer than usual, despite those huge eyes that see everything. I didn't notice the orange "baggage" it's carrying in its "leg-pits" until I got back to my computer and looked closer. At first I thought maybe hitchhiking mites. Now I guess this critter is infected with a fungus, and those orange blobs are fruiting bodies - little mushrooms - like "Athlete's Foot" but maybe fatal. There are many fungi that attack insects - benign ones like Laboulbeniales and nasty ones like Entomophthora> or Cordyceps. Some of these fungi might be important for pest control, but damselflies are preditors not pests. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2005)

File.Name: 5901f_5901-118.jpg
Notes: 10893112 I love nature because it keeps catching me by surprise after all these years - and I love fotolog for getting me outside every day to look closer and get even more surprised! I thought this wasp was a Mud Dauber, but it has a different trick. I watched it chew off the dead end from a grass stem and carry it into this pipe we're using as a support in the garden. I reckon this is a Thread-Waisted Wasp (order Hymenoptera, family Sphecidae). It's photo shy. What's going on inside that pipe? (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2005)

File.Name: 5902a_5901-134.jpg
Notes: 10900607 Beautiful Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae - genus Hesperia or kin) - butterfly working milkweed flowers of non-native Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) in the garden. I don't even try to identify these skippers - there are so many different kinds, it's easier just to "skip her"! See my [Previous] photo to see what happens when these butterflies become careless. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2005)

File.Name: 5902b_5902-150.jpg
Notes:
10900530 "Look before you land" - This Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae - genus Hesperia or kin) - butterfly fell prey to a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a Zinnia flower in the garden. It should have little "X"s on its eyes to match that half-coiled proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2005)

Look close at the front legs of this butterfly and notice it's carrying milkweed "pollinia" or pollen sacks from another flower in our garden. This could be the same butterfly that's in my [Next] photo.

File.Name: 5902c_5902-154.jpg
Notes: 10900519 This Zinnia flower in the garden is a deadly trap for pollinators! The predator is a rather large Crab Spider (Thomisidae). Usually these spiders color-match their flowers (See [Previous] photo}, but this flower has been going for so long there's no color to match except brown. I guess it's trying. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2005)

File.Name: 5902d_5902-141.jpg
Notes:
10900485 Even at the end of our dry California summer, there are new flowers appearing in the woods like this native California Goldenrod aka Oreja de Liebre (Solidago californica). (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2005)

Did you spot the yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hiding between the flowers just below center?
CommonNames: California Goldenrod; Oreja de Liebre; Crab Spider

File.Name: 5902e_5901-130.jpg
Notes:
10900481 Maybe this is a Digger Wasp (family Sphecidae)? It's embracing a yellow milkweed flower of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) in the garden. Look those chains of dark milkweed pollen sacks or "pollinia" on its front legs. The wasp is getting sweet nectar, and the milkweed is getting pollination transport and service. At least here, the "invisible hand" helps both critters. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2005)

I have more info about milkweed pollination at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8108247

File.Name: 5902f_5831-103.jpg
Notes: 10900467 I've been seeing lots of these new Western Fence Lizards aka Blue Belly Lizards (Sceloporus occidentalis) in the canyon. This one is only about an inch long (2.5 cm), and it's very fast. Look - it's up on its toes like a sprinter, ready to go! (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2005)
CommonNames: Western Fence Lizards; Blue Belly Lizards

File.Name: 5903a_5902-147.jpg
Notes: 10908248 Dandelion-like seeds of Prickly Lettuce aka Compass Plant (Lactuca serriola) in the canyon. The mature leaves tend to line up east-west to face the sun in the south, hence the name. It's a common weed. The seeds are pretty, but each of them is an alien DNA packet built to travel and prosper! (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2005)

File.Name: 5903b_5828-084.jpg
Notes:
10908144 Ripening seeds of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia). They look delicious, but taste bitter. (Santa Ynez River, 28 August 2005)

There's a stumper here. The whole purpose of fruit is that critters eat them and (literally) pass the seeds. So why would any plant have bitter or poisonous berries??

File.Name: 5903c_5825-138.jpg
Notes:
10908130 New fruit of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella). The fruit come in colors: yellow is unripe; red is getting there; and purple is ripe. Birds like the berries, but I've never been tempted. This plant is cousin of Cascara Sagrada (R. purshiana), a powerful laxative. That makes sense as a way to literally pass seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2005)

Notice the white crust on the berries? This shrub is on the dry side of our garden, but it gets splashed by the sprinkler. That's how "hard" our well water is. Plumbing suffers, but I reckon minerals are good, right?

File.Name: 5903d_5823-119.jpg
Notes: 10908119 We have a good Apple (Malus pumila) crop this year, if the Scrub Jays don't knock them all down first. Like Johnny Appleseed - aka John Chapman - I started this tree from a seed, a spit seed or tossed core, when we built our house 25 years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2005)

File.Name: 5903e_5823-117.jpg
Notes: 10908091 Ripe Tomatillos (Physalis ixocarpa) in the garden. They are the essential ingredient in chile verde and much of my favorite food. The green fruit are good, and the ripe fruit are pretty good too. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2005)

File.Name: 5903f_5903-005.jpg
Notes: 10908057 Yipes - this pretty flower is a trap! Here's a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a Zinnia flower in the garden, poised to kill. The spider is white not yellow, but look how the dark claws match the lines of the composite flowers. I have more of this spider in the last few [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2005)

File.Name: 5904a_5904-011.jpg
Notes:
10916568 Face-to-face with a new Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly over a brunch plate of Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare). Pretty flowers, none for me, thanks. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2005)

I first noticed Bull Thistle in our mountains 10 or 15 years ago on a Forest Service fire break near home. It is now a real problem on local fire breaks that are not burned after clearing. I took all of today's photos on a fire break that was burned last spring. There are still butterflies and fire-follower wildflowers. Other cleared areas that were not burned have gone to invasive weeds. This seems easy and should be law - burning after clearing must be Forest Service policy.

File.Name: 5904b_5904-019.jpg
Notes:
10916328 I'm getting really confused by local wasps. I guess this critter is a solitary Vespid Wasp (order Hymenoptera, family Vespidae). It's photo-shy and buzzes in my face when I get too close like this. The flower is Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare) - a nasty invasive weed, but at least it supports some wildlife before it goes to seed. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2005)

Notice the yellow and black markings on the back and compare the next few photos.

File.Name: 5904c_5904-021.jpg
Notes: 10916310 This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) and the same solitary Vespid Wasp (order Hymenoptera, family Vespidae) I show in the [Next] photo are sharing a flower head of Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare). Doesn't it look like the wasp is giving the bee "the eye" - the truce didn't last long. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2005)
CommonNames: Honey Bee; Vespid Wasp; Bull Thistle
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Vespidae; Cirsium vulgare
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Vespidae; Cirsium vulgare

File.Name: 5904d_5904-036.jpg
Notes:
10916300 Mating wasps, joined back-to-back, resting for a moment on a stem of a native Everlasting aka Cotton-Batting Plant (Gnaphalium stramineum?) growing by a damp seep in the mountains. I reckon this proves they are solitary rather than social. This wasp has different markings from the [Next] and [Previous] wasps. I guess it is a different solitary Vespid Wasp (order Hymenoptera, family Vespidae). (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2005)

I only got one chance for this photo. No, I didn't notice which way they took off!

File.Name: 5904e_5904-037.jpg
Notes:
10916282 I'm sure these are Yellowjackets (order Hymenoptera, family Vespidae, genus Vespula) crowding at a tiny damp seep in the summer-dry mountains. The bees had another (drier) seep nearby, and they didn't mingle. These wasps are obviously social unlike their solitary cousins in the last few photos. Note how the yellow/black markings are all different from the wasps in my [Next] few photos. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2005)

These wasps are nasty. They get even nastier as summer ends and their days are numbered. They can get on your food between plate and mouth - Ouch!

File.Name: 5904f_5904-053.jpg
Notes: 10916275 Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working the last summer flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) with the sun behind. I reckon it's a female with orange rather than gray abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2005)

File.Name: 5905a_5904-040.jpg
Notes:
10924334 It feels like spring looking at these pretty flowers of Yellow-throated Phacelia (Phacelia brachyloba) in the mountains. It's really the end of summer, after months of hot desert weather since our last rain. That's our central California Mediterranean climate with wet winters and dry summers. But this flower (and the [Previous] flowers) are still flowering on a section of chaparral that was cleared and burned last spring. I don't understand, but it's a pleasure! (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2005)

*Sigh* I go back to school tomorrow (as teacher) after a grand summer. We have classes and a potluck dinner tomorrow, then a school camping trip. I`ll be back on the weekend with new photos!
Locality: as teacher) after a grand summer.  We have classes and a potluck dinner tomorrow

File.Name: 5905b_5904-028.jpg
Notes: 10924219 It's a treat to end summer with pretty flowers like this native Whispering Bells (Emmenanthe penduliflora). It's a fire-follower growing now in a place that burned last spring. Other plants of this species flowered back when this burned. I have no idea why or how it is flowering now after so many months of no rain. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2005)

File.Name: 5905c_5904-017.jpg
Notes:
10924209 Interesting flowers of native Dodder (probably Cuscuta californica) entwined around a branch of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus). Dodder is a parasitic plant with no green chlorophyll for photosynthesis. The bumps on the thread-like orange stems form "haustoria" that penetrate into the host plant to rip off water and nutrients. The flowers are related to Morning Glory (family Convolvulaceae), but now the plant is placed in its own Dodder Family (Cuscutaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2005)

Weird stuff. Always reminds me of "Invasion of the Body Snatchers".
CommonNames: Dodder; Greenbark Ceanothus; Morning Glory; Dodder Family
ScientificName: Cuscuta californica; Ceanothus spinosus; haustoria; Convolvulaceae; Cuscutaceae
associatedTaxa: Cuscuta californica; Ceanothus spinosus; haustoria; Convolvulaceae; Cuscutaceae

File.Name: 5905d_5904-045.jpg
Notes: 10924195 New fruit of native Islay aka Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the chaparral. These are real cherries, but they have a thin layer of flesh around a large pit. The fruit are bitter - even poisonous - too early, but they are tasty when ripe. Some critter (and a bug if you look close!) has been nibbling, like peeking at the presents before Christmas. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2005)

File.Name: 5905e_5829-080.jpg
Notes: 10924175 Spiky seedpod of Toloache (Datura wrightii). The spikes say "Don't mess with me" - good advice. Pretty though! (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2005)

File.Name: 5905f_5905-063.jpg
Notes: 10924162 This white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a Zinnia flower in the garden has scored another Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae - genus Hesperia or kin) - butterfly. I've been watching this particular spider for the past few days. Check the [Recent Photos] archive. What a nasty "face" on the abdomen - who is it meant to scare? (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2005)

File.Name: 5910a_5909-442.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 13822942 Chicken of the Woods aka Sulphur Shelf (Laetiporus sulphureus) mushroom fruiting at the base of Blue Gum Eucalyptus (Eucalyptus globulus or kin).
