This is the first new mushroom I've see this (rain) year, proof that the seasons are changing. Mushroom books say it`s edible, but I`ve gotten sick from it - Caveat emptor. (Morro Bay, 9 September 2005)
CommonNames: Chicken of the Woods; Sulphur Shelf; Blue Gum Eucalyptus
ScientificName: Laetiporus sulphureus; Eucalyptus globulus; Caveat emptor
associatedTaxa: Laetiporus sulphureus; Eucalyptus globulus; Caveat emptor

File.Name: 5910b_5909-453.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 13822906 The orange growth is not a lichen, but the algae Trentepohlia aurea var. polycarpa growing on dead branches of this Monterey Cypress (Cupressus macrocarpa) tree growing over the high tide line. (Morro Bay, 9 September 2005)
ScientificName: Trentepohlia aurea; polycarpa growing on dead branches of this Monterey Cypress (Cupressus macrocarpa
associatedTaxa: Trentepohlia aurea; polycarpa growing on dead branches of this Monterey Cypress (Cupressus macrocarpa

File.Name: 5910c_5908-302.jpg
Notes:
10963019 I thought these are all lichens growing on dead branches of this Monterey Cypress (Cupressus macrocarpa) tree hanging over the high tide line at Morro Bay near the Heron rookery. The bright orange growth is actually the algae Trentepohlia aurea var. polycarpa. Algae - think seaweed or "pond scum" - need constant moisture by themselves, but they partner with fungi to form lichens that are much tougher. This algae apparently gets enough moisture from regular fog and sea spray. The color is said to come from beta carotene. (Morro Bay, 9 September 2005)

Back to school! We took our school camping and exploring at Morro Bay and Montana de Oro. I was busy with my classes on the beach, but I managed to get some photos. It was almost always foggy and drippy. D'oh - I bumped my "ISO film speed" to get campfire photos at night and then forgot to reset it.

File.Name: 5910d_5909-396.jpg
Notes: 10962769 Chicken of the Woods aka Sulphur Shelf (Laetiporus sulphureus) mushroom fruiting at the base of Blue Gum Eucalyptus (Eucalyptus globulus or kin). This is the first new mushroom I've see this (rain) year, proof that the seasons are changing. Most mushroom books say it`s edible and "foolproof", but don`t trust it here in California! I`ve gotten sick from it and so have several friends - Caveat emptor. (Morro Bay, 9 September 2005)
CommonNames: Chicken of the Woods; Sulphur Shelf; Blue Gum Eucalyptus
ScientificName: Laetiporus sulphureus; Eucalyptus globulus; Caveat emptor
associatedTaxa: Laetiporus sulphureus; Eucalyptus globulus; Caveat emptor

File.Name: 5910e_5909-389.jpg
Notes: 10962760 Bull Kelp (Nereocystis luetkeana or kin) seaweed washed up on a rocky beach. You can have great fun with these making whips, totems, and even playable trumpets if you carve finger holes in a section of the stipe! (Montana de Oro, 9 September 2005)

File.Name: 5910f_5909-391.jpg
Notes: 10962735 Pretty composite flower of California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia, aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) with fog drip. We also have this species at home, but these flowers are different. It's another polymorphic complex in need of study, more than a species, the stuff of evolution. (Morro Bay, 9 September 2005)

File.Name: 5910g_5909-457.jpg
Notes: 10962723 Flower and seed pod of native California Croton (Croton californicus) growing in chaparral on stabilized sand near the beach. This perennial or subshrub is dioecious with separate male and female plants. This is a lady. (Morro Bay, 9 September 2005)

File.Name: 5910h_5909-440.jpg
Notes: 10962716 Western Spotted Cucumber Beetle (Diabrotica undecimpunctata) snuffling into a flower of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus). These northern plants have a glabrous calyx - flowers at home are hairy at the base, and used to be considered a different species - Mimulus longiflorus aka Diplacus longiflorus. It's hard enough to learn these plants without all the name changes! (Morro Bay, 9 September 2005)
CommonNames: Western Spotted Cucumber Beetle; Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Diabrotica undecimpunctata; Mimulus aurantiacus; Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus
associatedTaxa: Diabrotica undecimpunctata; Mimulus aurantiacus; Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus

File.Name: 5911a_5910-002.jpg
Notes:
10972725 Tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) tiptoeing over a Zinnia flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2005)

I expected cold and cloudy weather at Morro Bay, but it followed us home. The seasons are changing, but I reckon it's just a pirouette between hot and cool. Should I wear long pants or shorts for school tomorrow morning?

File.Name: 5911b_5910-004.jpg
Notes:
10972544 I think this is a female Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) working the yellow-form Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed flowers in the garden. The males have a dark spot/gland on the hindwing, but I don't see it here. I hope the male and female butterflies find each other. (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2005)

Compare my photo of the male monarch butterfly at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10885190

File.Name: 5911c_5911-017.jpg
Notes: 10972534 Female Cabbage White (Pieris rapae or maybe P. napi?) butterfly on a Snapdragon plant in the garden. The male butterfly is fluttering nearby. This lady is raising her abdomen up in the air to show that she's interested, but the male didn't return. Maybe that big guy with the camera queered the rendezvous. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2005)

File.Name: 5915a_5911-013.jpg
Notes: 11005925 Here's looking at you... Despite all the brilliant eyespots on its wings, this Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly almost disappears on the mulch of dry Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves on the ground in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2005)

File.Name: 5915b_5911-022.jpg
Notes: 11005885 Tiny immature Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a Zinnia flower petal in the garden. It's not much bigger than a tick except for those deadly dark claw-like front legs. This little spider is already missing a leg on its left side. Does the spider get it back when it molts? (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2005)

File.Name: 5915c_5911-018.jpg
Notes: 11005838 Mature leaf-like Katydid (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) in the garden. I love the two-tone eyes and the "specular" reflections from the wings, like water. This critter is all grown up and looking for action - you can see the impressive "genitalia" on its hindquarters. These large Katydids keep getting into our house at night - impressive when they land on the TV screen or make shadows on the lampshade. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2005)

File.Name: 5915d_5910-006.jpg
Notes: 11005637 Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working the way-over-my-head flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. As we slide into autumn, that's where the light is. (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2005)

File.Name: 5917a_5917-003.jpg
Notes: 11020231 Perfect camouflage - tiny Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) hiding in leaves of native Yerba Buena (Satureja douglasii). (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2005)
ScientificName: Pseudacris regilla; Hyla regilla) hiding in leaves of native Yerba Buena (Satureja douglasii
associatedTaxa: Pseudacris regilla; Hyla regilla) hiding in leaves of native Yerba Buena (Satureja douglasii

File.Name: 5917b_5917-050.jpg
Notes: 11020193 Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden Today. There were at least a dozen of these pretty ladies in the garden together, along with a few smaller American Ladies (Vanessa virginiensis) butterflies. This must be the southward migration to balance the remarkable northward migration that passed through last spring. It's a stumper HOW and WHY delicate butterflies like these Ladies and Monarchs can migrate across whole states and nations and oceans. They seem so vulnerable. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2005)
CommonNames: Painted Lady; Butterfly Bush; American Ladies

File.Name: 5917c_5917-011.jpg
Notes: 11020173 Some kind of large native Thread-Waisted Wasp (order Hymenoptera, family Sphecidae) getting intimate with a Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2005)

File.Name: 5917d_5917-027.jpg
Notes: 11020162 Tiny Ambush Bug (order Hemiptera, family Phymatidae) hiding in a flower head of native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica). (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2005)

File.Name: 5917e_5917-001.jpg
Notes: 11020114 Yellow-orange Milkweed Aphids (maybe Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) grazing on Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2005)
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 5917f_5917-023.jpg
Notes: 11020104 Face-to-face with a Grasshopper (family Acrididae) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2005)

File.Name: 5918b_5918-069.jpg
Notes: 11028155 Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working the flowers of the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden today. This is a true "Brush-footed" (subfamily Nymphalinae) butterfly. You can see the two reduced front legs below the eyes, so that the butterfly appears four-legged. I reckon it needs those big eyes to guide its two-channel proboscis into that tiny flower. I can't resist trying to get closer and closer! (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2005)

File.Name: 5918d_5918-073.jpg
Notes: 11028107 "Bells on her toes and ears on her knees" - This mature Katydid (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) in the garden really does have two-tone eyes and ears on its knees. That dark oval on its front leg is the "tympanum" sound/vibration sensor. Makes perfect sense - you know how you FEEL the loud music in a club through the floor! (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2005)

File.Name: 5918e_5918-081.jpg
Notes: 11028048 Pretty Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working a yellow-form Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed flower in the garden. The seasons are changing. These familiar butterflies are getting scarce. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2005)

File.Name: 5918f_5917-055.jpg
Notes: 11028024 Looking at the pointy wings, I reckon this is a female California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly working this Zinnia flower in the garden. The seasons are changing, and these familiar summer butterflies are getting scarce. How do they pass from one summer to the next? That is a real stumper for all insects! (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2005)

File.Name: 5923a_5922-192.jpg
Notes:
11069086 I almost missed the light show last night, but I stepped ouside while waiting for the Daily Show on TV - this is the trail of a missile launch from Vandenberg Air Force Base last night, heading south. I'd say "Rocket Summer" - except yesterday was the Autumnal Equinox, so this must be "Rocket Autumn" - sounds ominous. Vandenberg AFB is about 40 miles away, at the mouth of the Santa Ynez River. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2005)

I have a stumper and more photos about these beautiful National Defence Light Shows at:
   http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/27sep02.html

File.Name: 5923b_5923-235.jpg
Notes: 11068864 Little Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) resting on an old peeling ornamental goose in the garden. I love the individual "footprint" of each leg. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2005)

File.Name: 5923c_5922-188.jpg
Notes: 11068852 Awesome Tarantula Hawk (order Hymenoptera, genus Pepsis) wasp with golden antennae and wings working native flowers of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. This wasp is dragonfly-size, one of the biggest in the world. (Santa Ynez River, 22 September 2005)

File.Name: 5923d_5918-075.jpg
Notes:
11068792 Bristly little fly (order Diptera) - maybe a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae) or Scavenger Fly (Sarcophagidae) in the garden. It's perched on the lip of an old pipe in the garden we're using to hold up tomatoes. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2005)

This pipe happens to hold a colony of interesting Thread-Waisted Wasp (order Hymenoptera, family Sphecidae), see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10893112

The fly didn't want to leave. Some action is going down!
CommonNames: Tachinid Fly; Scavenger Fly; Thread-Waisted Wasp

File.Name: 5923e_5918-065.jpg
Notes:
11068780 Tattered but still pretty Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. There are at least a dozen of these ladies in the garden. This must be the southward migration to balance the remarkable northward migration that passed through last spring. It's remarkable that these Ladies and Monarch butterflies migrate across whole states and nations and oceans. They seem so vulnerable. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2005)

I posted this photo a few days ago - and got comments - but it disappeared into the database black hole. Here it is again.

File.Name: 5923f_5918-063.jpg
Notes:
11068761 Them there eyes! Tiny Damselfly (Zygoptera) resting on a leaf in the garden. It's a preditor that sees everything and catches its prey in the air between those bristles on its legs. I understand the big eyes and bristles, but why would any critter need such an intense color? (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2005)

I posted this photo a few days ago - and got comments - but it disappeared into a database black hole. Here it is again.

File.Name: 5924a_5924-004.jpg
Notes: 11076149 Mating Grasshoppers (order Orthoptera, family Acrididae) on a furry Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata) leaf in the garden. I guess that's the female in the foreground, much larger than her mate, ready to produce a generation of eggs. I also noticed she was distractedly nibbling on the leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2005)

File.Name: 5924b_5924-020.jpg
Notes: 11076087 This is a marble-size clay wasp nest on a stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the canyon. Five of these nests - all with exit holes - were arranged one above the other along this stem. I reckon this is the mud nest of a solitary Potter Wasp (order Hymenoptera, superfamily Vespoidea, family Eumeninae), related to the social Yellow Jackets and Paper Wasps. The nest looks like one of those sugar Easter eggs with a treasure inside - also like an Oriental Poppy (Papaver somniferum) seedpod! Other Eumenid wasps make clay-lined nests in the ground or in the pith of plant stems. Is that ornate circular lip in the upper-left where mama-wasp inserts her egg before sealing the chamber? It looks like culture. I might have photos of the adult wasps in the archive, but I wouldn't recognize them. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2005)
CommonNames: Chamise; Potter Wasp; Yellow Jackets; Paper Wasps; Oriental Poppy; Eumenid
ScientificName: Adenostoma fasciculatum; Hymenoptera; Vespoidea; Eumeninae; Papaver somniferum
associatedTaxa: Adenostoma fasciculatum; Hymenoptera; Vespoidea; Eumeninae; Papaver somniferum

File.Name: 5924c_5924-011.jpg
Notes: 11076083 This composite Feverfew (Tanacetum parthenium) flower in the garden is a deadly trap for pollinators - notice the well-camouflaged Crab Spider (Thomisidae) ready to pinch and suck! (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2005)

File.Name: 5924d_5924-007.jpg
Notes: 11076070 Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the garden with a little Fly (order Diptera) for breakfast on a composite Feverfew (Tanacetum parthenium) flower. This spider is a preditor, but note that it's missing most of its hind leg. Life is tough in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2005)

File.Name: 5924e_5924-017.jpg
Notes: 11076056 Bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) working new flowers of a male pollen-bearing Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea) in the canyon. This native shrub is "dioecious" with separate male and female plants that are just starting to flower. It is one of the late-summer natives that keep the insects alive through the year. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2005)

File.Name: 5924f_5924-025.jpg
Notes: 11076047 Bristly Tachnid Fly (Tachinidae) working new flowers of a male pollen-bearing Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea) in the canyon. A fly is just a fly... until you look close and see how different they really are. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2005)

File.Name: 5925a_5925-046.jpg
Notes: 11084256 This little "fingerling" Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) has caught a tiny fly (order Diptera) for breakfast, maybe a Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae). It's cool and breezy today - it feels like autumn, and there's a chance of rain tonight. I was sitting in the garden waiting for a photo, and here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2005)
CommonNames: Western Fence Lizard; fly; Syrphid Flower Fly

File.Name: 5925b_5925-044.jpg
Notes: 11084149 This tiny new Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) has caught a little fly (order Diptera) for breakfast - maybe a Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae). The inch-wide Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf serving as banquet table gives a sense of scale. I was laying flat on the ground to get this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2005)
CommonNames: Western Fence Lizard; fly; Syrphid Flower Fly; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Sceloporus occidentalis; Diptera; Syrphidae; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 5925c_5925-042.jpg
Notes: 11084141 Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed flower in the garden - do you see the Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting waiting waiting for prey - knives on throat, ready for the kill... It's officially autumn. Pollinators are getting scarce in the woods as the preditors and cool weather take them out. It's the end of the days of summer. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2005)

File.Name: 5925d_5925-035.jpg
Notes: 11084130 The giant spider guards the entrance to the Colossal Cave... This "cave" is the throat of a Snapdragon (Antirrhinum) flower in the garden. The irony in that few insects visit these flowers. The spider was waiting all day hoping for some action. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2005)

File.Name: 5925e_5925-032.jpg
Notes: 11084118 Katydid (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) munching pollen from a Zinnia flower in the garden. Gotta love those two-tone eyes and ears on the knees! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2005)

File.Name: 5925f_5924-006.jpg
Notes: 11084109 Little Grasshopper (order Orthoptera, family Acrididae) munching on a leaf of furry Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata) in the garden. Maybe it has something else on its mind - see [Previous] photo. Gotta love those eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2005)

File.Name: 5930a_5930-269.jpg
Notes:
11124557 Common Milkweed Bug (order Hemiptera, family Lygaeidae, species Lygaeus kalmii or kin) on an open seed pod of native Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis). It seems to be sucking the hard milkweed seed. Look close - this "true bug" has different names, colors, and behavior from the Large Milkweed Bug (Oncopeltus fasciatus) in the [Previous] photo. I didn't notice the differences until I looked close at my photos. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 September 2005)

The Milkweeds (family Asclepiadaceae) plants are so common and so interesting! They are interesting like orchids, with specialized anatomy and many unique associations with particular insects.
Locality: family Asclepiadaceae) plants are so common and so interesting! They are interesting like orchids

File.Name: 5930b_5930-272.jpg
Notes: 11124412 A group of Large Milkweed Bugs (order Hemiptera, family Lygaeidae, species Oncopeltus fasciatus or kin) congregating on a seed pod of native Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis). My book says these "true bugs" are often used in laboratory experiments because the are easily raised in large colonies. You notice different characteristics as a photographer - they always seem to congregate on the shady side of the plant. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 September 2005)

File.Name: 5930c_5930-270.jpg
Notes: 11124240 Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis) bursting with seeds. This native perennial mostly grows along the highway, and I think I know why. The cars and trucks whizzing by create a constant gusty breeze to disperse the dandelion-like seeds along the road - and frustrate photographers like me. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 September 2005)

File.Name: 5a01a_5a01-037.jpg
Notes: 11131623 Summer is past - the autumnal equinox was last week - so I didn't expect to find a new butterfly on my morning walk. But here is a beautiful male Great Purple Hairstreak (Atlides halesus) butterfly working these male pollen-bearing flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea) in the canyon. I don't think I've ever seen this critter before, and I only saw this one. I got a glimpse of the upper wings through my camera as it few away - all brilliant iridescent blue (not purple) like that crescent between the wings. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a01b_5a01-034.jpg
Notes: 11131496 Beautiful male Great Purple Hairstreak (Atlides halesus) butterfly working the new male pollen-bearing flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea) in the canyon. It's "sawing" its hind wings to show flashes of the brilliant iridescent blue (not purple) upper wings. I've lived in these mountains for 30+ years, and I don't think I've ever seen this butterfly. I know... it's easy NOT to notice butterflies unless you're looking for them - but this is a hard one to miss. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a01c_5a01-041.jpg
Notes: 11131487 Perfect Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly working male pollen-bearing flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea) in the canyon. Even past the end of summer and the equinox, this must be a new brood to lay eggs or whatever they do to get ready for winter. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a01d_5a01-019.jpg
Notes: 11131480 I guess this is a female Cabbage White (Pieris rapae, or maybe P. napi?) butterfly working the yellow-form Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed flowers in the garden today. These common butterflies lay eggs on plants in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family, but they visit many different flowers for a snack. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2005)
ScientificName: Pieris rapae; P. napi; Asclepias tuberosa; Brassicaceae
associatedTaxa: Pieris rapae; P. napi; Asclepias tuberosa; Brassicaceae

File.Name: 5a01e_5a01-013.jpg
Notes: 11131449 Female California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly working the disk-flowers of a composite Zinnia in the garden. Notice the yellow pollen grains on the butterfly's proboscis. Pollination happens. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a01f_5a01-043.jpg
Notes: 11131421 Must be a new brood - maybe the last for the year - of California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterflies in the canyon. This female is working a Zinnia flower in the garden, giving a peek under (over?) the wings with the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a02a_5a02-061.jpg
Notes:
11138979 "Strange fruit hanging from the Butterfly Bush..."

This Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly hanging like a strange fruit from the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) flowers in the garden caught my eye. See my [Previous] photo for a different view. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2005)

If you don't know the Billie Holiday song "Strange Fruit", there's a sample at:
   http://www.pbs.org/jazz/video/audioplayer/jazzaudio_strangefruit.html

It's shocking to hear this song for the first time. At first you wonder what it's about. Then you get it. This photo too.

File.Name: 5a02b_5a02-066.jpg
Notes: 11138947 Look at top-center of the photo to see the white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that has captured this Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly hanging like a "strange fruit" from the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) flowers in the garden. It's past the end of summer. Preditors are picking off the pollinators as we head towards winter. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2005)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Painted Lady; Butterfly Bush

File.Name: 5a02c_5a02-063.jpg
Notes: 11138936 Female Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly resting on a leaf in the garden. A moment later it was gone. I haven't seen any of these butterflies for weeks, but I guess there's time for one more brood before winter weather sets in. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a02d_5a02-075.jpg
Notes: 11138911 I used my camera in hyper-mode at 3 frames per second to get a photo of the iridescent top side of the male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly working the Zinnia flowers in the garden. I took 250 photos *snap*snap*snap* in five different series. These butterflies are so fast they go from rest to gone in 1/3 second almost every time! This is the best I got. I'm not complaining - this butterfly is doing a helicopter move, pushing off and flipping over at the same time! (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a02e_5a02-053.jpg
Notes: 11138883 I think this small "cruciform" Plume Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Pterophoridae) just emerged from the empty cocoon at the top. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a02f_5a02-054.jpg
Notes: 11138868 The spider and the fly - I was looking at something else and swung around just in time to catch this Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider carry away its unfortunate victim. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a04a_5a02-057.jpg
Notes: 11157210 I guess these first flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) are the first flowers of the new rain year rather than the last of the old. The seasons are really changing. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a04b_5a01-030.jpg
Notes: 11157125 Mating Grasshoppers (order Orthoptera, family Acrididae) on furry leaves of Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a04c_5a01-024.jpg
Notes: 11157117 Female California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly working a Zinnia flower in the garden. Notice the yellow pollen grains on the butterfly's proboscis. This is how butterfly pollination happens. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a07a_5a05-131.jpg
Notes:
11180705 That's my thumb at the bottom!

I finally captured this large male California Mantid (Stagmomantis californica) that was prowling in my study after midnight. I chased it for a while, and it got down in the dust under my desk. I think it was grateful when I finally caught it and released it outside. This native "Praying Mantis" is several inches long. I got this photo on the way to the door to release it. I'm holding it in my left hand, and working the camera with my right hand. The light is a mix of compact flourescent and television, so the white balance is weird. This critter is brown, not blue. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a07b_5a05-128.jpg
Notes: 11180548 It's spooky to make eye contact with an insect - a sense of intelligence and self-awareness. This native California Mantid (Stagmomantis californica) was trapped in my study after midnight. It sat on my desk and looked right at me in a pleading way, and it didn't struggle much when I finally captured it to release outside. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a07c_5a05-129.jpg
Notes: 11180540 Native California Mantid (order Orthoptera, family (Mantidae, species Stagmomantis californica) walking across my desk after midnight. This brown "Praying Mantis" is a California native. The larger green mantids are all introduced from Europe and China. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a08a_5a08-037.jpg
Notes:
11188619 I watched this new Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) caterpillar eat a whole leaf of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. Then it started munching the orange Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on the same plant! At first I thought the caterpillar was "licking" the aphids for honeydew, but it is definately consuming them. I don't usually think of caterpillars as preditors, but here it is. Is this documented? (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2005)

I found two Monarch caterpillars on the milkweed today - the first I've seen this year. I've been looking ever since I got this photo:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=10641714

And what is that giant pink aphid in the upper-right? Maybe it's carrying a parasite. I'll check back tomorrow.
CommonNames: Monarch Butterfly; Butterfly Weed; Milkweed Aphids
ScientificName: Danaus plexippus; Asclepias tuberosa; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens
associatedTaxa: Danaus plexippus; Asclepias tuberosa; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens

File.Name: 5a08b_5a08-036.jpg
Notes: 11188506 Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) caterpillar with a face full of aphid husks as it munches on the bright orange Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on this plant of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. The fibers are from an opening seedpod. I don't usually think of caterpillars as preditors, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2005)
CommonNames: Monarch Butterfly; Milkweed Aphids; Butterfly Weed
ScientificName: Danaus plexippus; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Danaus plexippus; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 5a08c_5a08-021.jpg
Notes:
11188500 This bright orange Milkweed Aphid (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on top is about to go for a ride on this dandelion-like seed of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2005)

I'm really puzzled by these bright orange aphids. I never see them on any plant except this garden milkweed late in the season. How do these aphids pass from summer to summer in our climate? It's almost like they come with the seed...
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 5a08d_5a08-011.jpg
Notes: 11188496 Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) with full pollen sacks working male pollen-bearing flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a08e_5a08-001.jpg
Notes: 11188487 Katydid (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a08f_5a08-043.jpg
Notes: 11188481 This native Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has perfectly matched the flowers of this non-native Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a09a_5a09-068.jpg
Notes:
11197772 Life and death in the garden - look close to see the white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the lower left clutching this Bee Fly (order Diptera, family Bombyliidae) in a deadly embrace on this composite Zinnia flower in the garden. You can tell by the yellow pollen that this little fly has been a good pollinator. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2005)

I put this as my stumper for the week:

I did photograph a new butterfly last weekend, but I mostly got photos of spiders and other predators preying on the pollinators. The seasons are a-changing. We still have our noisy cricket chorus at night, but all the summer butterflies and bees and pesky flies and other insects that delight and bedevil us - they mostly go unnoticed - will soon disappear for winter. Sure, the insects will be back next summer, and I'll be waiting with my camera and my flyswatter. How do all these little critters survive over the winter? How do they get from now to then?

File.Name: 5a09b_5a09-050.jpg
Notes: 11197702 Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting on a Zinnia flower in the garden. The seasons are a-changing and the pollinators are disappearing - these spiders are part of the reason why. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a09c_5a08-004.jpg
Notes: 11197694 Tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) - maybe an immature form of the spider in the [Next] two photos? - waiting on a Snapdragon (Antirrhinum) flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a09d_5a09-045.jpg
Notes: 11197691 Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on one of the last flowers of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon. This spider is a color-shifter, waiting like a set mousetrap for the right prey to come by. Maybe disturbing... but it's the end of summer, and all the pollinators will soon disappear in one way or another. These spiders are common now. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a09e_5a08-009.jpg
Notes: 11197685 Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) with full pollen sacks - like sticks of butter! - working male pollen-bearing flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a09f_5a08-024.jpg
Notes: 11197635 Not the Worm Ouroboros - the mythical serpent biting its own tail, encircling the world - this is a Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) caterpillar on Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. It consumed a milkweed leaf from the bottom up. I wondered what would happen when it got to the top - here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a10a_5a08-005.jpg
Notes: 11206498 Ripe Red Savina Chiles (Capsicum chinense) in the garden - said to be the hottest of them all. That little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) doesn't seen to mind. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a10b_5917-012.jpg
Notes: 11206450 Beautiful ripe Habenaro Chiles (Capsicum chinense) in the garden. The amazing color is also a warning that these fruit are muy picante! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a15a_5a15-007.jpg
Notes:
11250925 One of my books describes this Spiny Tachina Fly (order Diptera, family Tachinidae, species Paradejeania rutiloides or kin) as having "dense vestiture of enormous, stiff, black bristles" - I'll say it's hairy! This bumblebee-size fly is working the male ("staminate") flowers of (5 of 6 - Photos first - I'll come back with comments as soon as I can.) It's interesting that this bristly fly lays eggs that become internal parasites of the equally bristly Tiger Moth (order Lepitoptera, family Arctiidae) "woolly bear" caterpillars. Preditor and prey share a family resemblance! Maybe it's not a coincidence that both flies and moths are now on the wing. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2005)

Sure this fly has wings - I just happened to capture it with both wings on edge so they almost disappear - like a little hedgehog in the flowers!
ScientificName: Diptera; Tachinidae; Paradejeania rutiloides; staminate; Lepitoptera; Arctiidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Tachinidae; Paradejeania rutiloides; staminate; Lepitoptera; Arctiidae

File.Name: 5a15b_5a15-010.jpg
Notes: 11250761 I saw this Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) in an unlikely pose on one of the last flowers of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon. Then I got close for this photo and noticed the well-camouflaged Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that captured it. Preditors are finishing off the pollinators as the seasons turn. But this unfortunate pollinator has been laying eggs that will become internal parasites of other insects further down the line. That's everyday life and death in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2005)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Canyon Sunflower; Crab Spider

File.Name: 5a15c_5a14-261.jpg
Notes: 11250757 Nickle-size Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a15d_5a15-002.jpg
Notes: 11250755 Lobster-like male Snowy Tree Cricket (Oecanthus sp.) hiding on a leaf in the garden. The females have narrower wings that fold around the body. This is our "thermometer cricket" because pitch and frequency are a function of temperature. The folklore is to count the chirps in 15 seconds and add 40 for degrees Fahrenheit. Only these males sing. It's cold tonight, and quiet. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a15e_5a08-013.jpg
Notes: 11250739 Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) with a load of pollen approahing male pollen-bearing flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a15f_5a09-058.jpg
Notes:
11250735 Mushroom-like gall of the Live Oak Gallfly (Callirhytis pomiformes) - a tiny wasp in the Cynipidae family - coming from the midrib of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf near home. Galls are tumor-like growths on plants caused by certain insects that re-program the host tree to grow a distinctive home for their young. I reckon that's an exit hole, but it could also be the entry hole of a parasite. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2005)

These critters have a complicated life cycle with alternating generations of tiny stingless wasps. Male and female wasps will emerge from these leaf galls. They will mate and lay eggs that will develop into large "Oak Apple" type galls on stems. (See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=1804154)

Parthenogenic females will emerge from those galls and lay eggs without mating that will develop into more of these leaf galls. It's a complicated life cycle and a strange sort of genetic engineering that works as a life strategy for these tiny wasps!
CommonNames: Live Oak Gallfly; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 5a16a_5a16-075.jpg
Notes: 11260109 Pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) in the air approaching flowers of a non-native Sage in the garden - maybe Salvia guaranitica? (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a16b_5a16-067.jpg
Notes: 11259975 Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) with a full load of pollen working a garden Sage - maybe Salvia guaranitica? (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a16c_5a16-071.jpg
Notes: 11259959 Female Snowy Tree Cricket (Oecanthus sp.) hiding inside a Snapdragon (Antirrhinum) flower in the garden. She has narrow wings wrapped around her body. The males have broad wings and "sing" - really they stridulate - by rubbing their wings together. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a16d_5a16-072.jpg
Notes: 11259953 I keep noticing these Crab Spiders (Thomisidae) with prey in the garden and woods. This spider captured a moth (order Lepidoptera) and worked it all day long. The flower is Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. I reckon it's good advice to never eat anything bigger than your head! (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a16e_5a16-091.jpg
Notes: 11259939 Bright orange Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. This pretty milkweed is not native in California, nor is the aphid. I understand how the plant reseeds itself, but how do the aphids manage to come back year after year? (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2005)
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 5a16f_5a16-088.jpg
Notes: 11259925 Somehow the mushrooms know! Here are the first real mushrooms of the new (rain) year. An hour later we got a trace of rain. These are Bolete mushrooms with tan caps, pores rather than gills, the pores are yellow and instantly turn blue when bruised, the stem is not reticulate, the base is enlarged and maybe rooting - I had to dig it out - sounds like Boletus "marshii" - "Ben's Bitter Bolete" according to David Aurora's "Mushrooms Demystified" book. I nibbled and it didn't taste bad, but I don't trust it. I should change that message to DON'T EAT ME! (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a22a_5a22-003.jpg
Notes: 11312265 The spider and the fly (1 of 4) - I watched this little Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) move closer and closer to the motionless Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on this Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) flower in the garden. Like a horror movie - I won't scream... (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2005)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Crab Spider; Mexican Sunflower

File.Name: 5a22b_5a22-004.jpg
Notes: 11312208 The spider and the fly (2 of 4) - the little Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) got closer and closer until the waiting Crab SpiderThomisidae) grabbed it. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a22c_5a22-005.jpg
Notes: 11312204 The spider and the fly (3 of 4) - Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with its Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) prey on a Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2005)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Syrphid Flower Fly; Mexican Sunflower

File.Name: 5a22d_5a22-011.jpg
Notes: 11312201 The spider and the fly (4 of 4) - Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with its Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) prey on a Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) flower in the garden. A moment later it dropped the empty husk of the fly to the ground. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2005)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Syrphid Flower Fly; Mexican Sunflower

File.Name: 5a22e_5a22-014.jpg
Notes: 11312196 Little Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) working a new flower of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum). My, what a big proboscis you have! (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a22f_5a22-008.jpg
Notes: 11312189 Tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) working a flower of native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum) flower. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a23a_5a22-023.jpg
Notes: 11320411 Head and caboose of a Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) caterpillar in the garden. It's looking for the right place to make its cacoon and pupate. The caterpillar left the Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed host plant and reappeared on this Sage (Salvia sp.) plant a few feet away. After several hours, it crawled away to different plant. I hope I can find its chrysalis for more photos. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a23b_5a22-034.jpg
Notes: 11320224 Empty Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) chrysalis hanging from a leaf of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. Why does the pupa have that pretty yellow color band? Look at that amazing filagree-web that holds the pupa to the leaf! (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a23c_5a22-027.jpg
Notes: 11320217 Opening seedpod of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a23d_5a22-007.jpg
Notes: 11320212 Our seasons are turning and butterflies are scarce. I found this Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working a flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon. These butterflies and flowers are both running out of time as the seasons turn into winter. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a23e_5a23-035.jpg
Notes: 11320190 Quarter-size Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider in the canyon, with cloud-drops on the web. We were in a cloud today - overcast and windy and drippy all day, not good for photos, but a good chance to catch up with other work. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a23f_5a20-161.jpg
Notes: 11320172 Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens) guarding her egg sack in a seed-head of Creek Clematis (Clematis ligusticifolia). (Santa Ynez Valley, 20 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a29a_5a29-016.jpg
Notes:
11373008 This is some kind of Woolly Inky Cap (Coprinus lagopus or kin) mushroom in the oak mulch along the road. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2005)

We had our first real rain this week. Nights are cold, and the sun is low. It's a shift-change between seasons in the woods. There are still insects and flowers, but today I was looking for mushrooms!

File.Name: 5a29b_5a29-017.jpg
Notes: 11372908 Some kind of Woolly Inky Cap (Coprinus lagopus or kin) mushroom in the oak mulch along the road - doing a curtsy! (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a29c_5a29-019.jpg
Notes: 11372902 Tiny LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" (maybe Mycena?) growing from Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a29d_5a29-013.jpg
Notes: 11372897 Pretty little mushrooms growing from horse apples - maybe Stropharia or Panaeolus? I should know these mushrooms by now, but every year I forget the names and have to start over! (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a29e_5a29-005.jpg
Notes:
11372893 Another pretty little mushroom in the woods that I've seen before and can't quite remember. I should know all these 'shrooms by now, but every year I forget the names and have to start over with the books. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2005)

The woods are already greening up. I think these early sprouts are all annual weeds. The natives usually wait for the next rain. That's a good strategy if the rains fail, but sometimes the annuals get ahead.

File.Name: 5a29f_5a29-010.jpg
Notes: 11372800 Handsome fist-size Bolete mushroom with tan cap, yellow pores that instantly turn blue when bruised or scratched with a twig like this, etc. I reckon this is Boletus "marshii" - "Ben's Bitter Bolete" - according to David Aurora's "Mushrooms Demystified" book. Not poisonous, not edible. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a30a_5a30-032.jpg
Notes: 11381509 Female Anna's Hummingbird working flowers of this pretty non-native Sage (maybe Salvia guaranitica?) in the garden. You can see the stamens of the flower rubbing the bird's forehead - pollination happens. I was lucky to get this close - these hummers are so touchy! (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a30b_5a30-033.jpg
Notes: 11381318 Female Anna's Hummingbird hovering in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a30c_5a30-037.jpg
Notes: 11381296 Welcome to my world... Katydid (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a30d_5a30-042.jpg
Notes: 11381286 Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus - or kin in the Vespidae family) slurping sweet honeydew from the Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on this Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed leaf in the garden. The honey is dripping down from the leaf above - the wasps avoid the aphids. I licked - it is sweet! (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2005)
CommonNames: Golden Paper Wasp; Milkweed Aphids; Butterfly Weed
ScientificName: Polistes fuscatus; Vespidae; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Polistes fuscatus; Vespidae; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 5a30e_5a30-043.jpg
Notes: 11381267 New seeds of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. Baby's leaving home. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a30f_5a29-024.jpg
Notes: 11381261 Ripe berries of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula var. vacillans) in the canyon. I think they taste bitter, but the birds love them. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a31a_5a30-052.jpg
Notes:
11390945 I reckon this big spider is scary enough for Halloween!

It's an Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider in the canyon, the size of a quarter or more. They spin their perfect orb-webs several feet across. They suspend their webs on long strong threads - sometimes between trees or across roads, spanning 20 feet or more. It's very spooky to run into one of these webs at night and wonder: **where**is**the**spider** (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a31b_5a30-054.jpg
Notes: 11390884 Another scary spider for Halloween! Compare the [Next] photo. This is a different quarter-size Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider in the canyon that makes a big perfect orb-web. These spiders all seem to have a scary face pattern on the top of their abdomen. Maybe to scare preditors - it works for me! (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a31c_5a30-055.jpg
Notes: 11390880 This scary Halloween spider is the same quarter-size Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider shown in the [Next] photo. I know, it's not quite sharp - the spider just extended its front legs and started vibrating for a few seconds. Sometimes communication between species is perfect - it was pissed off because I was getting too close! I backed off... quickly. The color bands on its legs are perfect camouflage in the woodland sunbeams - like a zebra. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2005)

File.Name: 5a31d_5a29-002.jpg
Notes: 11390877 Here is a scary Halloween tableau. The unfortunate Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) is just a husk sucked dry by the Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on this Snapdragon (Antirrhinum) flower in the garden. Six hours later, the bee had fallen away and the spider was in the same pose waiting for another victim. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2005)

File.Name: 5b05a_5b05-025.jpg
Notes:
Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working some kind of "Mexican Sage" (Salvia sp.) in the garden. This is the only butterfly I saw today. The weather is crisp and windy, the sun is low... it's definately autumn. The shift is changing. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2005)

This butterfly looks fresh and new. We had a remarkable northward migration of these Painted Lady butterflies last spring. Do they return south like swallows, or are they pioneers hoping to expand their territory?
CommonNames: Painted Lady; Mexican Sage; Painted Lady

File.Name: 5b05b_5b05-028.jpg
Notes: Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly in an intimate embrace with a flower of the prolific hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the garden. This is the only butterfly I saw today. It was crisp and windy today - it's not summer any more. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b05c_5b05-013.jpg
Notes:
Face-to-face with a Katydid (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) on a Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed plant in the garden. It sure looks like this critter has been munching the Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens). I catch crumbs in my beard. I think I recognize those aphid-crumbs smeared on the Katydid's palps! (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2005)

See my photo at
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=11188619
for another unlikely aphid preditor.
ScientificName: Orthoptera; Tettigoniidae; Asclepias tuberosa; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens
associatedTaxa: Orthoptera; Tettigoniidae; Asclepias tuberosa; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens

File.Name: 5b05d_5b05-024.jpg
Notes: Little Inky Cap (Coprinus sp.) mushroom in the woods, catching some color from a naural light tent of California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) leaves in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b05e_5b05-007.jpg
Notes: Pretty little white mushrooms in the woods - I guess Lepiota with free gills and a distinct ring on the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b05f_5b05-009.jpg
Notes: Tiny white mushrooms growing from a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) twig - I moved it to look under the skirts. There are so many different kinds of little white and brown mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b06a_5b06-041.jpg
Notes: Here's an action shot, but you have to look close. The female Anna's Hummingbird in the lower-left is chasing away the Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly in the upper-right. The flowers are from the prolific hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the garden. Several male and female hummers were fighting all day for the territory of this shrub - I can hear them bash together in the air! This female is holding her own against the males. Maybe it is the redirected bloodlust of the fight that made her chase this butterfly rather than the other hummingbirds. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2005)
CommonNames: Anna's Hummingbird; Painted Lady; Waverly Sage

File.Name: 5b06b_5b06-046.jpg
Notes: I was sitting on the porch reading lab books - teacher stuff - when I noticed this pair of tiny flies (order Diptera, suborder Nematocera) "doing it" on the white paper on top of my clipboard. I've been seeing clouds of these little flies dancing in the sunbeams since our first rain. Identifying any critter this small is hard enough - but look how different the male and female are! (I guess that's the female on the right - bigger abdomen full of eggs.) (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b06c_5b06-044.jpg
Notes:
Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) chrysalis hanging from a Chrysanthemum in the garden. This is the vessel for the astounding transformation within of caterpillar to butterfly. The yellow band looks like a metallic girdle. I see a hint of wings forming in the lower half. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2005)

The caterpillar crawled a few feet away from the honey bee where it fed. I have more photos in the archive.

File.Name: 5b06d_5b06-038.jpg
Notes: Female Anna's Hummingbird working flowers of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b06e_5b06-034.jpg
Notes: This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) is trapped by a flower of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. It managed to pull away free. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b06f_5b05-020.jpg
Notes: Tiny LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" (maybe Mycena?) - pushing up a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf to make a "mushrump" in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b12a_5b12-002.jpg
Notes: Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) in the woods. We had almost three inches of rain this week. That's a big deal here in central California with our summer-dry Mediterranean climate - the last real rain was in April or May. The seasons really have changed, and there's a new shift starting to work in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b12b_5b12-001.jpg
Notes:
Female Anna's Hummingbird perching in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) with an eye on the garden. Note the yellow pollen on her bill - she's really flying mother nature's seed to a new home in the sun. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2005)

I just bought a Tamron SP AF 200~500mm F/5-6.3 Di LD [IF] [BFD!] telephoto lens for my Nikon D70. This is my first photo with the new lens. It works. Looking for birds and mushrooms sure is different!

File.Name: 5b12c_5b12-021.jpg
Notes: Little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed leaf in the garden. The orange color in the background is a mass of Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens) that managed to hang on during the rain this week. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2005)
CommonNames: Jumping Spider; Butterfly Weed; Milkweed Aphids
ScientificName: Salticidae; Asclepias tuberosa; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Asclepias tuberosa; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens

File.Name: 5b12d_5b12-006.jpg
Notes:
New trees in the woods. Maybe edible, but tough, and some people are sensitive to them. Edible, schmedible - these little 'shrooms are beautiful, and a sign that the winter mushroom season is under way! (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2005)

Last year we had near-record rainfall, but few mushrooms. I hope they're all just waiting...

File.Name: 5b12e_5b12-022.jpg
Notes: Pretty little LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - (maybe white-spored Mycena?) - pushing up a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf in the woods. I've been seeing many tiny ephemeral mushrooms in the woods after the good rain this week. Here today, gone tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b12f_5b12-012.jpg
Notes: Tiny dark-stemmed "Horsehair Fungi" (Marasmius androsaceus or kin) mushrooms sprouting from a single fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf in the woods. These little 'shrooms have a connection with lichens - they have a remarkable ability to dry out and then revive after new rain. This little mushroom does not smell like garlic. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b13a_5a27-078.jpg
Notes:
My after-school Hike Club kids inside a secret cave in the Santa Barbara mountains. Look close to see the real pipestem stalactites hanging from the ceiling in the upper right. This cave is big enough to walk around in - and crawl through to another opening. An ancient Chumash village site is nearby. It`s a secret place, so don`t bother asking. Find your own secret places! (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2005)

I'm preoccupied with school stuff today. These photos are from two weeks ago.

File.Name: 5b13b_5a27-087.jpg
Notes: My after-school Hike Club kids at the mouth of a secret cave in the Santa Barbara mountains. The cave is big enough to walk around in and crawl through to another narrow opening. The rock is a massive exposure of sandstone much higher than the photo shows. Water slowly seeping through the massive rock picks up enough calcium to form real pipestem stalactites. I believe the ferns on the upper lip of the cave are California Maiden-Hair Ferns (Adiantum jordanii). (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2005)

File.Name: 5b13c_5a27-093.jpg
Notes: I believe this fern is California Maiden-Hair (Adiantum jordanii). It's growing from the upper lip of the cave in the [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2005)

File.Name: 5b13d_5a27-061.jpg
Notes: Real pipestem stalactites in a secret cave in the Santa Barbara mountains. Each one holds a drop of calcium-rich water that leached through the massive sandstone outcrop above. The entire cave ceiling is lumpy with calcite deposits. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2005)

File.Name: 5b13e_5a27-097.jpg
Notes: Inside the secret cave in the Santa Barbara mountains - real pipestem stalactites! This is what "sense of wonder" means. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2005)

File.Name: 5b13f_5a27-092.jpg
Notes: It's always bothered me that there is graffiti inside our secret cave in the Santa Barbara mountains, with dates back to the 1950s. I always assumed it was spray paint, but I just noticed that all the graffiti is the same dark-green color. Now I realize the words are just scratched on the rock wall, and the green color is a living green algae that flourishes where the calcium deposit on the sandstone is scratched away. I'm still bothered - but I learned something today! (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2005)

File.Name: 5b17a_5b17-051.jpg
Notes:
This little Common Checkered-Skipper (Pyrgus communis) butterfly took off from the Italian Thistle (Carduus pycnocephalus) flower just as I took the photo - like an angel. Compare the [Previous] photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 November 2005)

We have long days of parent-student-teacher conferences at school all week. It was good to get out with my camera into Julie's garden and restoration area during a break.

File.Name: 5b17b_5b17-050.jpg
Notes: Common Checkered-Skipper (Pyrgus communis) butterfly working a flower head of Italian Thistle (Carduus pycnocephalus). Compare the [Next] photo. This thistle is a bad weed, but it supports life. (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b17c_5b17-047.jpg
Notes: The bright orange underwings and four eyespots on the hind wing peg this as a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly. This late lady - with antennae akimbo - is working a composite Zinnia flower in Julie's garden at school. (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b17d_5b13-031.jpg
Notes:
Fully charged male spider with swollen dark spermaphores on the palps on his head. I didn't see a female nearby, so maybe he's just waiting on his "sperm web" for a mate. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2005)

Spider love is complicated. I have a series of photos and more info on spider love at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=9242198
and [Previous] from that.

File.Name: 5b17e_5b17-044.jpg
Notes: Large "oak apple" gall on Valley Oak (Quercus lobata). I believe this gall is caused by the tiny California Gall Wasp (Andricus californicus), a species of tiny wasps known only from parthenogenic females - apparently no males are known for this species. The round holes are probably "exit holes" for the new wasps that matured within. Adults emerge and oviposit in the fall. They are starting new galls for their offspring right now. What a life! (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b17f_5b17-045.jpg
Notes: Little muffin-like galls of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) or kin on a twig of a sapling Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) tree. The galls release a sweet nectar that attracts ants. I didn't see any ants today, but I do see a dusty fungus growing on the caps. (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 November 2005)
CommonNames: Clasping Twig Gall Wasp; Valley Oak

File.Name: 5b19a_5b19-017.jpg
Notes:
Ripe dandelion-like seeds of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the canyon. The seeds are everywhere. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2005)

School conferences are done. Julie and I are heading to Santa Cruz Island early tomorrow for a few days of weed-clearing and restoration work. I'll be back mid-week, hopefully with many photos from this remarkable island.

kt23 - the black background in my photos is natural. Look for your subject in a shaft of sunlight with dark shadow behind. I have no choice at home on the north shady side of the mountains! Focus close and use center-weighted metering or cut the exposure a few stops to keep the background dark.

File.Name: 5b19b_5b19-016.jpg
Notes: Little spider on a seed head of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the canyon. Judging by the long front legs and the sepulchral "face" on its abdomen, I guess this is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae). The stumper is that these spiders usually wait on flowers for their prey. Insects don't visit these ripe seed heads, so what is the spider waiting for? Notice the swollen palps on the spider's head. I guess this is a mating pose. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b19c_5b19-024.jpg
Notes: Tiny Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b19d_5b19-028.jpg
Notes: Tiny Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider perched on her perfect CD-size web among the blackberrys in the woods, caught in the backlight. What fine geometry. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b19e_5b19-022.jpg
Notes:
Large Boletus satanas mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. This is Satan's Bolete - poisonous - but easy to recognize with pink cap and reddish pores, net-like "reticulate" markings on the yellow stem, and large bulbous base. It has a "DON'T EAT ME* attitude. All Bolete mushrooms have sponge-like pores underneath instead of gills. Learn this bad one and nibble all the others - in small doses of course - and watch out for funky worms and mold as they age! (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2005)

There is a very good guide to "The Boletes of California" by Harry Thiers available on the Web at:
   http://www.mykoweb.com/boletes/

File.Name: 5b19f_5b19-007.jpg
Notes: I guess this is a Ringed Panaeolus (Panaeolus semiovatus aka P. separatus) mushroom growing on horse dung in the canyon, with lichens and native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens). Last week we had almost 3 inches of rain, but this week has been hot and dry. The first flush of mushrooms has disappeared, and early seedlings are hanging on. Autumn is our changing time. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2005)
ScientificName: Panaeolus semiovatus; P. separatus; Agrostis pallens
associatedTaxa: Panaeolus semiovatus; P. separatus; Agrostis pallens

File.Name: 5b24a_5b21-110.jpg
Notes:
I reckon ripe fruit are a good image for our national Thanksgiving harvest festival today. Here are ripe fruit of Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood) aka Christmas Berry (Heteromeles arbutifolia). These berries are fruit of a native shrub in Pozo Canyon in the remote southwest corner of Santa Cruz Island. The island Toyon berries seem bigger and redder and more succulent than Toyon berries back home in the canyon. *Sigh* I guess that's how remote islands should always be. (Santa Cruz Island, 21 November 2005)

Julie and I spent the last several days doing volunteer restoration work on Santa Cruz Island, the largest of the Santa Barbara Channel Islands. Thanks Ken! Check out:
   http://www.channelislandsrestoration.com/
   http://www.nps.gov/chis/

File.Name: 5b24b_5b21-070.jpg
Notes:
View along the Ridge Road on Santa Cruz Island - looking west towards Sierra Blanca Peak across the upper end of Laguna Canyon. In the distance you can see Santa Rosa Island. We're heading past the White Mountain to do restoration work in Pozo and Johnson and Sauces canyons beyond. This steep mountain landscape supports a unique flora of shrubs and trees mostly found nowhere else, but closely related to our flora at home 25+ miles across the channel. The orange soil is weathered Santa Cruz Island shist. (Santa Cruz Island, 21 November 2005)

We had remarkably clear weather on our trip thanks to offshore winds. At one time on the Ridge Road we saw (left to right) the Santa Monica Mountains, Palos Verdes, Santa Catalina, San Clemente, Santa Barbara, San Nicholas, Santa Rosa, and San Miguel Islands. The only island we couldn't see was the closest - Anacapa. Next trip I want to climb Pichaco del Diablo (2470 feet) - the high point of the island - and see everything!
Locality: left to right) the Santa Monica Mountains

File.Name: 5b24c_5b22-183.jpg
Notes: I first thought these are new flowers of Santa Cruz Island Manzanita (Arctostaphylos insularis) - now I'm totally confused! This shrub grows from a burl, though other manzanitas with no burl are nearby. I guess this is Arctostaphylos tomentosa - maybe Island Manzanita (A. tomentosa ssp. insulicola). The pretty flowers are pinker then neighbor plants. (Santa Cruz Island, 22 November 2005)
CommonNames: Santa Cruz Island Manzanita; Island Manzanita
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos insularis; Arctostaphylos tomentosa; A. tomentosa; insulicola
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos insularis; Arctostaphylos tomentosa; A. tomentosa; insulicola

File.Name: 5b24d_5b22-217.jpg
Notes: The pretty red flowers suggest that this endemic Island Buckwheat (Eriogonum grande) is really Red Buckwheat (E. grande var. rubescens). It is growing on the grassy hills above Canada de los Sauces on the west end of Santa Cruz Island. (Santa Cruz Island, 22 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b24e_5b22-177.jpg
Notes:
New cones of Bishop Pine (Pinus muricata). There's a remarkable forest of these small pine trees along the ridge road on the west end above the Christy Ranch. There are also stands of these pines in the hills between Lompoc and Orcutt on the mainland. Trees with nearly symmetrical cones attached at right angles to the branch (like these) are known as Santa Cruz Island Pine (Pinus remorata) - but the populations are mixed, and the names are now in synonomy. (Santa Cruz Island, 22 November 2005)

These closed-cone pinecones normally remain closed and only open to release seeds after the heat of a fire. That's a stumper in the Channel Islands which are not a fire regime like the mainland.
CommonNames: Bishop Pine; Santa Cruz Island Pine

File.Name: 5b24f_5b22-232.jpg
Notes: Looking back at Santa Cruz Island from the beach at Forney's Cove on the west end near Fraser Point. We've been doing restoration work this week. It's like going back in time - except for the arundo and fennel and ice plant and star thistle and all the other invasive weeds... (Santa Cruz Island, 22 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b25a_5b22-167.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful fern-like leaflets of Santa Cruz Island Ironwood (Lyonothamnus floribundus ssp. aspleniifolius) growing along the Ridge Road. Another form of this island endemic with entire leaflets grows on Catalina Island. It is only known from the fossil record and recent transplants on the mainland. (Santa Cruz Island, 22 November 2005)

Today's photos are all endemic species found only on Santa Cruz Island and maybe the other Channel Islands. Julie and I spent the last several days doing volunteer restoration work on Santa Cruz Island, the largest of the Santa Barbara Channel Islands. Thanks Ken!

File.Name: 5b25b_5b23-244.jpg
Notes: Ripe berries of endemic Island Redberry (Rhamnus pirifolia). This shrub is growing along the road in the Central Valley of Santa Cruz Island - pretty, but dusty. (Santa Cruz Island, 23 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b25c_5b21-121.jpg
Notes: Pretty pea-flowers of Island Deerweed (Lotus dendroideus). This island endemic is more shrub-like than our similar Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) at home across the channel. (Santa Cruz Island, 21 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b25d_5b22-169.jpg
Notes: Woolly flower heads of endemic Northern Island Hazardia (Hazardia detonsa) growing along the Ridge Road, though I also found it growing near the beaches. Most flowers are done. It's an island relative of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the mountains back home on the other side of the channel. (Santa Cruz Island, 22 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b25e_5b22-184.jpg
Notes: Flower head of the island endemic shrub Santa Cruz Island Buckwheat (Eriogonum arborescens) growing along the Ridge Road. This proves the saying that "rare" really means rarely common. I found it here and there, but only a few plants still had flowers like this. (Santa Cruz Island, 22 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b25f_5b23-264.jpg
Notes: Strange Dudleya succulent growing from a mossy rock in Canada del Puerto on Santa Cruz Island. I guess this is the "Candleholder Dudleya" (Dudleya candelabrum) because of the fat unbranched stem, but compare D. greenei. Either way, this succulent is an island endemic found nowhere else. (Santa Cruz Island, 23 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b26a_5b23-282.jpg
Notes:
Elegant flower of Island Morning-glory (Calystegia macrostegia ssp. macrostegia) growing in Canada del Puerto near Prisoners' Harbor on Santa Cruz Island. We have a different subspecies (C. macrostegia ssp. cyclostegia) with smaller bracts and keel below the flowers back home on the mainland side of the Santa Barbara channel. (Santa Cruz Island, 23 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b26b_5b20-049.jpg
Notes: Beautiful Santa Cruz Island Silver Lotus (Lotus argophyllus var. niveus) growing on the dry sandy wash in the Central Valley of Santa Cruz Island. There is a mainland form in the Santa Barbara backcountry with flowers on stalks rather than crowded like this. (Santa Cruz Island, 20 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b26c_5b20-055.jpg
Notes: I believe these are new flowers of the endemic Santa Cruz Island Manzanita (Arctostaphylos insularis). Note the scale-like bracts and glandular pedicels below the flowers. This is a tall shrub without a burl, growing along the Ridge Road above the south side of the island. It's confusing because the lower Island Manzanita (A. tomentosa ssp. insulicola) with a burl is growing nearby. I might have these mixed up. Sure is pretty - whatever it is! (Santa Cruz Island, 20 November 2005)
CommonNames: Santa Cruz Island Manzanita; Island Manzanita

File.Name: 5b26d_5b23-294.jpg
Notes: Pretty yellow pea-flowers of Island Deerweed (Lotus dendroideus var. dendroideus) in the dry wash of Canada del Puerto on Santa Cruz Island. This island endemic is woodier than our similar Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) at home across the channel, and it keeps its leaves. (Santa Cruz Island, 23 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b26e_5b22-175.jpg
Notes:
Early flowers of Island Big-pod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus ssp. insularis). The species is also common on the south-facing slopes of the Santa Ynez Mountains back home across the channel, but mainland shrubs have alternate leaves while the island shrubs have opposite leaves that are clearly visible in this photo. These are the differences that keep local botany so interesting. (Santa Cruz Island, 22 November 2005)

It's not quite flowering time in central California. These flowers are early and other flowers I've managed to photograph are late. The island - like the mainland - is not covered with flowers as we approach winter, but there are surprises if you look close!

File.Name: 5b26f_5b21-114.jpg
Notes: Pretty flowers of Island Paintbrush (Castelleya lanata ssp. hololeuca) growing in Pozo Canyon near the beach in the south-west corner of the island. This furry white perennial is almost a shrub. It's odd that the species (C. lanata ssp. lanata) occurs only in Texas and Northern Mexico - I expect a revision! (Santa Cruz Island, 21 November 2005)
ScientificName: Castelleya lanata; hololeuca; C. lanata; lanata
associatedTaxa: Castelleya lanata; hololeuca; C. lanata; lanata

File.Name: 5b27a_5b27-011.jpg
Notes:
Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing around the base of an old Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) stump. I used flash, though I prefer natural light - these 'shrooms are growing in a place where the sun never shines at this time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2005)

We had a sprinkle of rain a few days ago, but it's been cold and very windy since then. The woods are dry. I was surprised to find mushrooms today - including a Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius)!
CommonNames: Honey Mushrooms; Coast Live Oak; Chanterelle
ScientificName: Armillariella mellea; Quercus agrifolia; Cantharellus cibarius
associatedTaxa: Armillariella mellea; Quercus agrifolia; Cantharellus cibarius

File.Name: 5b27b_5b27-016.jpg
Notes: Small part of a huge clump of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing around the base of an old Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) stump in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b27c_5b27-014.jpg
Notes: More Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing around the base of an old Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) stump in the woods, with California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) leaves in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2005)
CommonNames: Honey Mushrooms; Coast Live Oak; California Blackberry
ScientificName: Armillariella mellea; Quercus agrifolia; Rubus ursinus
