File.Name: 5b27d_5b27-017.jpg
Notes: Blewitt (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom growing from Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the woods. These edible mushrooms are blue when fresh, but they loose their color as they dry out. They always have a "blewit-look" that you learn to recognize. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b27e_5b27-019.jpg
Notes: I thought this was another edible blue-capped Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom growing under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. (See [Next] photo.) But look closer - you can see the rusty-orange-colored spore deposit on the stem, a sure sign that this is a Cortinarius mushroom. Probably not edible, though there are MANY species and I've only nibbled a few. I think of these as winter mushrooms. I'm surprised to find this now in such dry weather. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2005)

File.Name: 5b27f_5b25-301.jpg
Notes: Perfect little Inky Cap (Coprinus sp.) mushrooms in the garden. A day later they melted away. (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2005)

File.Name: 5c01a-5c01-072.jpg
Notes: The train trestle over the mouth of the Santa Ynez River, with reflections. It's foggy and still at dusk, just beginning to drizzle as a new storm approaches. (Surf Beach, 1 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c03a-5c03-027.jpg
Notes: A fractal diadem of raindrops on a new leaf of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the woods. We had some rain yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c03b-5c03-020.jpg
Notes:
New flowers of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the canyon. The flowers remind me of those old-time Easter sugar eggs that you peer inside to see something wonderful. (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2005)

Did you notice the tiny red-colored critters inside the top flower? I didn't notice when I laid on my back looking up to capture the photo. Now I wonder what they are.

File.Name: 5c03c-5c03-007.jpg
Notes:
Edible and delicious Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom in the canyon. It has round ridges underneath instead of sharp-edged gills like most mushrooms, easy to recognize once you've seen it. (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2005)

I found my first Chanterelle of the year last weekend, but I had to dig down an inch under a suspicious "mushrump" pile of leaves under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods to get it. Today I found a basketful. Native Chanterelle mushrooms have become a local market and restaurant commodity. Real money is involved, and commercial collectors fight over their territories. I don't approve. These delicious wild mushrooms are one time that us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings.
Locality: Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods to get it. Today I found a basketful. Native Chanterelle mushrooms have become a local market and restaurant commodity. Real money is involved
CommonNames: Chanterelle; Chanterelle; Coast Live Oak; Chanterelle

File.Name: 5c03d-5c03-006.jpg
Notes: Here's what the delicious orange-color Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms look like coming up under the oak leaves in the woods. It's so easy to walk right past without noticing! (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c03e-5c03-015.jpg
Notes: New Milky Cap mushroom in the woods, probably Yellow-Staining Milky Cap (Lactarius chrysorheus or kin). It's still holding raindrops from yesterday's storm. (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c03f-5c03-045.jpg
Notes: Fist-sized Bolete mushroom in the woods with big yellow pores and no red on the stem - maybe the Boring Brown Bolete (Boletus subtomentosus), as David Arora calls it in his fine book "Mushrooms Demystified". Maybe edible, but soggy and bland at best. It's pretty though, coming up from the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2005)
CommonNames: Bolete; Boring Brown Bolete; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 5c04a-5c04-054.jpg
Notes: This LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - is white, growing from a mass of green moss in the woods. The gills have a brownish look, but the edges are white with microscopic cystidia cells. I don't know what this beauty is, or those black dots. That's cool. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c04b-5c04-066.jpg
Notes:
Looking under the skirts of a Cortinarius mushroom - maybe Cortinarius fulmineus or kin in the "Bulbopodium" group - entirely yellow/orange, with a slime veil as well as a cortina and a bulbous base. The cap is slimy, but the stem is not. A group of these was growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2005)

Store-bought Agaricus mushrooms have a membranous skin or "veil" that covers the gills before they open. These Cortinarius mushrooms have this "cortina" veil of cobwebby hairs that cover and protect the gills. The cortina hairs fall away from the cap and catch the falling rusty-brown spores. As the mushrooms mature, you can still recognize the spore-stained hairs on the stem. This one also has a "slime veil" - a thick layer of slime over the cap and cortina that quickly dries up. It's barely visible here as an orange crust at the base of the gills, like a dried out tissue.

There are dozens or hundreds or maybe gazillions of Cortinarius species of mushrooms in our woods. The old ones all look the same, but I love the fresh ones for their color and character. Edibility unknown.
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Cortinarius fulmineus; Bulbopodium; Quercus agrifolia; Agaricus; Cortinarius; cortina; Cortinarius
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Cortinarius fulmineus; Bulbopodium; Quercus agrifolia; Agaricus; Cortinarius; cortina; Cortinarius

File.Name: 5c04c-5c04-070.jpg
Notes: Mature and young Cortinarius fulmineus or kin mushrooms in the "Bulbopodium" group. They have a bulbous base. You can still see a bit of the "cortina" veil of cobwebby hairs that cover and protect the new gills of the little one. All that's left on the big stalk are a few hairs stained with rusty-brown spores. That's the key for identifying these mushrooms in the woods. See my [Next] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c04d-5c04-079.jpg
Notes: New Entoloma rhodopolium mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). This large poisonous mushroom has "pink" or flesh-colored spores and a distinct chlorine smell - like a swimming pool. Pretty though. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c04e-5c04-083.jpg
Notes: I broke open the stem of the Entoloma rhodopolium mushroom in the [Next] photo and found these tiny Springtail (order Collembia) insects devouring it. Gotta be quick - these cute critters are 1 mm long or less, and they hop away with any light. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c04f-5c04-075.jpg
Notes:
Fly maggots - larvae of some fly in the order Diptera, lots of them! - devouring Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing around the base of an old Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) stump. I wonder what kind of flies these are? (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2005)

I have photos of the original mushrooms at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=11651650
and [Previous] to that. What is it about mushrooms and meat?

File.Name: 5c10a-5c10-045.jpg
Notes:
If you collect any wild mushrooms for the table, then learn this one first! Here is a Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushroom under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. This mushroom is deadly poisonous - one of the most toxic things in nature. It's particularly dangerous because the symptoms don't show up for 12 or more hours after eating, when there's not much left to do about it. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2005)

The cap is greenish or yellowish or almost white, sticky when fresh but smooth and lustrous when it dries; it has white spores and white gills and a white "partial veil" or "skirt" on the stem; the stalk comes from a white sack or "volva" at the base; and the edge of the cap is not usually "striate" or marked with lines. That's pretty definite, but all mushrooms become vague with age, and if you pluck a 'shroom rather than lift it up from below, you might miss the "death cup" volva at the base. The rumor is that it tastes very good. It has been confused with edible Agaricus and Lepiota and Volvariella mushrooms with fatal results.

I found a dozen of these handsome 'shrooms in the woods today - beautiful but deadly. There's no reason to confuse them with anything else if you're careful. It is a thrill to hold death in your hand - but we do it every time we drive cars or cut veggies. Just be careful!
ScientificName: Amanita phalloides; Quercus agrifolia; Agaricus; Lepiota; Volvariella
associatedTaxa: Amanita phalloides; Quercus agrifolia; Agaricus; Lepiota; Volvariella

File.Name: 5c10b-5c10-049.jpg
Notes:
A bouquet of deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. Notice how the mushrooms change with age, so the color, ring, and cup become less distinct. See my [Next] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2005)

It's interesting that these deadly mushrooms might be introduced from Europe. I didn't find any last year, but here they are.

File.Name: 5c10c-5c10-020.jpg
Notes: Rodents have been nibbling on this Red-Cracked Bolete (Boletus chrysenteron or kin) mushroom under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. (There's no red cracks on the cap of this 'shroom, but there are on similar mushrooms nearby, and it does have red on the stalk.) Don't assume that a mushroom is edible just because critters nibble it - Wood Rats make mistakes too! This one probably is edible, but not very good. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2005)
CommonNames: Red-Cracked Bolete; Coast Live Oak; Wood Rats

File.Name: 5c10d-5c10-022.jpg
Notes:
Another Cortinarius mushroom in the woods. This one has a violet cap and it's not particularly slimy. You can see the cinnamon-colored spores collected on the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia)leaf in the foreground and on the hairs of the "cortina" left on the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2005)

There are so many different kinds of Cortinarius mushrooms in the woods - edible and not - I never think of collecting them for the table. Pretty though!

File.Name: 5c10e-5c10-012.jpg
Notes: A cascade of white spores falling down a cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in the garden - like natural spore prints. These are edible wild mushroom in the woods that grow from dead wood, but they are also known as Oak Root Fungus

File.Name: 5c10f-5c10-007.jpg
Notes:
The huge cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) has become a liquid mass of wriggling fly maggots - larvae of some fly in the order Diptera. I wonder what kind of flies these are? (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2005)

I have more photos of the original mushrooms at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?photo_id=11722301
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=11651650

File.Name: 5c11a_5c10-002.jpg
Notes:
The weather has been cold, with some frost, and insects are scarce. I've seen a few of these "Stink Bugs" (order Hemiptera, maybe family Pentatomidae). This one is trying to warm up on a Salvia leaf in the garden. Pretty antennae. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2005)

Today's photos are all critters rather than mushrooms. I'm busy today, and it's been cloudy with a few raindrops, so I dipped into my archives.

File.Name: 5c11b_5c10-019.jpg
Notes: I've been watching this Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) chrysalis in the garden since November 6. (See http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=11445938) That's too much time, but maybe the recent cold weather has slowed it down? The coccoon is very dark and still, but there's still a hint of something inside. I'll keep checking. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c11c_5b22-221.jpg
Notes: Despite the copper color, I believe this is a Western Pygmy Blue (Brephidium exile) butterfly resting on a plant stem near the mouth of Canada de los Sauces on the west-end of Santa Cruz Island last month. I also saw Painted Ladies and Monarchs, though butterflies were scarce. (Santa Cruz Island, 22 November 2005)
CommonNames: Western Pygmy Blue; Painted Ladies; Monarchs

File.Name: 5c11d-5c10-016.jpg
Notes: Pelvis and a bit of the tail vertebrae of a dead Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c11e-5b13-025.jpg
Notes: Little Wrentit (Chamaea fasciata) in the woods. These perky little birds freely fly through the brambles and other understory shrubs that I can barely push through. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c11f_5b23-290.jpg
Notes: Very dead California Sea Lion (Zalophus californianus) on the beach at Prisoners' Harbor on Santa Cruz Island. I was standing upwind. The canine-like teeth show why they are sometimes called Sea Dogs. You can see the little external ear in the upper left corner that helps separate sea lions from seals. (Santa Cruz Island, 23 November 2005)

File.Name: 5c12a_5b20-069.jpg
Notes:
A little forest of different lichens growing on a branch of Island Scrub Oak (Quercus pacifica) along the South Ridge Road on Santa Cruz Island. (Santa Cruz Island, 20 November 2005)

Lichens are little self-sufficient communities of algae and fungi that grow as one in a mutually beneficial biological marriage known as symbiosis. The bright red caps look like little Ascomycete Cup Fungi - probably the fungus partner making a few spores on its own without its algal partner. Why do some kinds of trees and shrubs always support more lichens than others? I wonder if the shrub is a third partner in the marriage?

File.Name: 5c12b_5b22-226.jpg
Notes: A "shrubby" Fruticose Lichen growing on a mysterious old stone wall near the west end of Santa Cruz Island. See my [Next] photo for more info. (Santa Cruz Island, 22 November 2005)

File.Name: 5c13a_5b21-127.jpg
Notes:
This is not the "goosenecks" in the high desert rimrock country. It's just the little fresh water creek at the bottom of Pozo Canyon on the south-west corner of Santa Cruz Island, carving a fractal path through the sand and on to the beach. I can almost see excited micro-river rafters heading downstream! (Santa Cruz Island, 21 November 2005)

I like the new Fotolog archive system that's organized like a calendar rather than a daisy chain. But my photo-calendar looks pretty sparse when I'm busy with school and only get out in the woods with my camera on weekends. I'll try harder to post something every day. It's a chance to show more good stuff from my own (recent) archives.

File.Name: 5c13b_5b22-211.jpg
Notes: New invaders of Giant Reed (Arundo donax) trying to start a new colony on the beach at the mouth of Canada de los Sauces on the west-end of Santa Cruz Island last month. Rootstocks and branches of this invasive bamboo-like grass wash out to sea during winter storms and float across the Santa Barbara Channel. Note the mass of stems washed up on the beach behind the green growth. Stopping this aggressive weed will take constant vigilance. At least we stopped this colony from spreading. (Santa Cruz Island, 21 November 2005)

File.Name: 5c13c_5b21-119.jpg
Notes: Greg and Janet attack a new colony of invasive Tamarisk (Tamarix ramosissima) in Pozo Canyon on the south-west corner of Santa Cruz Island. It's scary how this aggresive weed has taken over miles of river bank along southwest rivers like the Green River in Utah. Tamarisk was first collected on Santa Cruz Island in 1977, and there's still a chance to stop it. It's not enough just to cut it down and kill it. First you have to cut and bag the flowers and seeds like this. (Santa Cruz Island, 21 November 2005)

File.Name: 5c13d_5b22-219.jpg
Notes: We ate lunch near the mouth of Canada de los Sauces on the west-end of Santa Cruz Island - then I noticed it was an ancient Chumash Native American midden exposed by erosion. I see shells, shiny chert rock, and even a bit of marine mammel bone in the upper right corner. We picked up our refuse, but I'm glad they didn't! It's a glimpse into another time. (Santa Cruz Island, 21 November 2005)

File.Name: 5c13e_5b22-233.jpg
Notes: Looking south at the submerged end of Fraser Point and Forney's Cove on the west end of Santa Cruz Island. Waves are coming at this headland from both sides. The water was so clear... I want to go snorkling here! (Santa Cruz Island, 22 November 2005)

File.Name: 5c13f_5b20-064.jpg
Notes: More of the lichen micro-world on a branch of Island Scrub Oak (Quercus pacifica) along the South Ridge Road on Santa Cruz Island. See my [Previous] photo for more info. (Santa Cruz Island, 20 November 2005)

File.Name: 5c14a_5b23-293.jpg
Notes: Little spider on a flower cluster of Sea Rocket (Cackile maritima) on the beach at Prisoners' Harbor on Santa Cruz Island. This plant in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family is common on most Channel Islands and mainland. Islands are so interesting for what they have and what they don't have. My flora says this plant was first collected on the island in 1958. I wonder if the progenitor of this spider "ballooned" across the channel hanging by a thread during a fierce offshore Santa Ana Wind? (Santa Cruz Island, 23 November 2005)

File.Name: 5c14b_5b22-207.jpg
Notes: Pretty flowers of Sticky Sand-Verbena near the beach at the mouth of Canada de los Sauces. It's a common beach plant at home and on the Channel Islands. (Santa Cruz Island, 22 November 2005)

File.Name: 5c14c_5b23-268.jpg
Notes: Pretty California Fuschia (Epilobium canum) flowers. These late-season natives are the most important Hummingbird flowers on the mainland and the Channel Islands at this dry time of year. (Santa Cruz Island, 22 November 2005)

File.Name: 5c14d_5b21-114.jpg
Notes: Odd flowers of Island Paintbrush (Castilleja lanata ssp. hololeuca) growing along the creek in Pozo Canyon on the south-west corner of Santa Cruz Island. It's odd that this endemic almost-shrub is considered a subspecies of a plant that only grows in northern Mexico and Texas. Go figure! (Santa Cruz Island, 21 November 2005)

File.Name: 5c14e_5b20-067.jpg
Notes: Perfect green acorn of Island Scrub Oak (Quercus pacifica) in the central valley of Santa Cruz Island. But notice the tiny undeveloped acorns to the right. According to my flora, these shrubs develop their acorns in just one year. So what are those tiny acorn-lets? I don't see these at home on the mainland. (Santa Cruz Island, 20 November 2005)

File.Name: 5c14f_5b23-286.jpg
Notes: The rocky beach at Prisoners' Harbor on Santa Cruz Island faces the mainland across the channel, so it's protected from the full force of Pacific Ocean storms. The hard rock is igneous rather than the more friable sandstone and shale on the mainland. It's not a beach you want to lay on, but it looks like paradise to me! (Santa Cruz Island, 23 November 2005)

File.Name: 5c15a_5c03-050.jpg
Notes:
Odd little Stalked Puffball (Tulostoma brumale or kin?) mushrooms on the edge of our garden. It's a puffball on a stalk with a nipple-like opening on top to release rusty-orange spores. Note the cap has two layers. The rough outer cap (or peridium) is peeling away showing its white underside and the smooth inner cap. I sometimes find related species in the desert. (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2005)

Today's photos are all interesting Puffball (order Lycoperdales) mushrooms. You can see what they have in common, and the differences are interesting.
Locality: order Lycoperdales) mushrooms. You can see what they have in common

File.Name: 5c15b_5c10-036.jpg
Notes: Pretty little walnut-size Puffball mushrooms in the new grass along our road. They have a "slight" sterile base and they definately release spores from an "apical pore" - the small opening on top - so I reckon they are in the genus Lycoperdon, maybe the Dark Puffball (Lycoperdon foetidum). They remind me of little vacuum cleaner bags! (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c15c_5c03-043.jpg
Notes: Strange Earthstar (Astreus hygrometricus or kin) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. It's an odd kind of Puffball (order Lycoperdales) fungus with a tough outer layer that splits into the star and a thin inner sack that holds the spore mass and soon breaks apart and disappears. I rarely find the inner skin intact like this. You can see that it releases spores through an "apical pore" like the puffballs in the [Next] two photos. The tough outer star-shaped rinds persist for months, like washed-up shells on the beach. (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c16a_5c16-246.jpg
Notes: 11850979 I found this Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus - or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera) resting on a furry Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata) leaf in the garden. It wasn't moving much in the chilly morning air, but it still looked me in the eye with a nasty scowl. I got this head-on photo at 1/15 second @ f/11. We're just a week away from the Winter Solstice when the sun is low and days are short. We don't get much light in our north-facing canyon at this time of year. I reckon my white balance is off in this photo in the winter shade - too much blue, but perfect. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2005)
ScientificName: Polistes fuscatus; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Stachys lanata
associatedTaxa: Polistes fuscatus; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Stachys lanata

File.Name: 5c16b_5c16-247.jpg
Notes: White-trimmed gills of brown-spored Poison Pie (Hebeloma crustuliniforme or kin) mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. As the common name suggests, it is not edible. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c16c_5c16-248.jpg
Notes: I guess this dark-spored fragile mushroom is Psathyrella longipes or kin under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. There are white cottony veil remnants on the edge of the cap. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c16d_5c16-259.jpg
Notes: This little mushroom in the woods has an almost black cap. The gills are free (not attached) and white. I don't know what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c16e_5c10-026.jpg
Notes: I noticed these tiny mushrooms when I turned over a piece of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) bark in the woods. There's an oak leaf on the left for scale - the spines are less than 1 mm. The pink color of the mushroom caps suggests Mycena acicula or kin. You can see the white fungus mycelium at the base. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c16f_5c16-263.jpg
Notes: A new cluster of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing at the base of a standing dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon. They are just pinhead to pencil eraser size now, but they will expand greatly when we get some rain. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c17a_5c17-012.jpg
Notes:
It's gloomy with a chance of rain, and the sun is below the trees almost all day at this time of year just a week from the Winter Solstice in our north-facing canyon. But it's hard to miss the brilliant color of these Santa-colored Russula emetica (or kin) mushrooms poking up through the oak leaves in the woods. Some critter has been nibbling to make the "eyes" - but it didn't nibble much. This mushroom is always listed as poisonous, and it apparently lives up to its "emetic" species name to induce vomiting. I like to nibble on it too - just a bit - it has a surprising hot peppery taste and no effects so far. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2005)

Like that cartoon car that falls to pieces at the end of the trip, I made it through school to the holidays and then got the flu/cold that's been going around. *blah* I managed to take a walk today and some photos. I'm glad Nikon puts that "sneeze-guard" over the LCD screen!

File.Name: 5c17b_5c17-018.jpg
Notes: These drab little LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - are growing from very rotten wood that crumbles like soil. Did you notice the tiny "buttons" at the bottom? These mushrooms have attached gills, a brown cap, a ring on the fibrillose stem colored by rusty-brown spores, base with white mycelial strands... Yipes, I think these are Deadly Galerina (Galerina autumnalis) mushrooms in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) woods near home. If so, they contain Amanita-toxins, and they are DEADLY POISONOUS! These mushrooms are a good reason not to eat ANY "Little Brown Mushrooms". (The other good reason is "Why bother?") (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c17c_5c17-031.jpg
Notes: Cluster of Laccaria laccata (or L. amethystina?) mushrooms in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) woods. Maybe the caps were purplish when fresh, but these have lost all color with the dry weather except underneath - see [Previous] photo. Note the tough, hairy, fibrous stalks. Said to be edible, but why bother when there are Chanterelles in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2005)
ScientificName: Laccaria laccata; L. amethystina; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Laccaria laccata; L. amethystina; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 5c17d_5c17-033.jpg
Notes:
Looking under the mushrooms in the [Next] photo - I reckon these are Laccaria laccata (or maybe L. amethystina?) mushrooms in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) woods. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2005)

I know the colors are different between this and the [Next] photo of the same mushrooms. This one is natural light (1/8 second @ f/16), and the next is flash. I try to "look good" - matching white balance is another big step I don't know how to do yet.
ScientificName: Laccaria laccata; L. amethystina; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Laccaria laccata; L. amethystina; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 5c17e_5c17-010.jpg
Notes: Another fist-sized Bolete mushroom coming up from Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the woods. It has no red on the stem or in the cracks on the cap - I guess this is the "Boring Brown Bolete" (Boletus subtomentosus), as David Arora calls it in his excellent book "Mushrooms Demystified". It may be edible, but bland at best, why bother? The white patch on the stem is the start of a different fungus infection - fungus on fungus - that will consume the mushroom into a yellow puddle. I'll get the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2005)
CommonNames: Bolete; Coast Live Oak; Boring Brown Bolete

File.Name: 5c17f_5c17-003.jpg
Notes: Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from the base of a dead sapling Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. The cap starts folded under so the white patches on the cap join the fluffy white ring tissue on the stem to make a "partial veil". You can see how the gills have pulled away from the stem leaving trails above the ring. Fresh caps are edible, but they soon get yeasty and full of maggots. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c18a_5c18-040.jpg
Notes: It's been dark and drizzly all day. I went out looking for a rain photo and was surprised to find this inch-long caterpillar hanging from a thread attached to an old native White Sage (Salvia apiana) flower stalk. I guess it's some kind of Moth (order Lepidoptera). It was rapidly spinning around one way and then the other, often jerking into this acrobatic "comma-shape", and slowly hauling itself up the web, leg over leg over leg. It took the caterpillar about an hour to climb back to the plant - see my [Previous] two photos. I've seen this behavior before. Did the caterpillar fall off the branch, stopped by its belay line? (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c18b_5c18-046.jpg
Notes:
This Moth (order Lepidoptera) caterpillar is hauling itself up a strand of web. Notice how the web is doubled over through its legs and back to its mouth. I'm sure the caterpillar is eating the web as it climbs! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2005)

I wish I could see more detail on the legs to see how this critter manages the "rope". This must be a special adaptation. I was lucky to get a shot this clear - standing on a chair in the drizzle with my tripod fully extended over my head, with the caterpillar continuously jerking and spinning.

File.Name: 5c18c_5c18-048.jpg
Notes: The Moth (order Lepidoptera) caterpillar in the [Next] two photos finally climbed back up its web to the old White Sage (Salvia apiana) flower stalk. At least it's a piece of the old sage flowers suspended by webbing from the plant above. I'll check back tomorrow. I wonder if it's getting ready to spin a cocoon? (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c19a_5c19-053.jpg
Notes:
Red-Cracked Bolete (Boletus chrysenteron or kin) mushroom under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. Said to be edible, but mediocre. I've never been inspired to try it. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2005)

I'm still fighting a cold/flu. I took my walk today, but I wasn't inspired. *blah*

File.Name: 5c19b_5c19-063.jpg
Notes:
Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. Beautiful but deadly. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2005)

If you collect any mushrooms to eat, you must learn this one first. For more info, see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=11785997

File.Name: 5c19c_5c19-064.jpg
Notes: Looking under the "skirt" of a beautiful but deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. The skirt is the remnant of the "partial veil" membrane that covers the gills before they mature. Notice the yellow droplets. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c19d_5c19-050.jpg
Notes: Tiny little Mycena acicula or kin in the woods. For scale, that's a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf on the left with 1 mm spines. I'm not surprised that there are tiny mushrooms, but why are they so pretty? (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c19e_5c19-065.jpg
Notes: Pretty little LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - probably little white-spored Mycena - pushing up through Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the woods after a bit of rain yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c20a_5c20-079.jpg
Notes:
I was mostly looking for mushrooms today, but I noticed these mushroom-like insect galls on a branch of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the woods. I believe these are the galls of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella, family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera). The galls start with bright yellow spots, but they are losing their summer colors. I reckon these are ready to "hatch" and release the tiny adult wasps within. (This gall is "monothalamous" - just one wasp per gall.) Galls are tumor-like growths on plants caused by certain insects that re-program the host tree to grow a distinctive home for their young. Mushrooms are the fruiting bodies of fungi that release spores. So why are mushrooms and galls often so beautiful - is nature just like that or is there some hidden adaptation? (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2005)

I have another photo of this gall at
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=1236170
It's said to be a "twig gall" - but the galls sure look like super-sized versions of the oak buds on the same branch, right down to the characteristic beak. Maybe it's really a "bud gall"?
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Beaked Twig Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 5c20b_5c20-092.jpg
Notes:
I guess this pretty mushroom is the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata). It's growing under both Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and one of our "other" Scrub Oaks (Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens). Like most Amanitas, it has a ring on the stem, a cup at the base, and white fragments of the primal universal-veil "egg" on the cap. I'm not sure of this ID because we also have a confusing yellow-form of Amanita muscaria in our canyon, but this one has a real collar-like cup at the base. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2005)

This Amanita gemmata probably contains the same toxins as Amanita muscaria. That's the classic "Smurf Mushroom" with Christmas colors that everyone knows, as caricature at least. Try a web search for "Cosmic Jimmy".
CommonNames: Jonquil Amanita; Coast Live Oak; Scrub Oaks
ScientificName: Amanita gemmata; Quercus agrifolia; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Amanitas; Amanita muscaria; Amanita gemmata; Amanita muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita gemmata; Quercus agrifolia; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Amanitas; Amanita muscaria; Amanita gemmata; Amanita muscaria

File.Name: 5c20c_5c20-083.jpg
Notes: Interesting bolete-type mushroom with sponge-like gills rather than gills growing under both Scrub Oak (Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens) and Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa. It doesn't look right for a local Bolete, but I guess it's the closely related Manzanita Bolete (Leccinum manzanitae) mushroom. Notice the dark scales ("scabers") on the stem, and the sterile rim around the cap. The pores look reddish on the edge. Maybe edible - caveat emptor! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2005)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Eastwood Manzanita; Manzanita Bolete
ScientificName: Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; Bolete; Leccinum manzanitae
associatedTaxa: Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; Bolete; Leccinum manzanitae

File.Name: 5c20d_5c20-076.jpg
Notes: Yellow-Staining Milky Cap (Lactarius chrysorheus or kin) mushrooms in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca). If you cut across the gills with a twig, they exude a white juice that turns bright yellow in a few seconds. Bitter taste, not edible. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2005)
CommonNames: Yellow-Staining Milky Cap; Coast Live Oak; Bigberry Manzanita
ScientificName: Lactarius chrysorheus; Quercus agrifolia; Arctostaphylos glauca
associatedTaxa: Lactarius chrysorheus; Quercus agrifolia; Arctostaphylos glauca

File.Name: 5c20e_5c20-098.jpg
Notes: Pretty Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushrooms under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c20f_5c20-103.jpg
Notes: One of our resident Anna's Hummingbirds perched in the still-flowering hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the garden. Note the yellow pollen on its bill - it's still doing polination duty. The hummers have a hard time in winter during the cold and rain. It would be easier if they didn't fight so much! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c21a_5c21-104.jpg
Notes:
New flowers of California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) - a native tree in the canyon. The sun only nudged above the trees and through the clouds in our dark north-facing canyon for a short time today. I got this photo looking up into the tree with my Tamron 200-500 lens at full zoom and minimum focus from about 8 feet away, with a cloudy sky behind. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2005)

This is my Winter Solstice photo for today. It's the shortest day of the year north of the equator. Tomorrow will be light just a bit longer and the sun will be just a bit higher. It's a good time for this bright photo of new flowers and a new year. Today's photos are all natural light, such as it is.

File.Name: 5c21b_5c21-113.jpg
Notes:
Looking into the micro-world... These are tiny Mycena acicula mushrooms growing from a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) twig on the ground in the woods. An oak leaf would be too big for scale, but look close and see the tiny white and yellow quartz sand crystals. Look closer at the space between the base of the mushroom stalks - there's a little insect husk on the twig! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2005)

It's interesting that even at this scale, something has been nibbling on the mushrooms!

File.Name: 5c21c_5c21-111.jpg
Notes:
Perfect edible delicious Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms pushing up a "mushrump" pile of leaves in the woods, catching a hint of Winter Solstice light. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2005)

Chanterelles are one of the "safe edible mushrooms" that are easy to learn *once you've seen them* - but you still have to learn for yourself! They are yellow-orange, but see my [Previous] photo - and they have blunt ridges underneath rather than sharp gills. Books and photos help, but you really have to learn the mushrooms from another person who knows, and then you will know. It's like learning music - real culture!

I'm recovering from a nasty flu, and still have no appetite. Fortunately the chanterelles last for many days - even weeks - without drying out or getting wormy. I'll fill a basket to take over the mountains and through the woods to Grammy and Pop-pops for Christmas dinner!

File.Name: 5c21d_5c21-116.jpg
Notes:
I've been watching this new cluster of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing at the base of a standing dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon. It's still growing, but very s_l_o_w_l_y, waiting for real rain. The caps are still pencil-eraser size. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2005)

These orange mushrooms are poisonous, and they look very different from the delicious orange Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms (see [Next] photo) that are also fruiting in the woods right now. They grow in clusters on wood; they have an unappetizing greeninh tinge that becomes stronger as they grow; they have thin gills rather than blunt ridges; they have a nasty "mushroom aura" you learn to recognize. Mushrooms are different, just like people are - you have to learn them. No shortcuts - don't rely on simple rules!
Locality: see [Next] photo) that are also fruiting in the woods right now.  They grow in clusters on wood; they have an unappetizing greeninh tinge that becomes stronger as they grow; they have thin gills rather than blunt ridges; they have a nasty "mushroom aura" you learn to recognize. File.Name: 5c21e_5c20-070.jpg
Notes: This fungus has a fungus. It's hard to tell from this specimen, but from other caps nearby I guess this is a Russula or Lactarius mushroom despite the shape of the cap. It's enveloped by an invading fungus that's spilling over to the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves. Wash between your toes! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c21f_5c20-089.jpg
Notes:
Perfect urn-shaped flowers of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa) - they look like baby birds waiting for a meal. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2005)

On this mid-winter Solstice day, I wonder what insects will visit these early flowers with the gift for next year's seeds?

File.Name: 5c22a_5c22-119.jpg
Notes:
We had gale-force winds and clouds in the mountains today, but there were rumors of big waves, so I went to the beach at Rincon. There were waves, but the weather went from hazy to fog. My photos were all grey and needed some work. This is one of those nasty waves "in the soup" of the big wave that came before. That's nature too! (Rincon Point, 22 December 2005)

Fun afternoon - I met a former student and an old friend. I was a surfer-kid growing up in Santa Monica in the 60s. I lost a board into the Rincon seawall during another big swell back in 1964 or so. I got started taking surf photos every day and making B/W prints in my closet darkroom to sell at the beach the next day. I had my photos published in old issues of Surfer Magazine. I lost all those old photos, but some of them have resurfaced as the tiny scans along the left edge of:
   http://www.davesweetsurfboards.com/SweetHistory.htm

(Thanks Danny - I still want better scans!)

File.Name: 5c22b_5c22-120.jpg
Notes: Big winds in the mountains today, and big waves at the beach. I went to Rincon to watch the surfers. Measuring on the screen, this wave is easily three-times overhead. It looks so calm! (Rincon Point, 22 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c22c_5c22-125.jpg
Notes:
[nature] 11478127 Gull on the beach with a mollusk - you can see the critter's FOOT. (Rincon Point, 22 December 2005)

What are YOU thinking of?

File.Name: 5c23a_5c23-158.jpg
Notes: First new flowers of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the woods along West Camino Cielo road. I hope our resident Anna's Hummingbirds find them! Maybe I shouldn't be surprised. We are just past the Winter Solstice hump, so the days are getting a bit longer and brighter. Weather has been mild - we need rain. Native plants and mushrooms are getting confused. This sure is different from last year when we were getting pounded by record rainfall about now! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c23b_5c23-175.jpg
Notes:
First new flowers of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus). It's interesting that the shrubs in our mountains have alternate leaves, but shrubs on the Channel Islands (C. megacarpus var. insularis have opposite leaves. Both here and there, I find some plants that break the rule. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2005)

I have a photo of the Channel Island variety at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=11641261

Sometimes in a good year these shrubs cover the mountains with flowers like snow. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=6606020

File.Name: 5c23c_5c23-128.jpg
Notes:
The "Coccora" mushroom - Amanita calyptroderma - growing under Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) and some non-native Mediterranean two-needle Pine (Pinus sp.) trees at an old homestead site in the mountains. My book says "edible and unsurpassed" - but I've never tried it because it looks so much like the deadly poisonous Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) that I've also been finding in the woods lately. That's good advice unless you really know your mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2005)

Maybe I shouldn't worry, since this edible Amanita is distinctive: yellow or orange cap; edge of cap "striate" with lines from the gills underneath; soft and thick white patch on the cap; white gills and membranous white "skirt" on the stem; large cup-like sack at the base like it's made of cottony felt. "Coccora" is Italian for "coccoon" - perfect description of the soft white "egg" these mushrooms emerge from.

The deadly cousin Death Cap (Amanita phalloides)has a thin cup at the base and only a hint of a patch on the cap, if any. It's not soft and fuzzy or coccoon-like. I've been finding many of this year. See my photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=11880839
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=11785997
Locality: Amanita phalloides)has a thin cup at the base and only a hint of a patch on the cap
CommonNames: Madrone; Pine; Death Cap; Death Cap
ScientificName: Amanita calyptroderma; Arbutus menziesii; Pinus; Amanita phalloides; Amanita; Amanita phalloides

File.Name: 5c23e_5c23-142.jpg
Notes: Pretty Blewitt (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms growing along an old trail under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees. These mushrooms are edible and tasty when fresh - but they lose their color when cooked. Fresh mushroom caps like these are easy to recognize by their lavender color. They loose the pretty color as they age and look like every other old mushroom. By then it doesn't matter. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2005)

associatedTaxa: Clitocybe nuda; Quercus agrifolia; Arbutus menziesii

File.Name: 5c23f_5c23-136.jpg
Notes: Little white-spored Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushrooms under some non-native Mediterranean two-needle Pine (Pinus sp.) and Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees at an old homestead site in the woods. You can see the spent "pollen cones" on the ground. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2005)
CommonNames: Oak-Loving Collybia; Pine; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 5c24a_5c23-149.jpg
Notes: Sticky stockings hung from the pines with care? This is as close as we have (so far) to icicles this dry winter - pretty "sapcicles' of pine resin hanging from a non-native Mediterranean two-needle Pine (Pinus sp.) tree at an old homestead site in the mountains. Gotta be careful - a close enounter with his sticky stuff would destroy my favorite lens. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c24b_5c23-162.jpg
Notes: New male staminate catkins of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) hanging like Christmas ornaments in the woods. The best present is the gift of life when days are dark. I give thanks for new flowers. Now it's time for rain! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c24c_5c23-170.jpg
Notes: Some "deciduous" natives drop their leaves all at once and go dormant. Other "evergreen" natives shed their leaves one by one so they are always green. Our native Scrub Oaks (Quercus berberidifolia) are "semi-deciduous" - they drop their leaves all at once, but they get new leaves at the same time. These are the new leaf buds. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c24d_5c24-008.jpg
Notes: Brilliant new flowers of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanun xanti) growing on a burned area in the mountains. These flowers are "shakers" - the pollen-bearing stamens clasp the female style. Pollen falls out as wind and critters jiggle the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c24f_5c11-055.jpg
Notes: New flowers of a native California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) tree in the canyon. Look close to spot the little the little Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) resting on the leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2005)
CommonNames: California Bay; Oregon Myrtle; Syrphid Flower Fly

File.Name: 5c25a_5c24-009.jpg
Notes:
I don't know if this flower head of Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) is the last of the old or the first of the new. Either way, here it is in the mountains with Christmas colors. The red "petals" are just leafy bracts, but you can see the real flowers on the left and right sides. See my [Next] photo for a closer view. Our real Christmas present may be coming tonight - there's a chance of much needed rain. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2005)

I wish all you friends and favorites at fotolog the very best - seek the light and share it!

File.Name: 5c25b_5c24-009.jpg
Notes: Looking close at a real flower of Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa). The brilliant red leafy bracts get your attention, but the real petals are fused into that thin tube in the lower-left. The two-lobed female stigma sticks out at the tip, and you can see a bit of the mostly hidden male stamens below. I think of this as a spring flower, but here it is with perfect Christmas colors. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c25c_5c23-168.jpg
Notes:
Ripe fruit of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood) aka Christmas Berry (Heteromeles arbutifolia). The shrubs flower in summer and mature in mid-winter. They are a real gift for the birds during a hard time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2005)

I have a stumper about these pretty native berries:

Drive in the country, and you will notice the brilliant ripe Christmas Berry shrubs - aka Toyon or California Holly, the namesake of Hollywood. The cooked red fruit were a native American Chumash staple, though many Web sites list them as poisonous. I understand why foliage and seeds might be toxic - to protect the plant. But plants use extra resources to grow succulent fruit and berries. Surely the point is to attract critters (like us!) to eat them and spread the seeds. That's the stumper. Are there really poisonous fruit and berries? What's the advantage for the plants?
ScientificName: Heteromeles arbutifolia; Christmas Berry; Toyon; California Holly; Chumash
associatedTaxa: Heteromeles arbutifolia; Christmas Berry; Toyon; California Holly; Chumash

File.Name: 5c25d_5c23-167.jpg
Notes: Waxy flower buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) family. The flowers wait fully-formed for months before opening. Sometimes it's called the "taco plant" because of the folded leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c25e_5c23-156.jpg
Notes: Early flowers of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the woods. This particular plant is the only one I've seen with flowers so early - and so many flowers! I hope our resident Anna's Hummingbirds find them! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c25f_5926-085.jpg
Notes: A light in the dark... north or south of the equator at solstice time, our holidays celebrate light. I wish all you friends and favorites at fotolog the very best - seek the light and share it! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c26a_5c26-024.jpg
Notes:
New flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum). It's so dark in our north-facing canyon at this solstice-time of the year, but it's a pleasure to hike over to the sunny side. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2005)

We had a bit of much-needed rain last night. It's windy with some clouds today, but nice to get out.

File.Name: 5c26b_5c26-036.jpg
Notes:
New flowers of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) starting to flower in the mountains. These shrubs in our mountains have alternate leaves, but shrubs on the Channel Islands (C. megacarpus var. insularis have opposite leaves. We also have C. cuneatus and C. crassifolius with white flowers and opposite leaves - and other species with blue flowers, and many more species if I go for a drive. All these species seem like tight boxes when you use a flora to identify one specimen, but they blur together in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2005)

Sometimes in a good year these shrubs cover the mountains with flowers like snow. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=6606020
ScientificName: Ceanothus megacarpus; C. megacarpus; insularis; C. cuneatus; C. crassifolius
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus megacarpus; C. megacarpus; insularis; C. cuneatus; C. crassifolius

File.Name: 5c26c_5c26-032.jpg
Notes: Here's a different white-flowered shrub that will soon flower in the mountains - Snowball or Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius). The leaves have odd dark-colored breast-like stipules at the base of the opposite leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c26d_5c26-039.jpg
Notes:
Last summer's insect galls on the underside of a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) leaf in the woods. I believe these are caused by the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus chrystallinus). The galls have brilliant red colors when fresh, but no more. These oaks are "semi-deciduous" - they drop their leaves all at once, then get new leaves at the same time. I reckon the galls will soon fall with the leaves to do whatever they do to carry their seed into another year. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2005)

There sure is a lot to know about the woods! See my photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=10876618
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=1236813
for more info about this interesting gall.

File.Name: 5c26e_5c26-051.jpg
Notes: I noticed an unusual dark mass on this branch of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) late this afternoon in the shade. I had to use flash to capture these Ants (family Formicdae in the order Hymenoptera) tending the tiny Aphids (family Aphididae). I don't know what kind of ant this is. It looks like our usual aggressive and smelly "piss-ant" - the California Harvester Ant (Pogonomyrmex californicus or kin). (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c26f_5c26-042.jpg
Notes: Fog trying to break like a wave over the mountains. We had a bit of much-needed rain last night. Today is windy and unsettled. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c27a_5c27-055.jpg
Notes:
Tiny mosquito-size fly (order Diptera, suborder Nematocera) resting on a leaflet of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) with rain drops. We don't get much light at solstice time. I took this photo at 1/2 second @ f/32 using tripod and self-timer. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2005)

Today's photos are all small perfect things in the woods. I've been thinking of the poet Rilke:

"If you trust in Nature, in the small Things that hardly anyone sees and that can so suddenly become huge, immeasurable; if you have this love for what is humble and try very simply, as someone who serves, to win the confidence of what seems poor: then everything will become easier for you, more coherent and somehow more reconciling, not in your conscious mind perhaps, which stays behind, astonished, but in your innermost awareness, awakeness, and knowledge...

File.Name: 5c27b_5c27-077.jpg
Notes:
These delicate white flowers of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) are the very first herbaceous "spring" wildflower in our canyon. They have four long stamens and two shorter ones, like all members of the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2005)

It's been mostly dry this winter, but we've had several little drizzles just when we need it. Last year was very wet. I got a photo of this flower on January 17, 2005:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=9247246
The year before was dry. I didn't find it until February 7, 2004:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=6025741

Every year is different, which keeps it interesting for photographers and naturalists!

File.Name: 5c27c_5c27-054.jpg
Notes: Tiny "Little Brown Mushrooms" (LBMs) with decurrent gills that grow down the stalk, maybe Clitocybe? We don't get much sunlight in our north-facing canyon at this time of year. Fortunately mushrooms don't move around much, so I can play with long exposures to increase the DOF. I got this photo in the shade at 1 second @ f/32 @ ISO 200. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c27d_5c27-058.jpg
Notes: Ants (family Formicdae, order Hymenoptera) tending the tiny Aphids (family Aphididae) on a green branch of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus). Hard to get a sharp photo even in the light because the ants move so fast touching the aphids with their antennae and collecting their honeydew. These ants look and smell like our usual "piss-ants" - the California Harvester Ant (Pogonomyrmex californicus or kin), but I don't usually find them doing this. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c27e_5c27-074.jpg
Notes:
Tiny "acorn-lets" on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). My books say this oak developes acorns in just one year. Are these aborted acorns that never developed, or are they still waiting? (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2005)

I also saw this on Island Scrub Oak (Quercus pacifica) in the central valley of Santa Cruz Island last month. Look at the base of the green acorn in my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=11819631

File.Name: 5c27f_5c27-067.jpg
Notes: New fruit and flowers of Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca). The berries are bigger than other local manzanitas, and they have a single large nut inside. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c28a_5c20-102.jpg
Notes:
Tiny Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider with prey. We have a new winter-shift of spiders picking off the new Fungus Gnats (etc.) to replace the autumn-shift Crab Spiders (Thomisidae) that have been getting the last of the pollinators on last year's flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2005)

We had rain and wind today - no chance for photos. I worked on a VCD slideshow disk of my "Treebeard's Photos" for my parents. I made a 1,900 photo (since July 2004) slideshow with music by Zoot Sims and Chet Baker and Pee Wee Russell that plays on a DVD player. It looks and sounds fabulous. I wish I could share it here!

File.Name: 5c28c_5c27-068.jpg
Notes: I guess this is a Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) mushroom under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) at "The Playground" in the mountains - or is it the yellow-form of Amanita muscaria? Either way it contains interesting and dangerous toxins. I mostly find this mushroom around native American Chumash sites like this. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2005)

Notes: I guess these are White Russula (Russula albidula or kin) mushrooms in the woods. Notice the little green "X" of the California Bedstraw (Gallium californicum ssp. flaccidum) and the white fuzz on the mushroom behind. The fungus has a fungus infection! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c28e_5c27-072.jpg
Notes: New fruit and flowers of Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca). (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c28f_5c27-076.jpg
Notes:
I think this interesting native oak is a "Shreve Oak" - which may be Quercus parvula; or Quercus parvula var. shrevei; or maybe Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens. It's a small tree with leaves that are shiny and hairless on top and bottom. It seems to be "semi-deciduous" - they drop their leaves all at once, then get new leaves at the same time - just like our native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c30a_5c30-092.jpg
Notes:
Brilliant red Russula emetica (or kin) mushroom catching a last ray of sunlight in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2005)

There's a hush in the woods today - and growing clouds and wind as a real winter storm moves in for the weekend. Everything is waiting for rain!

File.Name: 5c30b_5c30-086.jpg
Notes: I guess these are edible Candy Cap (Lactarius camphoratus or kin) mushrooms in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). They have a sweet spicy smell and taste. All Lactarius mushrooms should exude a latex (milk or juice) when you slice the gills near the stalk. I tried and didn't see anything, but the weather has been very dry, and I can see old latex-stains on the gills. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c30c_5c30-104.jpg
Notes:
One little Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushroom dropping its white spores on another in the woods. When I got back home to my computer, I noticed the mushroom-colored Fly (order Diptera) perched on the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf in the foreground. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2005)

I wonder if this is the fly that took over our Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea). See my photo and links at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=11722301

File.Name: 5c30d_5c30-091.jpg
Notes:
I believe this pretty mushroom is Cortinarius varius or kin - it has a slimy violet cap and a stem without a bulbous base. This photo shows the "partial veil" that covers the gills before they open. As the cap expands, it leaves a "cortina" - a ring of cobwebby hairs that usually turn rusty-brown as they catch falling spores. We have many Cortinarius species that all look the same as they get old, but the young 'shrooms are beautiful. Maybe edible, maybe not. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2005)

I've mistaken these mushrooms for edible Blewitts (Clitocybe nuda), but you can tell when you dig them up.

File.Name: 5c30e_5c30-089.jpg
Notes: Strange Black Elfin Saddle (Helvella lacunosa) mushroom in the woods. I call it the "Brain Mushroom" because that`s what it looks like. Said to be edible, but I haven't been tempted to try it. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2005)

File.Name: 5c30f_5c30-084.jpg
Notes: As an apple on an apple tree, or the hip on a rose, so is the mushroom for a fungus. Mushrooms are the fruiting bodies of fungi that disperse spores to reproduce. This is the growing mycelium of a fungus working to decompose these dead leaves in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2005)

[January - 1 of 3]

Looking across the Santa Ynez Valley from Figueroa Mountain at sunset. You can see the peak of Mt. Tranquillon in the upper right, our only local volcano, long extinct. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 January 2002)

We had 2+ inches of good soaking rain today, just what we need to end a dry autumn and get ready for the predicted deluge tomorrow night. No chance for photos today, but it's New Year's time - a perfect chance to look back and think ahead.
