File.Name: 4101b_4101-206.jpg
Notes: Looking into a cluster of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from the stump of a dead oak tree. They're poisonous. Some varieties are said to glow in the dark, but I've never seen it here. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2004)

File.Name: 4101h_3c31-186.jpg
Notes: Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila) is probably the most common mushshroom in the woods right now. These clusters are part of a large (but spotty) fairy ring. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2003)

File.Name: 4101g_3c29-091.jpg
Notes: Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus or kin) fungus growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It's an odd puffball with a tough outer rind that splits into the star and a thin inner sack that holds the spore mass and soon breaks apart. You can see the remains of the inner membrane here. These are a natural hygrometer that open when it's damp and close when it's dry. I rarely find fresh ones with an intact spore sack. The old "stars" last for months. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2003)

File.Name: 4101i_4101-205.jpg
Notes: Looking into a cluster of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from the stump of a dead oak tree. (See previous photo.) You can see a faint dusting of the white spores on the caps. The greenish-tones and sharp gills distinguish these poisonous mushrooms from the delicious Chanterelles (Cantharellus cibarius) that will hopefully grow nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2004)

File.Name: 4101c_4101-202.jpg
Notes: A cluster of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from the stump of a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. They're poisonous. Some varieties are said to glow in the dark, but I've never seen it here. Some of these caps are split from the dry weather this week. It's been overcast and gloomy all week, but there's no rain to speak of. Maybe tonight. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2004)

File.Name: 4101d_4101-217.jpg
Notes: Pretty Psathyrella longipes (or kin) mushroom in the woods. Note the white veil fragments on the edge of the cap. The caps are large - a couple of inches across - and very brittle. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2004)

File.Name: 4101e_4101-210.jpg
Notes: A heart-rot fungus devoured the inside of this oak log. Now these bracket fungi (Trametes?) are digesting the sapwood and slowly turning it into mulch. Life goes on. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2004)

File.Name: 4101f_3c29-102.jpg
Notes: I meet the darndest critters while hunting for mushrooms in our woods. This is a six inch long Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) on the prowl. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2003)

File.Name: 4101a_1a11-043.jpg
Notes: The new year is in our hands. We can make it anything we want, so make it good!
Locality: The new year is in our hands.  We can make it anything we want, so make it good!

File.Name: 4102i_4102-248.jpg
Notes: Cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea and kin) growing bigger on an oak stump at home. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2004)

File.Name: 4102h_4102-242.jpg
Notes: Bracket Fungi (Trametes versicolor) on an oak log. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2004)

File.Name: 4102f_4102-221.jpg
Notes: [noir 4171492] Raindrops hanging from the porch rail reflecting the oak trees beyond. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2004)

File.Name: 4102a_4102-255.jpg
Notes: New flower buds on Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the drizzle today. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2004)

File.Name: 4102b_4102-245.jpg
Notes: Old Man Cactus (Opuntia sp.) in the garden with raindrops on a drizzly day. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2004)

File.Name: 4102c_4102-238.jpg
Notes: Tiny Marasmius plicatulus is one my favorite mushrooms, though you have to look close to find it. The cap is deep wine-red with a frosty white coating that's visible on the edges in this photo. The stem is brittle but smooth like metal. I found several growing in the humus under our Coast Live Oaks (Quercus agrifolia) at home. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2004)

File.Name: 4102d_4102-243.jpg
Notes: It was drizzly all day today. I never wandered far from home, but it's amazing how BIG this is when you start looking close at the micro-world. I think this bulls-eye pattern on a single Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf is a fungus growing outward through the leaf, like a tiny fairy ring. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2004)

File.Name: 4102e_4102-233.jpg
Notes: Pretty little fruticose lichen (Cladonia?) fruiting bodies (called "podetia") growing on a sandstone boulder at home, along with mosses and liverworts and tiny fern prothalli. Look close and see that each tiny lichen cap holds a raindrop on top. Is this an adaptation to spread spores and soredia? I wish I could get even closer with my camera, but I have to use a tripod even for this and photos are "iffy". (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2004)

File.Name: 4102g_4102-224.jpg
Notes: Raindrops hanging from the far side of the porch rail reflecting the oak trees beyond. I cropped but otherwise this is straight from my camera. Why do the drops have such perfect black borders?? (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2004)

File.Name: 4103g_4102-235.jpg
Notes: Pretty little fruticose lichen (Cladonia?) fruiting bodies (called "podetia") growing on a sandstone boulder at home, along with mosses and liverworts and tiny fern prothalli. Look close and see that each tiny lichen cap holds a raindrop on top. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2004)

File.Name: 4103a_4103-001.jpg
Notes: Fronds of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta) with the sun behind, showing shadows of the sporangia on the back side of the leaves. We live on the north slope of these east-west mountains, and sunlight is scarce this time of year. On the other hand, it's always dramatic! (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2004)

File.Name: 4103b_4103-019.jpg
Notes: New leaves of Coffee Fern (Pellea andromedifolia). The name is a mystery to me. The contrasting colors of stem and leaflet are beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2004)

File.Name: 4103c_4103-010.jpg
Notes: Pretty Cortinarius collinitus (or kin) mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Bay (Umbellularia californica) near the creek. It has a very slimy cap and slimy bands on the stem. Said to be edible, but it's so slimy it would never occur to me to try. This is the first Cortinarius I've seen this year, but we have many species. See the [previous] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2004)
ScientificName: Cortinarius collinitus; Quercus agrifolia; Umbellularia californica; Cortinarius
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius collinitus; Quercus agrifolia; Umbellularia californica; Cortinarius

File.Name: 4103d_4103-012.jpg
Notes: The under-parts of Cortinarius collinitus (or kin). Store-bought Agaricus mushrooms have a membranous skin that covers the gills before they open, but these Cortinarius mushrooms have a veil of cobwebby hairs that turn rusty-brown from the falling spores. This species is distinctive for having slimy bands on the stem below the veil, as well as a very slimy cap. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2004)

File.Name: 4103e_4103-022.jpg
Notes: Yesterday's raindrops on new leaves of Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata). They will get the distinctive round leaves when they flower. Now or then, they are a delicious salad green. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2004)

File.Name: 4104e_4104-064.jpg
Notes: Today's basket of beautiful Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms meet their destiny as Cream of Chanterelle soup! The first step is to dry saute the mushrooms to concentrate the flavor. Clean and slice them into a hot dry pan, and let them stew in their own juices to concentrate the flavor until almost dry. Now it's time to make soup! (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2004)

File.Name: 4104a_4104-041.jpg
Notes: Beautiful geometry of a Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom. Note how the thick blunt gills are forked and cross-veined. There`s no reason to confuse these delicious mushrooms with anything else. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2004)

File.Name: 4104b_4104-048.jpg
Notes: A Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom poking up through the oak leaves at home. Feel around under the leaves and you'll probably find many more. I hear these are going for $20 a pound in the local farmers' markets. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2004)

File.Name: 4104c_4104-046.jpg
Notes: Yum, a basket of delicious Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms for dinner tonight, and there are lots more coming! Also known as girolle and pfifferling. Yes, they are dirty with the damp soil. We'll clean them with a knife rather than water. Hmmm, soup or pizza? (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2004)

File.Name: 4104d_4104-051.jpg
Notes: Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus or kin) fungus growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). You can see the tough outer rind that splits into the star and the thin inner sack that holds the spore mass and soon breaks apart and disappears. I always find them sitting on top of the leaves not attached to anything. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2004)

File.Name: 4105g_4103-011.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Pretty Cortinarius collinitus (or kin) mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Bay (Umbellularia californica) near the creek. It has a very slimy cap and slimy bands on the stem. Said to be edible, but it's so slimy it would never occur to me to try. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2004)
ScientificName: Cortinarius collinitus; Quercus agrifolia; Umbellularia californica
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius collinitus; Quercus agrifolia; Umbellularia californica

File.Name: 4105a_4104-027.jpg
Notes: Julie tending her magic garden at home! Of course the grasses are just overexposed. The real magic is that Julie can grow natives like this California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass from collected seed for our garden and the restoration project at school. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2004)

File.Name: 4105b_4104-026.jpg
Notes: California Brome (Bromus carinatus) seedlings in Julie's nursery at home. See [next] photo for a different view. This is a beautiful California native bunch grass that we are encouraging at school and home. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2004)

File.Name: 4105c_4104-034.jpg
Notes: Tiny Mycena mushroom growing in a pot with native California Buckwheats (Eriogonum fasciculatum) grown from seed in our home nursery. These fragile white-spored mushrooms are everywhere in the woods when you look close. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2004)

File.Name: 4108f_4104-030.jpg
Notes: Tiny Mycena mushroom growings in a pot with native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass grown from seed in our home nursery. These fragile white-spored mushrooms are everywhere in the woods when you look close. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2004)

File.Name: 4108a_4108-271.jpg
Notes: Gills of old Leucopaxillus albissimus mushrooms growing in deep leaf litter under oak and pine. These mushrooms resist decay and must be left over from the last rain. Now I'm waiting for the next rain. I hope it comes soon! (Figueroa Mountain, 8 January 2004)

File.Name: 4108b_4108-265.jpg
Notes: Looking close at the gills of a Short-Stemmed Russula (Russula brevipes). We didn't see many mushrooms on my after-school Hike Club foray today, but there were some barely visible "mushrumps" - humps in the the deep leaf mulch - and we found the mushrooms hiding underneath. The "Russula test" is to break the stem. If it snaps like a piece of chalk then bingo! I don't eat Russulas, but I always nibble and spit. This one starts mild and gets hot. (Figueroa Mountain, 8 January 2004)

File.Name: 4108c_4108-292.jpg
Notes: Looking northwest from the top of Figueroa Mountain past Ponderosa Pine (Pinus ponderosa) and Goldencup Oak (Quercus chrysolepis). There are many layers of mountains in the distance to explore in the Santa Barbara backcountry. (Figueroa Mountain, 8 January 2004)

File.Name: 4108d_4108-286.jpg
Notes: Looking past a Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) into the Santa Barbara backcountry from the top of Figueroa Mountain. That's Hurricane Deck catching the late sun and the Sierra Madre Mountains beyond. It's a grand wilderness. (Figueroa Mountain, 8 January 2004)

File.Name: 4109h_4108-262.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Short-Stemmed Russula (Russula brevipes). We didn't see many mushrooms on our foray, but there were some barely visible "mushrumps" - humps in the the deep leaf mulch - and we found these mushrooms hiding underneath. The "Russula test" is to break the stem. If it snaps like a piece of chalk then bingo! I don't eat Russulas, but I always nibble and spit. This one starts mild and gets hot. (Figueroa Mountain, 8 January 2004)

File.Name: 4109a_4109-336.jpg
Notes: BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) flowers look white as you drive past. Up close, there's a lot of color and texture. Some years there are so many flowers that you can see them from the beach and the mountains look frosted. I'll keep watch. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 January 2004)

File.Name: 4109b_4109-332.jpg
Notes: The first native California-Lilac to flower. This is BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) on the south-facing slopes of our mountains. Central California seasons are confusing. Sycamores are still dropping leaves (fall) and we're building fires every night (winter), but this Ceanothus is a sign that spring is on the way! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 January 2004)

File.Name: 4109c_4108-255.jpg
Notes: Another Cortinarius mushroom in mixed woods under oak and pine. There are many species that all have a veil of cobwebby hairs that turn rusty-brown from the falling spores. The hairs are still visible on the stalk. Not edible, but I enjoy finding them because they are so diverse. (Figueroa Mountain, 8 January 2004)

File.Name: 4109d_4108-259.jpg
Notes: Unknown rusty-spored mushrooms, maybe Gymnopilus? We uncovered these in a "mushrump" or hump in the deep leaf mulch. There are mushrooms now, but they are hidding under the leaves and we had to dig under the leaves to find them. These were too old to collect. The woods are full of holes where critters have dug up mushrooms, so something (besides us) is finding them. (Figueroa Mountain, 8 January 2004)

File.Name: 4110f_4110-002.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Stately cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from an oak stump on the edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2004)

File.Name: 4110g_4110-036.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Unknown Polypore Bracket Fungus (Trametes sp.?) growing on a dead oak log. Note how the fungus is engulfing the stick and oak leaves in the upper right! (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2004)

File.Name: 4110e_4110-061.jpg
Notes: New leaves of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) with the sun behind. The gland-tipped hairs each have a tiny drop of sticky stuff at the tip that gives the plant its pleasant taste and smell. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2004)

File.Name: 4110a_4110-009.jpg
Notes: First flowers on California Bay (Umbellularia californica). I never noticed before how the small clusters of flowers are first covered by paperey bracts that fall away as the individual flowers open. The leaves have an intense Bay Laurel taste and are useful for cooking if you don't overdo it. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2004)

File.Name: 4110b_4110-012.jpg
Notes: Old fronds of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens). This unlikely fern dies back in the winter just as all other ferns in our summer-dry climate are starting to grow. For some reason, my digital camera has a hard time picking up the color of these fronds. Something about white balance? (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2004)

File.Name: 4110c_4110-023.jpg
Notes: Seedling of Bedstraw (Galium aparine) casting an elegant shadow on an unknown LBM ("Little Brown Mushroom"). (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2004)

File.Name: 4110d_4110-018.jpg
Notes: Unknown Cortinarius (maybe C. calyptratus or kin?) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). There are many species that all have a "cortina" - the exquisite veil of cobwebby hairs that covers the immature gills. As the cap opens, the hairs pull free and form a ring on the stem that turns reddish-brown from falling spores. This specimen also has patches of a white outer veil on the cap that originally covered the entire mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2004)

File.Name: 4110h_4110-001.jpg
Notes: Stately cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from an oak stump on the edge of our garden. The mushrooms are edible, but they are named for their color not their taste. I'd rather have them in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2004)

File.Name: 4114h_4114-108.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Fool's Agaric ("Agaricus californicus") pushing up wedges of dry soil in a waste area at school. There must be more mushrooms that can't quite make it and mature underground. This common agaric looks good, but it has a bad phenol-like taste and smell. It's mildly poisonous, and I've also been the fool. We need rain here in central California SOON to keep the mushrooms coming. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 January 2004)

File.Name: 4114g_4110-022.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] A colony of tiny little Cup Fungi (Aleuria or kin) on a dead oak branch in the woods. It's a stumper for me why these tiny fruiting bodies that just release spores are so pretty. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2004)

File.Name: 4114a_4111-086.jpg
Notes: New flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum). The flowers and leaves are glandular and sticky. They have snagged a few wayward dandelion-like composite seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2004)

File.Name: 4114b_4110-048.jpg
Notes: This pretty pink-capped Russula mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) has been nibbled by some woodland critter that left it's "calling card" on the top of the cap. I nibbled too. The taste was pretty mild, a bit spicy. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2004)

File.Name: 4114c_4110-053.jpg
Notes: Unknown Cortinarius mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). (See [Previous] photo.) These mushrooms have beautiful rusty-red and violet colors when fresh, but they lose the color as they age. Mushrooms grow up from the earth and push up a "mushrump" of leaf mulch before they emerge. Learning to spot these mushrumps is the key to finding fresh mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2004)

File.Name: 4114d_4110-041.jpg
Notes: Unknown Cortinarius mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). This photo shows the cobwebby hairs of the cortina that cover the immature gills. The stem is splitting and curling upwards because it is getting dry. Are the cortina hairs actually pulling the stem apart? (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2004)

File.Name: 4114e_4110-010.jpg
Notes: Another new leaf of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) with the sun behind. The gland-tipped hairs of the sage contrast with the non-glandular hairs of the different plant in front. Maybe that's a California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula)? (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2004)

File.Name: 4115a_4115-161.jpg
Notes: This is the mouth of the lizard at "Lizard Mouth," a popular rock-climbing and exploring and party place on the western edge of San Marcos Pass. It looks more like a turtle to me. The mouth is big - 15 or 20 feet high, and much higher from the ground. There is an anchor bolt on top, but I've never made it into the mouth. I call that wonderful erosion pattern in the sandstone a "wind rock". I don't know how it's formed. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2004)

File.Name: 4116a_4116-339.jpg
Notes: Looking at the low sun over Goleta and the ocean from the Old Pass Road, framed by a bare Tree Tobacco (Nicotiana glauca) shrub. It looks wintery, but it feels like spring. Our weather has been clear and dry this week. The woods are crunchy, too dry for mushrooms. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 January 2004)

File.Name: 4117g_4117-042.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Looking under the cap of a Boletus truncatus mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Bay (Umbellularia californica). Boletes have tubes instead of gills, and they look like a sponge underneath. This one instantly turns blue when you scratch it. It's edible but not very inviting. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2004)
ScientificName: Boletus truncatus; Quercus agrifolia; Umbellularia californica
associatedTaxa: Boletus truncatus; Quercus agrifolia; Umbellularia californica

File.Name: 4117h_4117-036.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] A camouflaged mound of Boletus truncatus mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Bay (Umbellularia californica). The bright contrasty light shows that this is NOT mushroom weather. We need rain! (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2004)
ScientificName: Boletus truncatus; Quercus agrifolia; Umbellularia californica
associatedTaxa: Boletus truncatus; Quercus agrifolia; Umbellularia californica

File.Name: 4117a_4117-044.jpg
Notes: Male staminate flowers of Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii, but some with wavy leaves approaching G. elliptica). The dangling catkins are like beautiful chains of beads that grow to be 5+ inches long and get tangled in the wind. This is a dioecious species that also has female pistillate shrubs with catkins, but I haven't seen them yet. Oaks are also dioecious, and the male catkins always appear first. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2004)

File.Name: 4117b_4117-049.jpg
Notes: Male staminate flowers of Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii, but some approaching G. elliptica). The dangling catkins are like beautiful chains of beads that get to be 5+ inches long. The female pistillate shrubs of this dioecious species also have catkins. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2004)

File.Name: 4117c_4117-023.jpg
Notes: This seedling Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) has an embryonic quality, almost like a mushroom. Many are sprouting after a small wildfire last summer. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2004)

File.Name: 4117d_4117-019.jpg
Notes: Sprouting Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) after a small wildfire last summer. The soil is an interesting mix of stone and charcoal. Many unusual native plants are adapted to survive the heat and flower after wildfires. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2004)

File.Name: 4117e_4117-027.jpg
Notes: Young Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) re-sprouting after a small wildfire last summer. It looks like a toasted pineapple! It's impressive that it is sprouting after a fire. It's also impressive that it is growing in such a small pocket of soil in the sandstone. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2004)

File.Name: 4117f_4117-043.jpg
Notes: Water channels on Mars? Nah, this is the cracked cap of a Boletus truncatus mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Bay (Umbellularia californica). Mushrooms are scarce. We need rain. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2004)
ScientificName: Boletus truncatus; Quercus agrifolia; Umbellularia californica
associatedTaxa: Boletus truncatus; Quercus agrifolia; Umbellularia californica

File.Name: 4118g_4118-090.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] These Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) on the edge of our garden has been dropping microscopic white spores for weeks. They pile up on the lower caps like snow. The caps are cracked because it's been dry. We need rain. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4118h_4118-061.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Honey Mushroom (Armillariella mellea) returning to - uh - mush. But it left a thick deposit of white spores before it was done. May we all do so well! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4118a_4118-068.jpg
Notes: First flowers of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii). Flowers, leaves, and stems are glandular. The photo shows how the red "petals" are really sepals. I see a lady dancing, with skirts aflutter. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4118b_4118-124.jpg
Notes: Wood Rush (Luzula comosa) foliage under the oaks along the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4118c_4118-097.jpg
Notes: Seedling Canyon Lupines (Lupinus latifolius) in Julie's nursery at home. Julie grew these natives from seed collected in our canyon. They will be planted here. May they live long and prosper! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4118d_4118-139.jpg
Notes: Mushrooms snuggling. I think they are Entoloma species because they have pink/flesh colored spores and gills and a bleach-like odor when fresh (but not an hour later). The remarkable stems are 7+ inches long! I collected these in deep leaf-mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4118e_4118-085.jpg
Notes: This clump of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) on the edge of our garden has been dropping microscopic white spores for weeks. They pile up on the caps below like snow. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4118f_4118-105.jpg
Notes: This is a stumper photo. These hand-sized white rings are slowly growing across a sandstone boulder covered with moss and lichens. It looks like a growth in a petre dish. Is it a fungus escaping from a lichen? Or a micro-preditor? I want to get my microscope out to examine those tiny red fruiting bodies within the circles. There are mysteries everywhere when you look close! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4119g_4118-065.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) returning to mush. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4119h_4118-063.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) returning to mush. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4119a_4118-119.jpg
Notes: Surreal pattern of an aging cluster of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from the stump of a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. The caps are cracked because it's so dry. We need rain. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4119b_4118-076.jpg
Notes: This cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) on the edge of our garden has been dropping microscopic white spores for weeks. They pile up on the lower caps like snow. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4119c_4119-158.jpg
Notes: As if it's not hard enough to learn mushrooms, you also have to learn how they change as they age. This is the White Russula (Russula albidula or kin) with a young cap in front and an older cap in the back. I love stalking mushrooms, but I only eat a few that are very hard to mistake. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2004)

File.Name: 4119d_4118-111.jpg
Notes: Close shot of the gills of an unknown mushroom with brown spores and an umbonate fibrillose cap. Maybe Hebeloma? (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4119e_4118-108.jpg
Notes: Close shot of the gills of an unknown Cortinarius mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). You can see the rusty-red spores on the gills and stem. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4119f_4119-147.jpg
Notes: Bright red Russula emetica (or kin) hiding in the oak leaves. I always nibble these brittle Russulas. This one has a spicy hot taste (like mustard or wasabe, not chile), but the species name is a clue that we should not actually eat it. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4122g_4122-337.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Volleyball-size Bear's Head (Hericium erinaceus) mushroom growing from a wound on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). This is ten feet up the tree. I'll try to get back with a ladder for a better shot! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 January 2004)

File.Name: 4122a_4122-320.jpg
Notes: My after-school Hike Club kids resting in the mouth of a unique cave in the Santa Barbara mountains. The ferns above are California Maidenhair (Adiantum jordanii). We walked around inside and crawled through a narrow passage back into the light. There is an ancient Chumash Native American village site nearby. The massive rock outcrop is sandstone, but there are small stalactites inside. It`s a secret place, so don`t bother asking. Find your own secret places! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 January 2004)

File.Name: 4122b_4122-317.jpg
Notes: Inside a unique cave in the Santa Barbara mountains. This is sandstone not limestone, but note the slender stalactites formed by calcium leaching through the rock above! I tried taking close-up photos, but my digital camera couldn`t get focus in the darkness. I`ll try again with a tripod. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 January 2004)

File.Name: 4122c_4122-315.jpg
Notes: Inside a unique cave at the base of a massive sandstone outcrop in the Santa Barbara mountains. It looks scoured by water in this photo, and maybe it`s an eroded stream bed embedded in the ancient marine sandstone? This is sandstone not limestone, but there are slender stalactites and nubs of stalagmites formed by calcium leaching through the rock above. In the lower-left, you can see an exposed tree root that has grown into the cave from outside. It feels bumpy with calcium deposits. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 January 2004)

File.Name: 4123a_4118-073.jpg
Notes: In the deep of winter, it feels like spring here in central California. This unknown "Mexican Sage" (Salvia sp.) has survived hard frost and low light with beautiful flowers. If (wet) winter doesn't return soon, it will be a disaster next (dry) summer. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4123b_4118-095.jpg
Notes: Borage (Borago officinalis) is an ancient herb that has naturalized in our garden. It`s flowering now. I think our mild winter is confusing all our garden plants. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4123c_4118-099.jpg
Notes: Soap Plant (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) sprouting spring leaves in our garden. These native lily-relatives have interesting bulbs covered with a thick coat of useful fibers. We rescued this one from a slide along the road, and it seems happy in the dry end of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2004)

File.Name: 4124b_3418-036.jpg
Notes: We found this Gopher Snake (Pituophis melanoleucus) along the trail. The snake is in one hand, camera in the other. This one was gentle, but sometimes they have a temper so be careful holding them. Be careful with the snake too, even if it's ornery! (Figueroa Mountain, 18 April 2003)

File.Name: 4124c_1420-083.jpg
Notes: Southern Alligator Lizard (Elgaria multicarinatus). These handsome critters have a nasty temper. Keep your fingers away from its mouth, or you'll have to pry it off. That's the voice of experience! (Santa Ynez Valley, 20 April 2001)

File.Name: 4124d_1808-021.jpg
Notes: Reason #36 why I need a real telephoto lens: I surprised this little Striped Skunk (Mephitis mephitis) in the driftwood at Goleta Beach. We both stayed calm and went our separate ways. (Gaviota Coast, 8 August 2001)

File.Name: 4124e_2509-064.jpg
Notes: We found this dead California Sea Lion (Zalophus californianus) on the beach at Refugio last year. Note that it has visible ears, unlike seals. This must be the original "sea dog". We`ve found many dead dolphins and seals and birds on Santa Barbara beaches in the past few years. Apparently it`s caused by domoic acid poisoning, a natural biotoxin produced by a bloom of marine diatoms that concentrates through the foodchain. (Gaviota Coast, 9 May 2002)

File.Name: 4124f_2509-085.jpg
Notes: By-The-Wind Sailor (Velella velella) washed up on the sand at Refugio beach. It's a small jellyfish with a sail. They soon lose that brilliant blue color when they are beached, sometimes by the thousands. (Gaviota Coast, 9 May 2002)

File.Name: 4125h_4125-087.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Golden Milk Cap (Lacterius insulsis or kin) mushroom. You can see drops of white "milk" on the gills, see [Previous] photo. I don't know if it's edible, but it has a spicy acrid taste that I like to nibble. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004)

File.Name: 4125g_4125-068.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Mushroom milk? This is Golden Milk Cap (Lacterius insulsis or kin). When you bruise the gills, they release this white "milk" that doesn't change color in the air. I don't know if it's edible, but it has a spicy acrid taste that I like to nibble. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004)

File.Name: 4125a_4125-009.jpg
Notes: Raindrops on Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) with the sun behind. This is right from my camera with no tricks. You can see the sun and sky reflected upside-down in each drop. I don't understand the rays from the drops. Maybe my lens was smeared in just the right way. Julie's comment was "Who would guess the most perfect jewels are in our yard!" (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004) Today's photos are all raindrops and spider webs. Yesterday's rain was no more than a drizzle, but that's just right to leave the drops hanging. We still need a real rain to keep the woods alive.

File.Name: 4125b_4125-002.jpg
Notes: Raindrops on Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) with the sun behind. This is right from my camera with no tricks. You can see the sun reflected in each drop. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004)

File.Name: 4125c_4125-016.jpg
Notes: Raindrops on Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) with the sun behind. This is right from my camera with no tricks. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004)

File.Name: 4125d_4125-019.jpg
Notes: Raindrops on Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus). The odd green circles are drops on the other side of the leaf, with the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004)

File.Name: 4125e_4125-020.jpg
Notes: Raindrops and spider webs and hairs (oh my) tangled on California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula). Maybe the hairs are from our dog Mojo? (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004)

File.Name: 4125f_4125-024.jpg
Notes: Raindrops caught on a spider web hung between leaves of Wood Mint (Stachys bullata). (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004)

File.Name: 4126a_4125-035.jpg
Notes: Unknown Narcissus flowers in our garden holding raindrops from the weekend drizzle. Our native and garden plants seem confused because of our mostly dry weather this year. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004) Can you hold a raindrop? Today's flowers and yesterday's leaves and spider webs can. I think it's a stumper why some plants (but not others) hold the raindrops so perfectly. Is this an adaptation or just a happy accident for nature photographers?

File.Name: 4126b_4125-031.jpg
Notes: The first Bellflower (Campanula sp.) flower in our garden, holding raindrops from the weekend drizzle. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004)

File.Name: 4126c_4125-062.jpg
Notes: Tough Fuchsia (Fuchsia hybrida?) with raindrops. This plant is a survivor through summer heat and 20 degree frost. I'm almost embarassed that it's still flowering. It's under the roof next to our house which must give some protection. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004)

File.Name: 4127g_4119-161.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Close shot of the gills of an unknown mushroom with dull brown spores and an umbonate fibrillose cap. Maybe Hebeloma? (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2004)

File.Name: 4127a_4125-037.jpg
Notes: Tiny white mushrooms with a beautiful textured white cap. That Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf on edge on the left is only about an inch long, so this is smaller than a pencil erasor. Think that's small? Look close at the white shape in the upper right corner. This backyard micro-world is full of surprises! (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004)

File.Name: 4127b_4125-038.jpg
Notes: Little Polypore Bracket Fungi (Trametes ?), mosses and lichens and I don't know what else growing on a few Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) twigs. This micro-world is huge! (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004)

File.Name: 4127c_4125-075.jpg
Notes: Golden Milk Cap (Lacterius insulsis or kin) mushroom pushing up through the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the local woods. See the [Previous] photo for a close shot and a special talent these mushrooms have. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004)
CommonNames: Golden Milk Cap; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 4127d_4125-070.jpg
Notes: Mushroom milk? This is Golden Milk Cap (Lacterius insulsis or kin). When you bruise the gills, they release this white "milk" that doesn't change color in the air. I don't think it's edible, but it has a spicy acrid taste that I like to nibble. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004)

File.Name: 4127g_4119-161.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] I've been photographing this same cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) since Christmas. Why not - they are right outside my door and always beautiful. Edible, but watching the changes is a better feast! (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004)

File.Name: 4128g_4128-112.jpg
Notes: [pyro] A triple-decker match rocket. Try a web search!
Locality: [pyro] A triple-decker match rocket. Try a web search!

File.Name: 4128a_4125-044.jpg
Notes: An understory of native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) growing in the shade between sapling . Native perennial grassland is scarce in central California because the introduced annuals are so aggresive. This native grass looks like a very thin crabgrass, and it's holding its own here. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004)

File.Name: 4128b_4125-041.jpg
Notes: The woods behind my house. The gnarly Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees support a rich understory of blackberry and ferns and much more. There are mushrooms hiding under there! (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004)

File.Name: 4128c_4125-039.jpg
Notes: The tracks of Beetle larvae engraved under the bark of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) log. I reckon the bundles are "frass", a polite word for what the leave behind after eating. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2004)

File.Name: 4129a_4129-134.jpg
Notes: I took my after-school Hike Club kids to a local canyon where uncountable Convergent Lady Beetles (Hippodamia convergens) converge and snuggle for the winter. This party has been going since New Year's Eve at least. Compare http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=4108253. It's a memorable experience to hold a handful of lady bugs. As a bonus we collected a basketful of Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms further down the trail. Now that's education! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 January 2004)

File.Name: 4129b_4129-141.jpg
Notes: Convergent Lady Beetles (Hippodamia convergens) covering an oak branch. They converge and snuggle for the winter in a few local canyons. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 January 2004)

File.Name: 4129c_4129-146.jpg
Notes: King of the hill for these Convergent Lady Beetles (Hippodamia convergens) in a local canyon. This hill is just a stick. Who's on top? (Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 January 2004)

File.Name: 4129d_4129-151.jpg
Notes: Convergent Lady Beetles (Hippodamia convergens) covering a sapling Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. They converge in this canyon to snuggle for winter and then disperse back to our gardens. This party has been going since New Year's Eve at least. Compare http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=4108253. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 January 2004)
CommonNames: Convergent Lady Beetles; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 4129e_4129-129.jpg
Notes: Peanut-size Bolete (Boletus chrysenteron?) mushroom in the leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Tiny mushroom buds are fully formed and then swell with water when it rains. But we haven't had much rain this year. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 January 2004)

File.Name: 4202g_4201-018.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] The beautiful rusty spores and colorful stem of a Cortinarius mushroom growing under oak. There's another photo at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=5854872 (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2004)

File.Name: 4202h_4201-022.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] I found this old dry Leucopaxillus albissimus mushroom sitting upside down like this under oak and pine. These large mushrooms absolutely resist decay. Some critter must have collected this specimen. You can see where it nibbled on the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2004)

File.Name: 4202a_4201-002.jpg
Notes: Looking up the trunk of a large Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) in our canyon. There is a stand of these native pines on the rocky ridge above, but a few trees grow along the creek below. I love this geometry. You can see exuding sap on some of the branches. There's good mushroom hunting under this tree. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2004)

File.Name: 4202b_4202-043.jpg
Notes: There was a rainbow around the not-quite-full moon last night. In this photo the moon is over-exposed making outward rings of yellow to red. But you can also see the raindow colors on the outside making inward rings of red to indigo. Of course it's a weather sign. We had two inches of much needed rain this afternoon and hopefully more is on the way! (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2004)

File.Name: 4202c_4201-011.jpg
Notes: I thought this was an edible Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom at first because of the brilliant blue cap (fading to buff on top) poking up through the oak mulch. Nope, it's a beautiful Cortinarius species with rusty spores and an unpleasant bitter taste that I'm sure is NOT good to eat. (See the [Previous] photo.) Don't take anything for granted with mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2004)

File.Name: 4202d_4201-014.jpg
Notes: Looking under the brilliant blue cap of the beautiful Cortinarius mushroom in the [next] photo. The rusty spores on the gills and colorful stem show that this is not an edible Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) like I first thought. Don't take anything for granted with mushrooms, but always enjoy their beauty and the thrill of the hunt! (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2004)

File.Name: 4202e_4201-041.jpg
Notes: This mushroom under the oaks is dull on top, but brilliant orange underneath. Maybe Gymnopilus? (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2004)

File.Name: 4202f_4201-008.jpg
Notes: Witch's Hat (Hygrocybe conica aka Hygrophorus conicus) mushrooms. The brilliantly colored long stems turn black as you handle them. These specimens are old, and they are turning black on their own, hard to notice in the woods. The young caps are narrow, but even these old caps have a sharp point like a classic witch's hat. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2004)

File.Name: 4206a_4205-237.jpg
Notes: I took my after-school Hike Club kids to Surf Beach at the Santa Ynez River mouth during a minus low tide. Julie walked into my close photo of the foam left behind by a wave on the flat sandy beach. I grumbled and then realized it's perfect. (Surf Beach, 5 February 2004)

File.Name: 4206b_4205-222.jpg
Notes: A line of foam on the advancing edge of a wave moving from right to left during a minus low tide. Note the different textures. (Surf Beach, 5 February 2004)

File.Name: 4206c_4205-197.jpg
Notes: Old pilings at the mouth of the Santa Ynez River. Despite little rain this year, the river has cut a channel to the ocean. I hope the native Steelhead Trout notice. The colors on the pilings show how high the river could be. (Surf Beach, 5 February 2004)

File.Name: 4206d_4205-318.jpg
Notes: Full moon rising under a branch of Valley Oak (Quercus lobata). There is a brilliant sunset behind me, and the tree is reflecting some of the glow. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 February 2004)

File.Name: 4206e_4205-326.jpg
Notes: The beautiful sunset in the [Previous] photo is behind me. This Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) is reflecting the glow. It reminds me of a sea anemone or coral. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 February 2004)

File.Name: 4206f_4205-317.jpg
Notes: Sunset. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 February 2004)

File.Name: 4207g_4207-010.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Black Elfin Saddles (Helvella lacunosa) - I usually call it the Brain Mushroom because that`s what it looks like. The black cap is fresh, they turn gray as they age. Sometimes when you reach out and touch a fresh cap it will fire off a puff of spores. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2004)

File.Name: 4207h_4207-003.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus mushroom with characteristic ring of cobwebby hairs that turns reddish-brown by catching falling spores. The tiny green plant is native California Bedstraw (Galium californicum). (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2004)

File.Name: 4207a_4207-032.jpg
Notes: Another sign of spring! This Milk Maids (Cardamine californica) is the first real wildflower of the year in our shady canyon. It's a mustard relative. The flowers have a pleasant radish-like taste and are good in a salad. It's also known as Toothwort, but you have to dig it up to find the tasty "tooth" hidden underground. Don't even think about it with these first flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2004)

File.Name: 4207b_4207-026.jpg
Notes: New "fiddlehead" shoot of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta), the first I've seen this year. There are signs of spring in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2004)

File.Name: 4207c_4207-006.jpg
Notes: Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus mushroom with characteristic ring of cobwebby hairs that turns reddish-brown by catching falling spores. This species has a viscid cap and slimy bands on the stalk. I don't collect these diverse mushrooms for the table, but they are common and beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2004)

File.Name: 4207d_4207-012.jpg
Notes: Unknown Cortinarius mushroom (maybe C. collinitus - see [next] photo) with characteristic cobwebby hairs on the stem and cap that turn reddish-brown by catching falling spores. You can also see red-brown spores on the dirt under the cap in front. This mushroom has pushed up a "mushrump" of soil as it grew from the earth. Look for these mushrumps in the woods to find fresh mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2004)

File.Name: 4207e_4207-024.jpg
Notes: Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola or kin) mushroom. This is a woodland species, and you can see that the cap turns yellow with age or when bruised. It's edible, but be careful! Some people are allergic and there are other yellow-staining agarics with a nasty phenolic-disinfectant taste. This cap is old, and it fell apart when I tried to collect it. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2004)

File.Name: 4207f_4207-007.jpg
Notes: Black Elfin Saddles (Helvella lacunosa) - I usually call it the Brain Mushroom because that's what it looks like. Sometimes when you reach out and touch a fresh cap it will fire off a puff of spores. The holes in the stem are natural, the "lacunae" or missing parts of the species name. Said to be edible when cooked, but I wouldn't. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2004)

File.Name: 4208a_4208-109.jpg
Notes: Bright red Russula emetica (or kin) hiding in the oak leaves. All the books list it as poisonous, but I always nibble. It has a spicy hot taste (like mustard or wasabe, not chile), but the species name is a clue that we should not actually eat it. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2004)

File.Name: 4208b_4208-072.jpg
Notes: Unknown Agaricus mushroom in the woods, maybe A. silvicola but it doesn`t stain yellow when bruised. Like the usual store-bought mushrooms, it has a membraneous "partial veil" that covers the immature gills and tears free. Usually the veil leaves a ring on the stem, but this one only has a fragment. I nibbled like usual - tastes good. (Now I think it's the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) which is NOT good to eat.) (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2004)

File.Name: 4208c_4208-052.jpg
Notes: This aging cluster of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms has captured a branch of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula). It's like two worlds meeting. The mushrooms are turning to mush so the vine will soon be free. I've been photographing these same mushrooms for over a month now. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2004)

File.Name: 4209h_4208-086.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Witch's Hat (Hygrocybe conica aka Hygrophorus conicus) mushrooms have brilliant colors when fresh, but they turn black with age and handling. The young caps are narrow, but even these old 'shrooms have a sharp point ("umbonate") like a classic witch's hat. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2004)

File.Name: 4209g_4208-105.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] I don't know what this large mushroom is, but it sure is pretty. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2004)

File.Name: 4209a_4208-114.jpg
Notes: Leaflets on a frond of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta) lined up with their colorful spore-bearing sporangia facing the afternoon sun to the right. I think this is the right place at the right time, not an adaptation. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2004)

File.Name: 4209b_4208-113.jpg
Notes: Leaflets on a frond of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta) lined up facing the afternoon sun to the right. You can see shadows of the spore-bearing sporangia on the back side of the leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2004)

File.Name: 4209c_4208-116.jpg
Notes: Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) leaf in a pool on our creek. (It's a dry year. The creek hasn't run yet, but there are a few small pools.) I like all the layers in this photo, from the leaves on the bottom to the blue sky and tree shadows. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2004)

File.Name: 4209d_4208-077.jpg
Notes: Unknown Agaricus mushroom in the woods, maybe the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) which is NOT good to eat. Like the usual store-bought mushrooms, it has a membraneous "partial veil" that covers the immature gills and tears free to form a skirt-like ring on the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2004)

File.Name: 4209e_4208-081.jpg
Notes: Unknown Agaricus mushroom in the woods. Now I think it might be the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) which is NOT good to eat. (See [next] photo.) Mushrooms are fruiting bodies that produce spores on the flat surfaces of their gills. It's amazing to think how much fertile surface area there is on the gills of a single fist-sized mushroom like this. That's adaptation! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2004)

File.Name: 4209f_4208-066.jpg
Notes: The woods along the creek in our shady canyon support a rich understory of native grasses and rushes and mushrooms. I took all of today's and yesterday's photos here. The woods also tell a story. These young Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees have shaded out and replaced the leggy Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) and other chaparral shrubs that are still visible as dead branches and burls near the ground. I think this is plant fire succession in action. This is the south-facing slope on the north side of our canyon. I live on the north-facing slope on the south side of the canyon. I'm very interested in mapping this fire history to visualize the path a wildfire might take up our canyon. I believe this area last burned in the 1950s. I don't find this kind of shaded out chaparral in the woodland at home. I reckon that's a good sign. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2004)

File.Name: 4213k_4208-090.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Definately a Hygrophorus with waxy gills - maybe H. sordidus? The large cap on the right has a fungus of its own spreading with cobwebby mycelium over the gills. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2004)

File.Name: 4213l_4208-097.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] I don`t know what this pretty mushroom is. See the [Previous] photo for a look under the skirts. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2004)

File.Name: 4213m_4208-100.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] I don't know what this pretty mushroom is. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2004)

File.Name: 4213a_4212-291.jpg
Notes: I took my after-school Hike Club kids rock-hopping down San Jose Creek. It's been a very dry year, and the creek is just a trickle, but the scoured sandstone bedrock hints how intense this creek can be in a wet year. Further down it becomes a jumble of car- to house-sized boulders perfect for extreme rock-hopping. It's very dark and hard to photograph in the afternoon. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2004)

File.Name: 4213b_4212-293.jpg
Notes: Deciduous White Alder (<>Alnus rhombifolia) are still bare for winter. These trees are a good indicator of permanent water along our often dry creeks. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2004)

File.Name: 4213c_4212-278.jpg
Notes: Looking up at driftwood from under the huge boulders along San Jose Creek. It's been a very dry year, and the creek is just a trickle under the boulders that choke this wild canyon. The wood shows how high the water can get in a wet year. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2004)

File.Name: 4213d_4212-303.jpg
Notes: Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) is starting to flower in our mountains. It seems there is always a white flowered Ceanothus, but the species change from here to there. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2004)

File.Name: 4213e_4207-020.jpg
Notes: Fresh leaves of Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum) caught in a beam of afternoon sunlight without flash. It`s not common here and we rarely find fruit. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2004)

File.Name: 4213f_4207-018.jpg
Notes: Spring leaves of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula). I caught this in a beam of afternoon sunlight, no flash. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2004)

File.Name: 4214h_4214-024.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Our woods are crunchy-dry this year, but I'm still finding a few fresh mushrooms like this Cortinarius species. It has a very long tapered stem and grows under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Mushrooms produce spores on the surface of their gills. It's amazing how much fertile surface area there is on the gills of a single mushroom like this! (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2004)

File.Name: 4214g_4214-003.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Little dark-spored mushrooms (maybe Psathyrella candolleana?) growing from wood chips. Note the purple-black "spore-print" under the cap in the middle. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2004)

File.Name: 4214c_4214-014.jpg
Notes: Delicate flowers of Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica). It's our first herbaceous wildflower. Look close and you can see that it has four long stamens and two shorter ones, like all members of the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2004)

File.Name: 4214d_4214-035.jpg
Notes: The first Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) flowers in our canyon. It's a low shrub with needle-like leaves that prefers dry rocky outcrops. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2004)

File.Name: 4214e_4214-008.jpg
Notes: Small spider web in the woods. The tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) is resting in the center of her web, not much bigger than a pencil tip. She is not the darker shape in the center, but the lighter reddish shape below, hard to see with this backlighting. Perfect as her web is, it's interesting to spot the imperfections. I bet she's after the Fungus Gnats (Mycetophila etc.) that are breeding in the mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2004)

File.Name: 4214f_4214-036.jpg
Notes: New leaves on Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) - palmate, like the fingers from the palm of your hand. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2004)

File.Name: 4214b_1525-317.jpg
Notes: A wild desert rose for Valentine's Day. This is the Interior Rose (Rosa woodsii var. ultramontana). (Owens Valley, 25 May 2001)

File.Name: 4215g_4215-072.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] This Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silicola?) has a cap that bruises yellow on the margin (as you can see) and a very long tapered stem. It has a pleasant anise-like smell and taste. Said to be edible, but I find this group really confusing so I usually stick to a nibble. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2004)

File.Name: 4215h_4214-029.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Young Cortinarius mushrooms often show surprising colors, even when its dry enough to split the stems as they open. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2004)

File.Name: 4215a_4215-067.jpg
Notes: First flowers of Chaparral Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) growing in a place in our mountains that burned last year. The tree-like maroon pattern on the petals looks like a heraldic emblem, appropriate for a banner. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2004) Today's photos are mostly "first flowers" - the first I've seen up here in the mountains at least. I have next week as vacation from teaching at the perfect time - first flowers AND mushrooms, even in this very dry year.

File.Name: 4215b_4215-059.jpg
Notes: First flowers of Chaparral Nightshade (Solanun xanti) growing on a burned area in the mountains, shot against a gray sky. Note the green glands and the characteristic clasping stamens. Other plants have whitish stems and branching hairs or no hairs. It's a confusing group. Said to be toxic, but critters have been eating the foliage. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2004)

File.Name: 4215c_4215-042.jpg
Notes: Garden Daffodils (Narcissus sp.) flowering at a long-abandoned homestead in the mountains. These plants have been growing for many decades without help. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2004)
